d0cc4b86-1a9c-4800-9c09-5442ccdf477a.question: Toward the end of that phone call with Chen Hao, what made you suddenly hesitate and decide not to ask that question about mechanical engineering?

ranked_items.content:
**MEMORANDUM**

Date: March 28, 2022, 21:45

---

**To-Do: Conversation with Chen Hao**

Status: Incomplete / Needs Rescheduling

I called Chen Hao tonight. I originally intended to ask if he had any interest in mechanical engineering and if he’d like to learn some basic craftsmanship. But when the words were on the tip of my tongue, I held back. Instead, I just asked how work was going. He said the mortgage pressure has been heavy lately and the market is unstable.

I didn't push further after hearing that.

Later, in the workshop, I replayed the conversation in my head. The questions are—

1. Is he truly uninterested, or is it that I’ve never properly talked to him about these things?
2. If I start teaching him now, is it too late? Look at how old he is already.
3. How can I bring it up next time without it feeling abrupt?

The items in the tool cabinet have been sitting there for so many years. Some were left by my father; others I collected myself. They are still high-precision instruments, wiped and maintained regularly. But if no one uses them, maintenance is pointless.

**Timing for Next Attempt:**
- Talk face-to-face when he comes home? Or stick to the phone?
- Start with his work stress first, then bring it up?
- Or should I just document the use and maintenance of every tool and show it to him when the chance arises?

**Progress Log:**
Finished the first 30 items on the tool list. About 20-plus items left to organize. I need to clearly write down the purpose, precision grade, and maintenance method for each. I need to hurry up with this.

While I’m still mobile, I need to get these things sorted out.

---

**Misc:**
Li Meifang ate salted fish again today; said a friend gave it to her. Her blood pressure has been fluctuating again this month. Must urge her to go for a check-up next week.

ranked_items.content:
In the workshop, Chen Guilin sat on a simple wooden stool, a notebook open before him. On the tool rack, the precision instruments glinted coldly under the fluorescent lights. His phone screen lit up—a WeChat message from his wife, Li Meifang: "What do you want for dinner?"

He didn't reply immediately. Instead, he stared at the dense notes in his notebook and the words "Who would use this?" circled in red ink.

Five minutes later, he finally typed:

"Do you think... there's any point in me keeping these records?"

After sending it, he added another line:

"Just thinking about what happens after I retire. These tools will just be sitting there anyway."

He paused for a long time, stopping himself from typing more. His fingers hovered over the keyboard before he finally lowered them.

Then he added one last message:

"I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately. Not the work kind of tired."

---

*Chen Guilin put down his phone and turned back to wiping a precision vernier caliper on the rack. His movements were slow and meticulous, but his eyes were no longer on the tool.*

ranked_items.content:
# March 28, 2022, 9:45 PM

Had a phone call with Haozi.

He said business has been pretty good lately, and the mortgage pressure isn't as heavy anymore. I originally wanted to ask him if he had any interest in mechanical engineering—if he wanted to know more about what I’ve been working on all these years... but the words died in my throat. I changed the subject and asked how work was going instead. He said it was fine, then asked if there was something I wanted to say.

I told him it was nothing, that everything was under control.

After hanging up, I stood in the living room for a long while, phone still in hand.

I thought about the tools in my workshop. Forty years. Some I modified myself; others I repaired after they broke. Every single one has a story, but those stories only exist in my head. I’ve recorded some of it in my notebooks, but what’s the point of recording things? Haozi won't read them. Yuyu won't read them either. They have their own lives, which is only natural.

But I still feel... wrong.

These things, this knowledge—it just ends here. I’ve been in this position for twelve years, proposing several improvements every year that the company adopted. But those will end, too. When I retire and the new manager arrives, he’ll have his own methods. The optimizations I made might eventually just become a statistic—"defect rate down by 25%"—and nothing more.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I didn't go down.

I pulled out the last physical exam report and read it again. Blood pressure is a bit high. The doctor said to be careful. Mine has started to fluctuate, too. It was stable for the last two years, but now...

I can't control it.

That’s what hurts the most. I can control workflows, quality standards, and my subordinates' performance. But I can't control time. I can't control my body. I can't control whether Haozi or Yuyu will inherit anything.

In the margin of one page in my notebook, I wrote: "Who will ever use this?" I didn't erase it.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. There’s a quality control meeting in the afternoon. A new employee’s report isn't detailed enough; I need to point it out. It’s my responsibility. But I’m getting a bit tired of this kind of thing. Or rather... I know where this frustration is coming from. It’s because I’m demanding perfection from others while I myself am losing my grip on it.

Thinking this way makes me feel a bit hypocritical.

Forget it. Time for bed. There’s a lot to do tomorrow.

I need to hurry while I can still move.

ranked_items.content:
March 20, 2022, 11:47 PM

Sitting in the workshop. My wife is already asleep. The noises downstairs have all quieted down.

I finished page thirty today. Starting from the first page, my handwriting was neat; the purpose, specifications, and purchase date of every tool were recorded clearly. But toward the end, the pen started to shake. I’m not sure when it began—maybe around page twenty-five. The strokes became irregular, and some words looked rushed.

When I got to the entry for the wrench, I stopped for a long time. I bought this wrench in 1998. It’s a Japanese import with a precision of 0.02 mm. I’ve used it for over twenty years, and it shows very little wear. I wanted to record its use, but the pen just hovered there. Then I wrote—"Who will ever use this?"

After writing that sentence, I didn't cross it out. It’s strange. I’m not usually like this. The notebooks on my desk have always been spotless—no scribbles, no traces of hesitation. But this time, looking at that sentence, I just couldn't bring myself to erase it.

Hao is in Shenzhen; Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them will ever come to this workshop. Hao is in real estate sales, and Yu teaches. They have their own lives. I never expected them to inherit anything, but sitting here now, looking at these tools, I suddenly realized—these things are about to become useless. Not because they’re broken or lost, but because no one will know how to use them.

This morning, Meifang asked me what I was busy with. I only replied with a grunt. She didn't ask again. She’s used to my silence. After thirty-five years, she’s long known that I’m a man of few words. But when that "mm-hmm" came out today, even I could hear it—it wasn't silence; it was avoidance. I don’t know how to explain to her what I’m doing. I can’t even explain it to myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. My blood sugar is up a bit again. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m in my fifties now; my body is starting to disobey me. I used to think that as long as I kept things under control and followed the plan, nothing would go wrong. But the body doesn't follow a plan. Neither does time.

I’m a little over a year away from retirement. I’ve been waiting for this day, but now that it’s almost here, I’m starting to feel afraid. Afraid of what I’ll do after I retire. Afraid of being idle. Afraid that these tools will just keep sitting here, unused and misunderstood.

The notebook is still open. "Who will ever use this" is still there on page thirty. Tomorrow, I have to keep writing. There are still many tools left to record. I have to get them all down while I can still move.

But even if I record them, what then?

# Work Log

**Date:** Monday, March 28, 2022

**Weather:** Overcast

---

## Work Record

**Morning:** Completed the Q1 quality inspection data summary. The defect rate stands at 0.8%, down 0.2% quarter-on-quarter. Coordinated next week’s production capacity plan with the Production Department; order volume remains stable.

**Afternoon:** Audited the operating procedures of the newly hired inspectors and identified three non-compliances, which were corrected on the spot. Proposed a process optimization plan during the departmental meeting regarding batch processing logic for incoming inspections; it is expected to improve efficiency by approximately 12%. My supervisor indicated that it would be included in the upcoming review.

**Evening:** Organized the tool inventory and added some instructional notes for several older tools.

---

## Personal Status

**Mood Index:** 3/10

**Stress Level:** 7/10

**Physical Condition:** Normal

---

## Daily Reflections

Received a call from Chen Hao this evening. He mentioned that sales pressure has been high lately and he’s struggling a bit with the mortgage payments. I asked a few questions, but the communication didn't seem to achieve the desired effect. He hung up after a few words, claiming he still had client files to process.

After hanging up, I stood in the living room for a while.

——

Thinking it over, I feel I need to adjust the way I communicate with him. But as for how exactly to adjust, I haven't figured it out yet. Maybe the problem lies with me.

Li Meifang said I’ve become even more quiet lately. I told her I haven't, I’m just busy with work. She didn't say anything more and went back to watching her TV drama.

---

## To-Do List

- [ ] Complete preparations for the ISO system audit materials by Wednesday
- [ ] Organize the precision tool inventory (Progress: ~60%)
- [ ] Regularly check household financial accounts

---

## Notes

While I’m still able to get things done, I need to get everything in order.

# Work Log

**Date: Monday, March 21, 2022**

**Weather: Overcast**

---

**Morning Records:**

8:30 Arrived at the office. Reviewed last Friday’s QC reports and found that three products from Line B had dimensional deviations exceeding the standard by 0.2mm. This is unacceptable. Immediately called a meeting with the relevant personnel; the root cause must be identified today. There is no compromise on quality.

9:15 Discussed process issues with Master Li. His explanation was still too superficial. I pointed out the problem directly—it’s not a machine issue, but a failure to strictly follow operating procedures. This kind of low-level error should not occur in our department. Gave him a clear deadline: an improvement plan must be on my desk by this Friday. No room for negotiation.

10:00 Compiled the first draft of this month’s improvement proposals. I plan to submit four items to my superiors:
- Optimization of calibration cycles for testing equipment
- Upgrading classification standards for raw material warehousing
- Refining the skill assessment system for subordinates
- Preventive maintenance plan for the production lines

These are all necessary. We cannot slack off.

**Midday Notes:**

During lunch, it occurred to me that if we maintain current efficiency levels, we could reduce our defect rate by another 3-5%. But the prerequisite is that everyone must execute the standards to the letter. No one can be the weak link.

**Afternoon Plan:**

- 14:00 On-site production inspection
- 15:30 Monthly summary meeting with the QC team
- 16:30 Organize tool inventory (Need to document the specific uses of those precision tools in my home workshop, just in case—never mind, just get them recorded for now)

**Other:**

Lately, I feel the work pace needs to be tightened further. There can be no laxity. Time passes quickly; what can be done must be done early. Efficiency is paramount.

My wife mentioned yesterday that her blood pressure is a bit high again. Reminded her to take her medicine on time and stop eating so much salty food. These small matters must also be managed properly.

---

**Remarks:**

Focus on the improvement progress of Line B next week. A standard is a standard; there is no room for bargaining.

ranked_items.content:
Played some chess over the weekend. A chess friend mentioned how bad the market is lately—houses just aren't selling. It made me think of the pressure Haozi is under in Shenzhen; I’m a bit worried for him.

The weather was nice, but I’ve really felt my physical stamina declining these past two years. After three games of chess, I felt quite tired. Checked my blood pressure when I got home; luckily, it’s still within the normal range.

Spent some time watching TV with Lao Li this evening. She’s been controlling her diet lately and is sticking to it well. She has a follow-up appointment at the hospital tomorrow.

Gave the tools in the workshop another wipe-down. They’ve been sitting there for quite a few years now. Sometimes I wonder what will become of these things later on. But for now, I should just focus on getting the tasks at hand done.

Need to make the most of things while I’m still mobile.

ranked_items.content:
# May 8, 2022, past 10 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Meifang fell asleep a long while ago; I can hear her light snoring drifting in from the bedroom. Her blood pressure was a bit high again today. I saw the reading she took this afternoon, but I didn't tell her.

What happened at the chess room today keeps spinning in my head.

Midway through the game, I suddenly realized I was staring blankly at the board. My opponent asked what I was thinking about, and I said, "I'm getting older; my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." As soon as the words left my mouth, they felt foreign to me. I wouldn't have said that before. I would have said, "This is an interesting move," or I wouldn't have explained at all and just played the piece.

But today, I was just slow. The calculations in my head felt sluggish. I could feel it.

Player A tried to comfort me, saying my game has always been stable. I heard myself reply: "It’s stable because I’m afraid to change."

I regretted saying it immediately. Not because it wasn't true, but because it was *too* true. I’ve never been that blunt before. Stable... yes, I’ve always chased stability. Forty years, the same factory, the same way of working, the same rhythm of life. No job-hopping, no risks, every step planned out.

That’s not stability. That’s stagnation.

I left the chess room halfway through. That has never happened before. My friends were a bit surprised; I told them I was tired and needed to go home. As I turned to leave, I looked back at that place—the familiar boards, the familiar faces, everything so familiar. My eyes stung a little.

Why is this happening?

The tools in the workshop are still there. All these years, I’ve been wiping them down and organizing them, like I’m looking after something precious. But Hao is in real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked what those tools are for. Once, I tried to explain the use of a specific drill bit to Hao, but before I could finish, he said, "Dad, I’ve got a client I need to call back."

I’m recording the purpose of every tool in a notebook. Writing them down, one by one. But halfway through, I asked myself—what’s the point? Who is ever going to look at this notebook?

The physical exam report is still sitting in the drawer. The doctor said those figures "require attention." Every two days, I take it out and look at it, staring at the numbers, trying to find some kind of pattern. As if by doing that, I could control something. But the truth is, I can’t control anything. Blood pressure rises, blood sugar fluctuates, reactions slow down. These things are happening, and all I can do is record them.

Meifang’s blood pressure is rising too. I’m monitoring her diet and pushing her to get checkups. But what if one day I can’t move either? In our thirty-five years together, we’ve said too little. Now, even if I want to make up for it, I can't.

How much time is left?

I don't know. But I can feel time accelerating. It’s as if I used to have plenty of time to do something, and now I suddenly realize it’s gone. Retirement in two years, and then what? Empty days, no work, no one wanting anything from me, no one inheriting anything I know.

I sit here, pen in hand, but I don’t know what to write.

Maybe it’s best to just keep working tomorrow. At least when I’m working, I still know what I’m doing.

Location: Recreation Room
Participants: Player A, Player B, Player C

---

**Log:**
Today’s afternoon game. During the second round, my reactions were noticeably slower, and my strategy felt muddled. Player A asked if I’d been overworked lately; I told him no, I was just having an off day. But it’s not about being tired.

I stared at the board for nearly two minutes before making a move—something that never used to happen. My fingers were resting on the piece, but my mind was elsewhere—thinking about those figures in last week’s physical exam report, thinking about the dizzy spells I’ve been having some mornings.

I left early during the third round, claiming I had something to take care of at home. I didn’t. I just didn't want to keep going.

**Flag/Reminder: Contact the doctor next Monday. Ask if this recent sluggishness requires a follow-up. Might need a neurocognitive assessment.**

---

*Note: Review chess performance once a month. If things continue to deteriorate, must take the initiative to go to the hospital.*

ranked_items.content:
It was already past 7 p.m. by the time I got home. My wife was busy in the kitchen; hearing the door, she poked her head out and asked if I’d eaten. I told her I hadn't, and she said the food was still warm.

I set down my briefcase and sat on the sofa. She brought over a bowl of soup. I took two sips and then just sat there, motionless.

As she was clearing the dishes, she asked, "What’s wrong? You seem a bit down today."

I didn't answer directly. After a moment, I finally said, "I didn't play very well today."

"Oh? Did you lose?" she asked.

"I didn't lose. It’s just... my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." I paused. "My chess partner said I was slow today. I felt it myself, too."

She sat down beside me. She didn't say anything, she just stayed there with me.

I spoke again: "Have you noticed anything about me lately?"

"Like what?" she asked.

"Just... feeling a bit tired." I rubbed my temples. "Maybe work has been too intense recently."

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to say. What I wanted to ask was: Have you noticed me changing? Do you think I'm getting old? But I couldn't bring myself to say those words.

"Then get some rest early," she said. "I'll make you some red bean porridge; it's good for you."

I nodded. She got up and headed for the kitchen. I sat on the sofa, looking at my hands—the knuckles were a bit stiff, the skin becoming loose.

I took out my phone and opened last week’s physical exam report, looking over the data once more. My blood sugar was 0.3 higher than last year, and my blood pressure was fluctuating. I remembered what the doctor said, but I hadn't told my wife.

She was humming a song in the kitchen, her voice soft and gentle. It suddenly occurred to me that her blood pressure had been rising too. We are both changing; we are both aging. The thought made me feel a dull ache.

I turned off the screen, leaned back against the sofa, and closed my eyes.

I still have to go to the office tomorrow. There are still those tools, those records. Time is moving on, and it feels like there is less and less I can do.
ranked_items.subquestion: Old Chen, you used to be the one who valued a steady and solid game above all else. How is it that lately you’ve not only been lost in thought over the board, but even walked out mid-game for the first time ever? Is this habit of "seeking stability" making you feel particularly exhausted when facing the changes of today?

ranked_items.content:
April 5, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Wang Gang’s quality inspection report is still spinning in my head.

The formatting was off again when he handed it in this morning—the classification standards in the header didn't match last year’s specifications. It’s such a basic thing to get wrong. I couldn't help myself; I snapped at him. He tried to explain, but I cut him off. No excuses. Professionalism is about an obsession with detail.

His face changed the moment I said it.

Looking back now, my tone was harsh. Harsher than usual. He’s actually a decent employee. In the past, I would have pointed out these small mistakes, but never like that. Why today...?

My fingers have been tapping on the desk for a long while. Rapidly. I didn't even realize I was doing it.

What am I thinking? Was his report really that outrageous? Or... was I just taking it out on him?

It’s been like this lately. I’m becoming more demanding with my subordinates and more micro-managing with my wife. This morning, I was looking at her blood pressure logs again; a single high reading and I started feeling restless. I spent another whole night in the tool shed, numbering all the precision tools and documenting their uses. I’m terrified that one day I’ll suddenly... lose the ability to move, and these things will become completely useless because no one knows how to handle them.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

I know what I’m trying to do. I’m trying to control things. Control the work, the standards, everything at home—even the aging of my own body. It’s as if by managing every tiny detail, I can prove I’m still useful, that I can still... what?

Wang Gang has to come in for work tomorrow. I need to figure out how to handle this. Not for his sake, but because... I don’t want to become this kind of person. I don’t want to use harshness to mask my own fear.

But I don’t know how to change.

How much longer can I do this? How much longer can I keep things under control like this?

ranked_items.content:
April 28, 2022, 11:15 PM

I’m sitting in the study. The lights are off. It’s pitch black outside the window.

I’ve been sitting here in the dark for a long time. My hand is pressed against my chest; I can feel my heartbeat. The rhythm is irregular. My breathing is a bit shallow, but I haven't moved. I just sit here, feeling it all.

The physical exam report is still on the desk. Blood pressure 139/88, fasting blood sugar 6.8. There’s a waveform on the ECG that the doctor said needs observation. He used the phrase "requires attention." I heard him loud and clear.

I’m thinking: this is how it begins.

I press my fingers against my pulse and count—76 beats per minute. Within the normal range. But I know it was racing just a moment ago. Why did it speed up? No exercise, nothing special happened. I was just sitting here when I suddenly felt... something I can't put into words.

Hao called today. He said the mortgage pressure is heavy and he might have to pay back a little less this month. I didn't say much; I just noted it down in my ledger. The mortgage, his sales commissions, the fluctuations of Shenzhen’s housing prices. I track all these data points. But what’s the use of tracking? Can I change anything?

The tools in the workshop are still on the shelves. Lately, I’ve been numbering them, writing down their uses and the years they were purchased. Some were bought in 1995, others in 2008. I wonder, if I were suddenly gone, who would know how to use these things? Not Hao. Not Yu. And certainly not Meifang.

They would just become scrap metal. Or be tossed out as junk.

I’ve been sitting in the dark for a long time, without turning on the light.

Tomorrow, I need to schedule a more detailed check-up. Cardiology. I need to know exactly what that abnormal waveform means. I need to establish a long-term monitoring plan. Every month’s data must be recorded so I can see the trends.

I also need to keep supervising Meifang’s blood pressure. She’s started eating salty foods again; I have to remind her once more.

My breathing has settled down a bit now.

But that sense of unease remains.

It’s as if something is slowly coming loose, and I can’t reach out to catch it.

Spent some time watching TV with Lao Li this evening. She’s been controlling her diet lately and is sticking to it well. She has a follow-up appointment at the hospital tomorrow.

Gave the tools in the workshop another wipe-down. They’ve been sitting there for quite a few years now. Sometimes I wonder what will become of these things later on. But for now, I should just focus on getting the tasks at hand done.

Need to make the most of things while I’m still mobile.

ranked_items.content:
# May 8, 2022, past 10 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Meifang fell asleep a long while ago; I can hear her light snoring drifting in from the bedroom. Her blood pressure was a bit high again today. I saw the reading she took this afternoon, but I didn't tell her.

What happened at the chess room today keeps spinning in my head.

Midway through the game, I suddenly realized I was staring blankly at the board. My opponent asked what I was thinking about, and I said, "I'm getting older; my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." As soon as the words left my mouth, they felt foreign to me. I wouldn't have said that before. I would have said, "This is an interesting move," or I wouldn't have explained at all and just played the piece.

But today, I was just slow. The calculations in my head felt sluggish. I could feel it.

Player A tried to comfort me, saying my game has always been stable. I heard myself reply: "It’s stable because I’m afraid to change."

I regretted saying it immediately. Not because it wasn't true, but because it was *too* true. I’ve never been that blunt before. Stable... yes, I’ve always chased stability. Forty years, the same factory, the same way of working, the same rhythm of life. No job-hopping, no risks, every step planned out.

That’s not stability. That’s stagnation.

I left the chess room halfway through. That has never happened before. My friends were a bit surprised; I told them I was tired and needed to go home. As I turned to leave, I looked back at that place—the familiar boards, the familiar faces, everything so familiar. My eyes stung a little.

Why is this happening?

The tools in the workshop are still there. All these years, I’ve been wiping them down and organizing them, like I’m looking after something precious. But Hao is in real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked what those tools are for. Once, I tried to explain the use of a specific drill bit to Hao, but before I could finish, he said, "Dad, I’ve got a client I need to call back."

I’m recording the purpose of every tool in a notebook. Writing them down, one by one. But halfway through, I asked myself—what’s the point? Who is ever going to look at this notebook?

The physical exam report is still sitting in the drawer. The doctor said those figures "require attention." Every two days, I take it out and look at it, staring at the numbers, trying to find some kind of pattern. As if by doing that, I could control something. But the truth is, I can’t control anything. Blood pressure rises, blood sugar fluctuates, reactions slow down. These things are happening, and all I can do is record them.

Meifang’s blood pressure is rising too. I’m monitoring her diet and pushing her to get checkups. But what if one day I can’t move either? In our thirty-five years together, we’ve said too little. Now, even if I want to make up for it, I can't.

How much time is left?

I don't know. But I can feel time accelerating. It’s as if I used to have plenty of time to do something, and now I suddenly realize it’s gone. Retirement in two years, and then what? Empty days, no work, no one wanting anything from me, no one inheriting anything I know.

I sit here, pen in hand, but I don’t know what to write.

Maybe it’s best to just keep working tomorrow. At least when I’m working, I still know what I’m doing.

ranked_items.content:
# March 28, 2022, 9:45 PM

Had a phone call with Haozi.

He said business has been pretty good lately, and the mortgage pressure isn't as heavy anymore. I originally wanted to ask him if he had any interest in mechanical engineering—if he wanted to know more about what I’ve been working on all these years... but the words died in my throat. I changed the subject and asked how work was going instead. He said it was fine, then asked if there was something I wanted to say.

I told him it was nothing, that everything was under control.

After hanging up, I stood in the living room for a long while, phone still in hand.

I thought about the tools in my workshop. Forty years. Some I modified myself; others I repaired after they broke. Every single one has a story, but those stories only exist in my head. I’ve recorded some of it in my notebooks, but what’s the point of recording things? Haozi won't read them. Yuyu won't read them either. They have their own lives, which is only natural.

But I still feel... wrong.

These things, this knowledge—it just ends here. I’ve been in this position for twelve years, proposing several improvements every year that the company adopted. But those will end, too. When I retire and the new manager arrives, he’ll have his own methods. The optimizations I made might eventually just become a statistic—"defect rate down by 25%"—and nothing more.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I didn't go down.

I pulled out the last physical exam report and read it again. Blood pressure is a bit high. The doctor said to be careful. Mine has started to fluctuate, too. It was stable for the last two years, but now...

I can't control it.

That’s what hurts the most. I can control workflows, quality standards, and my subordinates' performance. But I can't control time. I can't control my body. I can't control whether Haozi or Yuyu will inherit anything.

In the margin of one page in my notebook, I wrote: "Who will ever use this?" I didn't erase it.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. There’s a quality control meeting in the afternoon. A new employee’s report isn't detailed enough; I need to point it out. It’s my responsibility. But I’m getting a bit tired of this kind of thing. Or rather... I know where this frustration is coming from. It’s because I’m demanding perfection from others while I myself am losing my grip on it.

Thinking this way makes me feel a bit hypocritical.

Forget it. Time for bed. There’s a lot to do tomorrow.

I need to hurry while I can still move.

ranked_items.content:
In the workshop, Chen Guilin sat on a simple wooden stool, a notebook open before him. On the tool rack, the precision instruments glinted coldly under the fluorescent lights. His phone screen lit up—a WeChat message from his wife, Li Meifang: "What do you want for dinner?"

He didn't reply immediately. Instead, he stared at the dense notes in his notebook and the words "Who would use this?" circled in red ink.

Five minutes later, he finally typed:

"Do you think... there's any point in me keeping these records?"

After sending it, he added another line:

"Just thinking about what happens after I retire. These tools will just be sitting there anyway."

He paused for a long time, stopping himself from typing more. His fingers hovered over the keyboard before he finally lowered them.

Then he added one last message:

"I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately. Not the work kind of tired."

---

*Chen Guilin put down his phone and turned back to wiping a precision vernier caliper on the rack. His movements were slow and meticulous, but his eyes were no longer on the tool.*

**[Sunday, May 8, 3:00 PM – Chess Game]**

Location: Recreation Room
Participants: Player A, Player B, Player C

---

**Log:**
Today’s afternoon game. During the second round, my reactions were noticeably slower, and my strategy felt muddled. Player A asked if I’d been overworked lately; I told him no, I was just having an off day. But it’s not about being tired.

I stared at the board for nearly two minutes before making a move—something that never used to happen. My fingers were resting on the piece, but my mind was elsewhere—thinking about those figures in last week’s physical exam report, thinking about the dizzy spells I’ve been having some mornings.

I left early during the third round, claiming I had something to take care of at home. I didn’t. I just didn't want to keep going.

**Flag/Reminder: Contact the doctor next Monday. Ask if this recent sluggishness requires a follow-up. Might need a neurocognitive assessment.**

---

*Note: Review chess performance once a month. If things continue to deteriorate, must take the initiative to go to the hospital.*

1ada68df-dbdd-4a16-8046-74d7381c107a.question: Old Chen, I remember you mentioning that "stability" is your guiding principle, but over the past month or so, I feel like you’ve changed quite a bit. First, you were torn over whether to talk to Chen Hao about the mechanical legacy, and then later, while we were playing chess, you lamented that "stability is just the fear of change." Have you started to have some second thoughts about what you’ve stood for all these decades?

ranked_items.content:
**MEMORANDUM**

Date: March 28, 2022, 21:45

---

**To-Do: Conversation with Chen Hao**

Status: Incomplete / Needs Rescheduling

I called Chen Hao tonight. I originally intended to ask if he had any interest in mechanical engineering and if he’d like to learn some basic craftsmanship. But when the words were on the tip of my tongue, I held back. Instead, I just asked how work was going. He said the mortgage pressure has been heavy lately and the market is unstable.

I didn't push further after hearing that.

Later, in the workshop, I replayed the conversation in my head. The questions are—

1. Is he truly uninterested, or is it that I’ve never properly talked to him about these things?
2. If I start teaching him now, is it too late? Look at how old he is already.
3. How can I bring it up next time without it feeling abrupt?

The items in the tool cabinet have been sitting there for so many years. Some were left by my father; others I collected myself. They are still high-precision instruments, wiped and maintained regularly. But if no one uses them, maintenance is pointless.

**Timing for Next Attempt:**
- Talk face-to-face when he comes home? Or stick to the phone?
- Start with his work stress first, then bring it up?
- Or should I just document the use and maintenance of every tool and show it to him when the chance arises?

**Progress Log:**
Finished the first 30 items on the tool list. About 20-plus items left to organize. I need to clearly write down the purpose, precision grade, and maintenance method for each. I need to hurry up with this.

While I’m still mobile, I need to get these things sorted out.

ranked_items.content:
# May 8, 2022, past 10 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Meifang fell asleep a long while ago; I can hear her light snoring drifting in from the bedroom. Her blood pressure was a bit high again today. I saw the reading she took this afternoon, but I didn't tell her.

What happened at the chess room today keeps spinning in my head.

Midway through the game, I suddenly realized I was staring blankly at the board. My opponent asked what I was thinking about, and I said, "I'm getting older; my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." As soon as the words left my mouth, they felt foreign to me. I wouldn't have said that before. I would have said, "This is an interesting move," or I wouldn't have explained at all and just played the piece.

But today, I was just slow. The calculations in my head felt sluggish. I could feel it.

Player A tried to comfort me, saying my game has always been stable. I heard myself reply: "It’s stable because I’m afraid to change."

I regretted saying it immediately. Not because it wasn't true, but because it was *too* true. I’ve never been that blunt before. Stable... yes, I’ve always chased stability. Forty years, the same factory, the same way of working, the same rhythm of life. No job-hopping, no risks, every step planned out.

That’s not stability. That’s stagnation.

I left the chess room halfway through. That has never happened before. My friends were a bit surprised; I told them I was tired and needed to go home. As I turned to leave, I looked back at that place—the familiar boards, the familiar faces, everything so familiar. My eyes stung a little.

Why is this happening?

The tools in the workshop are still there. All these years, I’ve been wiping them down and organizing them, like I’m looking after something precious. But Hao is in real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked what those tools are for. Once, I tried to explain the use of a specific drill bit to Hao, but before I could finish, he said, "Dad, I’ve got a client I need to call back."

I’m recording the purpose of every tool in a notebook. Writing them down, one by one. But halfway through, I asked myself—what’s the point? Who is ever going to look at this notebook?

The physical exam report is still sitting in the drawer. The doctor said those figures "require attention." Every two days, I take it out and look at it, staring at the numbers, trying to find some kind of pattern. As if by doing that, I could control something. But the truth is, I can’t control anything. Blood pressure rises, blood sugar fluctuates, reactions slow down. These things are happening, and all I can do is record them.

Meifang’s blood pressure is rising too. I’m monitoring her diet and pushing her to get checkups. But what if one day I can’t move either? In our thirty-five years together, we’ve said too little. Now, even if I want to make up for it, I can't.

How much time is left?

I don't know. But I can feel time accelerating. It’s as if I used to have plenty of time to do something, and now I suddenly realize it’s gone. Retirement in two years, and then what? Empty days, no work, no one wanting anything from me, no one inheriting anything I know.

I sit here, pen in hand, but I don’t know what to write.

Maybe it’s best to just keep working tomorrow. At least when I’m working, I still know what I’m doing.

ranked_items.content:
In the workshop, Chen Guilin sat on a simple wooden stool, a notebook open before him. On the tool rack, the precision instruments glinted coldly under the fluorescent lights. His phone screen lit up—a WeChat message from his wife, Li Meifang: "What do you want for dinner?"

He didn't reply immediately. Instead, he stared at the dense notes in his notebook and the words "Who would use this?" circled in red ink.

Five minutes later, he finally typed:

"Do you think... there's any point in me keeping these records?"

After sending it, he added another line:

"Just thinking about what happens after I retire. These tools will just be sitting there anyway."

He paused for a long time, stopping himself from typing more. His fingers hovered over the keyboard before he finally lowered them.

Then he added one last message:

"I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately. Not the work kind of tired."

---

*Chen Guilin put down his phone and turned back to wiping a precision vernier caliper on the rack. His movements were slow and meticulous, but his eyes were no longer on the tool.*

ranked_items.content:
April 5, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Wang Gang’s quality inspection report is still spinning in my head.

The formatting was off again when he handed it in this morning—the classification standards in the header didn't match last year’s specifications. It’s such a basic thing to get wrong. I couldn't help myself; I snapped at him. He tried to explain, but I cut him off. No excuses. Professionalism is about an obsession with detail.

His face changed the moment I said it.

Looking back now, my tone was harsh. Harsher than usual. He’s actually a decent employee. In the past, I would have pointed out these small mistakes, but never like that. Why today...?

My fingers have been tapping on the desk for a long while. Rapidly. I didn't even realize I was doing it.

What am I thinking? Was his report really that outrageous? Or... was I just taking it out on him?

It’s been like this lately. I’m becoming more demanding with my subordinates and more micro-managing with my wife. This morning, I was looking at her blood pressure logs again; a single high reading and I started feeling restless. I spent another whole night in the tool shed, numbering all the precision tools and documenting their uses. I’m terrified that one day I’ll suddenly... lose the ability to move, and these things will become completely useless because no one knows how to handle them.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

I know what I’m trying to do. I’m trying to control things. Control the work, the standards, everything at home—even the aging of my own body. It’s as if by managing every tiny detail, I can prove I’m still useful, that I can still... what?

Wang Gang has to come in for work tomorrow. I need to figure out how to handle this. Not for his sake, but because... I don’t want to become this kind of person. I don’t want to use harshness to mask my own fear.

But I don’t know how to change.

How much longer can I do this? How much longer can I keep things under control like this?

Have you started to have some second thoughts about what you’ve stood for all these decades?

ranked_items.content:
# March 28, 2022, 9:45 PM

Had a phone call with Haozi.

He said business has been pretty good lately, and the mortgage pressure isn't as heavy anymore. I originally wanted to ask him if he had any interest in mechanical engineering—if he wanted to know more about what I’ve been working on all these years... but the words died in my throat. I changed the subject and asked how work was going instead. He said it was fine, then asked if there was something I wanted to say.

I told him it was nothing, that everything was under control.

After hanging up, I stood in the living room for a long while, phone still in hand.

I thought about the tools in my workshop. Forty years. Some I modified myself; others I repaired after they broke. Every single one has a story, but those stories only exist in my head. I’ve recorded some of it in my notebooks, but what’s the point of recording things? Haozi won't read them. Yuyu won't read them either. They have their own lives, which is only natural.

But I still feel... wrong.

These things, this knowledge—it just ends here. I’ve been in this position for twelve years, proposing several improvements every year that the company adopted. But those will end, too. When I retire and the new manager arrives, he’ll have his own methods. The optimizations I made might eventually just become a statistic—"defect rate down by 25%"—and nothing more.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I didn't go down.

I pulled out the last physical exam report and read it again. Blood pressure is a bit high. The doctor said to be careful. Mine has started to fluctuate, too. It was stable for the last two years, but now...

I can't control it.

That’s what hurts the most. I can control workflows, quality standards, and my subordinates' performance. But I can't control time. I can't control my body. I can't control whether Haozi or Yuyu will inherit anything.

In the margin of one page in my notebook, I wrote: "Who will ever use this?" I didn't erase it.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. There’s a quality control meeting in the afternoon. A new employee’s report isn't detailed enough; I need to point it out. It’s my responsibility. But I’m getting a bit tired of this kind of thing. Or rather... I know where this frustration is coming from. It’s because I’m demanding perfection from others while I myself am losing my grip on it.

Thinking this way makes me feel a bit hypocritical.

Forget it. Time for bed. There’s a lot to do tomorrow.

I need to hurry while I can still move.

ranked_items.content:
It was already past 7 p.m. by the time I got home. My wife was busy in the kitchen; hearing the door, she poked her head out and asked if I’d eaten. I told her I hadn't, and she said the food was still warm.

I set down my briefcase and sat on the sofa. She brought over a bowl of soup. I took two sips and then just sat there, motionless.

As she was clearing the dishes, she asked, "What’s wrong? You seem a bit down today."

I didn't answer directly. After a moment, I finally said, "I didn't play very well today."

"Oh? Did you lose?" she asked.

"I didn't lose. It’s just... my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." I paused. "My chess partner said I was slow today. I felt it myself, too."

She sat down beside me. She didn't say anything, she just stayed there with me.

I spoke again: "Have you noticed anything about me lately?"

"Like what?" she asked.

"Just... feeling a bit tired." I rubbed my temples. "Maybe work has been too intense recently."

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to say. What I wanted to ask was: Have you noticed me changing? Do you think I'm getting old? But I couldn't bring myself to say those words.

"Then get some rest early," she said. "I'll make you some red bean porridge; it's good for you."

I nodded. She got up and headed for the kitchen. I sat on the sofa, looking at my hands—the knuckles were a bit stiff, the skin becoming loose.

I took out my phone and opened last week’s physical exam report, looking over the data once more. My blood sugar was 0.3 higher than last year, and my blood pressure was fluctuating. I remembered what the doctor said, but I hadn't told my wife.

She was humming a song in the kitchen, her voice soft and gentle. It suddenly occurred to me that her blood pressure had been rising too. We are both changing; we are both aging. The thought made me feel a dull ache.

I turned off the screen, leaned back against the sofa, and closed my eyes.

I still have to go to the office tomorrow. There are still those tools, those records. Time is moving on, and it feels like there is less and less I can do.

I stared at the board for nearly two minutes before making a move—something that never used to happen. My fingers were resting on the piece, but my mind was elsewhere—thinking about those figures in last week’s physical exam report, thinking about the dizzy spells I’ve been having some mornings.

I left early during the third round, claiming I had something to take care of at home. I didn’t. I just didn't want to keep going.

**Flag/Reminder: Contact the doctor next Monday. Ask if this recent sluggishness requires a follow-up. Might need a neurocognitive assessment.**

---

*Note: Review chess performance once a month. If things continue to deteriorate, must take the initiative to go to the hospital.*
ranked_items.subquestion: Old Chen, back then at the chess room, if you hadn't left early and had instead maintained that so-called "stability" of yours like you usually did, do you think that sense of powerlessness in your heart would have been eased, even just a little?

ranked_items.content:
It was already past 7 p.m. by the time I got home. My wife was busy in the kitchen; hearing the door, she poked her head out and asked if I’d eaten. I told her I hadn't, and she said the food was still warm.

I set down my briefcase and sat on the sofa. She brought over a bowl of soup. I took two sips and then just sat there, motionless.

As she was clearing the dishes, she asked, "What’s wrong? You seem a bit down today."

I didn't answer directly. After a moment, I finally said, "I didn't play very well today."

"Oh? Did you lose?" she asked.

"I didn't lose. It’s just... my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." I paused. "My chess partner said I was slow today. I felt it myself, too."

She sat down beside me. She didn't say anything, she just stayed there with me.

I spoke again: "Have you noticed anything about me lately?"

"Like what?" she asked.

"Just... feeling a bit tired." I rubbed my temples. "Maybe work has been too intense recently."

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to say. What I wanted to ask was: Have you noticed me changing? Do you think I'm getting old? But I couldn't bring myself to say those words.

"Then get some rest early," she said. "I'll make you some red bean porridge; it's good for you."

I nodded. She got up and headed for the kitchen. I sat on the sofa, looking at my hands—the knuckles were a bit stiff, the skin becoming loose.

I took out my phone and opened last week’s physical exam report, looking over the data once more. My blood sugar was 0.3 higher than last year, and my blood pressure was fluctuating. I remembered what the doctor said, but I hadn't told my wife.

She was humming a song in the kitchen, her voice soft and gentle. It suddenly occurred to me that her blood pressure had been rising too. We are both changing; we are both aging. The thought made me feel a dull ache.

I turned off the screen, leaned back against the sofa, and closed my eyes.

I still have to go to the office tomorrow. There are still those tools, those records. Time is moving on, and it feels like there is less and less I can do.
ranked_items.subquestion: Old Chen, back then at the chess room, if you hadn't left early and had instead maintained that so-called "stability" of yours like you usually did, do you think that sense of powerlessness in your heart would have been eased, even just a little?

ranked_items.content:
April 28, 2022, 11:15 PM

I’m sitting in the study. The lights are off. It’s pitch black outside the window.

I’ve been sitting here in the dark for a long time. My hand is pressed against my chest; I can feel my heartbeat. The rhythm is irregular. My breathing is a bit shallow, but I haven't moved. I just sit here, feeling it all.

The physical exam report is still on the desk. Blood pressure 139/88, fasting blood sugar 6.8. There’s a waveform on the ECG that the doctor said needs observation. He used the phrase "requires attention." I heard him loud and clear.

I’m thinking: this is how it begins.

I press my fingers against my pulse and count—76 beats per minute. Within the normal range. But I know it was racing just a moment ago. Why did it speed up? No exercise, nothing special happened. I was just sitting here when I suddenly felt... something I can't put into words.

Hao called today. He said the mortgage pressure is heavy and he might have to pay back a little less this month. I didn't say much; I just noted it down in my ledger. The mortgage, his sales commissions, the fluctuations of Shenzhen’s housing prices. I track all these data points. But what’s the use of tracking? Can I change anything?

The tools in the workshop are still on the shelves. Lately, I’ve been numbering them, writing down their uses and the years they were purchased. Some were bought in 1995, others in 2008. I wonder, if I were suddenly gone, who would know how to use these things? Not Hao. Not Yu. And certainly not Meifang.

They would just become scrap metal. Or be tossed out as junk.

I’ve been sitting in the dark for a long time, without turning on the light.

Tomorrow, I need to schedule a more detailed check-up. Cardiology. I need to know exactly what that abnormal waveform means. I need to establish a long-term monitoring plan. Every month’s data must be recorded so I can see the trends.

I also need to keep supervising Meifang’s blood pressure. She’s started eating salty foods again; I have to remind her once more.

My breathing has settled down a bit now.

But that sense of unease remains.

It’s as if something is slowly coming loose, and I can’t reach out to catch it.
ranked_items.subquestion: Old Chen, back then at the chess room, if you hadn't left early and had instead maintained that so-called "stability" of yours like you usually did, do you think that sense of powerlessness in your heart would have been eased, even just a little?

ranked_items.content:
April 5, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Wang Gang’s quality inspection report is still spinning in my head.

The formatting was off again when he handed it in this morning—the classification standards in the header didn't match last year’s specifications. It’s such a basic thing to get wrong. I couldn't help myself; I snapped at him. He tried to explain, but I cut him off. No excuses. Professionalism is about an obsession with detail.

His face changed the moment I said it.

Looking back now, my tone was harsh. Harsher than usual. He’s actually a decent employee. In the past, I would have pointed out these small mistakes, but never like that. Why today...?

My fingers have been tapping on the desk for a long while. Rapidly. I didn't even realize I was doing it.

What am I thinking? Was his report really that outrageous? Or... was I just taking it out on him?

It’s been like this lately. I’m becoming more demanding with my subordinates and more micro-managing with my wife. This morning, I was looking at her blood pressure logs again; a single high reading and I started feeling restless. I spent another whole night in the tool shed, numbering all the precision tools and documenting their uses. I’m terrified that one day I’ll suddenly... lose the ability to move, and these things will become completely useless because no one knows how to handle them.

Two years until retirement. Two years.

I know what I’m trying to do. I’m trying to control things. Control the work, the standards, everything at home—even the aging of my own body. It’s as if by managing every tiny detail, I can prove I’m still useful, that I can still... what?

Wang Gang has to come in for work tomorrow. I need to figure out how to handle this. Not for his sake, but because... I don’t want to become this kind of person. I don’t want to use harshness to mask my own fear.

But I don’t know how to change.

How much longer can I do this? How much longer can I keep things under control like this?
ranked_items.subquestion: Old Chen, back then at the chess room, if you hadn't left early and had instead maintained that so-called "stability" of yours like you usually did, do you think that sense of powerlessness in your heart would have been eased, even just a little?

ranked_items.content:
April 15, 2022. Evening.

Another entry today. Li Meifang added too much salt to the food at dinner. I tapped my bowl with my chopsticks. Her hand gave a little tremor. I got up, walked to the kitchen, took out my notebook, and wrote: "Added salt at dinner; blood pressure management inadequate."

She said she was afraid.

I didn't comfort her. I told her that data doesn't lie, and that this was for her own good. She kept her head down and didn't say another word.

It’s 7:30 PM now. She’s in the living room watching TV; I’m in the study. My hand is still trembling slightly.

It’s not out of anger. I have to admit, it’s not.

I’m thinking: if I can’t even manage her blood pressure, what else can I manage? Process optimization, quality standards—I can do all of that at work. But at home? She’s been used to my coldness for thirty-five years, and now that I’m using data and records to "care" for her, she’s actually afraid. What does that say?

I flip open my medical report. My blood sugar levels are fluctuating again. The doctor says it’s nothing major, but I know it’s the beginning. My body is weakening; I can feel it. I need to control everything more strictly—diet, routine, data—otherwise, things will spiral out of control.

But I’ve realized I can’t control her. I can’t control time. I can’t control where those tools will eventually end up.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, my daughter in Guangzhou. Neither of them will learn my craft. I’ve used this notebook to record the purpose of every tool, trying to turn the intangible into words, but halfway through, I stopped and asked myself—what’s the point? Who will read this? Who will care?

I’ll be retiring in two years. What then? No work, no processes to optimize, no data to manage. What will I have left?

She might be crying right now. I can’t hear it, but I know.

Should I go and apologize? But I was right. Her blood pressure *does* need managing. Data *is* objective.

Why does my rationality always hurt her? Why can’t I just say something soft like everyone else?

I keep flipping through the notebook. Page after page of entries. Neat, precise, and utterly cold.

Is this my life?

I put down the pen. My hand is still shaking.

To be continued tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
**MEMORANDUM**

Date: March 28, 2022, 21:45

---

**To-Do: Conversation with Chen Hao**

Status: Incomplete / Needs Rescheduling

I called Chen Hao tonight. I originally intended to ask if he had any interest in mechanical engineering and if he’d like to learn some basic craftsmanship. But when the words were on the tip of my tongue, I held back. Instead, I just asked how work was going. He said the mortgage pressure has been heavy lately and the market is unstable.

I didn't push further after hearing that.

Later, in the workshop, I replayed the conversation in my head. The questions are—

1. Is he truly uninterested, or is it that I’ve never properly talked to him about these things?
2. If I start teaching him now, is it too late? Look at how old he is already.
3. How can I bring it up next time without it feeling abrupt?

The items in the tool cabinet have been sitting there for so many years. Some were left by my father; others I collected myself. They are still high-precision instruments, wiped and maintained regularly. But if no one uses them, maintenance is pointless.

**Timing for Next Attempt:**
- Talk face-to-face when he comes home? Or stick to the phone?
- Start with his work stress first, then bring it up?
- Or should I just document the use and maintenance of every tool and show it to him when the chance arises?

**Progress Log:**
Finished the first 30 items on the tool list. About 20-plus items left to organize. I need to clearly write down the purpose, precision grade, and maintenance method for each. I need to hurry up with this.

While I’m still mobile, I need to get these things sorted out.

---

**Misc:**
Li Meifang ate salted fish again today; said a friend gave it to her. Her blood pressure has been fluctuating again this month. Must urge her to go for a check-up next week.

ranked_items.content:
# March 28, 2022, 9:45 PM

Had a phone call with Haozi.

He said business has been pretty good lately, and the mortgage pressure isn't as heavy anymore. I originally wanted to ask him if he had any interest in mechanical engineering—if he wanted to know more about what I’ve been working on all these years... but the words died in my throat. I changed the subject and asked how work was going instead. He said it was fine, then asked if there was something I wanted to say.

I told him it was nothing, that everything was under control.

After hanging up, I stood in the living room for a long while, phone still in hand.

I thought about the tools in my workshop. Forty years. Some I modified myself; others I repaired after they broke. Every single one has a story, but those stories only exist in my head. I’ve recorded some of it in my notebooks, but what’s the point of recording things? Haozi won't read them. Yuyu won't read them either. They have their own lives, which is only natural.

But I still feel... wrong.

These things, this knowledge—it just ends here. I’ve been in this position for twelve years, proposing several improvements every year that the company adopted. But those will end, too. When I retire and the new manager arrives, he’ll have his own methods. The optimizations I made might eventually just become a statistic—"defect rate down by 25%"—and nothing more.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I didn't go down.

I pulled out the last physical exam report and read it again. Blood pressure is a bit high. The doctor said to be careful. Mine has started to fluctuate, too. It was stable for the last two years, but now...

I can't control it.

That’s what hurts the most. I can control workflows, quality standards, and my subordinates' performance. But I can't control time. I can't control my body. I can't control whether Haozi or Yuyu will inherit anything.

In the margin of one page in my notebook, I wrote: "Who will ever use this?" I didn't erase it.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. There’s a quality control meeting in the afternoon. A new employee’s report isn't detailed enough; I need to point it out. It’s my responsibility. But I’m getting a bit tired of this kind of thing. Or rather... I know where this frustration is coming from. It’s because I’m demanding perfection from others while I myself am losing my grip on it.

Thinking this way makes me feel a bit hypocritical.

Forget it. Time for bed. There’s a lot to do tomorrow.

I need to hurry while I can still move.

ranked_items.content:
In the workshop, Chen Guilin sat on a simple wooden stool, a notebook open before him. On the tool rack, the precision instruments glinted coldly under the fluorescent lights. His phone screen lit up—a WeChat message from his wife, Li Meifang: "What do you want for dinner?"

He didn't reply immediately. Instead, he stared at the dense notes in his notebook and the words "Who would use this?" circled in red ink.

Five minutes later, he finally typed:

"Do you think... there's any point in me keeping these records?"

After sending it, he added another line:

"Just thinking about what happens after I retire. These tools will just be sitting there anyway."

He paused for a long time, stopping himself from typing more. His fingers hovered over the keyboard before he finally lowered them.

Then he added one last message:

"I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately. Not the work kind of tired."

---

*Chen Guilin put down his phone and turned back to wiping a precision vernier caliper on the rack. His movements were slow and meticulous, but his eyes were no longer on the tool.*

ranked_items.content:
March 20, 2022, 11:47 PM

Sitting in the workshop. My wife is already asleep. The noises downstairs have all quieted down.

I finished page thirty today. Starting from the first page, my handwriting was neat; the purpose, specifications, and purchase date of every tool were recorded clearly. But toward the end, the pen started to shake. I’m not sure when it began—maybe around page twenty-five. The strokes became irregular, and some words looked rushed.

When I got to the entry for the wrench, I stopped for a long time. I bought this wrench in 1998. It’s a Japanese import with a precision of 0.02 mm. I’ve used it for over twenty years, and it shows very little wear. I wanted to record its use, but the pen just hovered there. Then I wrote—"Who will ever use this?"

After writing that sentence, I didn't cross it out. It’s strange. I’m not usually like this. The notebooks on my desk have always been spotless—no scribbles, no traces of hesitation. But this time, looking at that sentence, I just couldn't bring myself to erase it.

Hao is in Shenzhen; Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them will ever come to this workshop. Hao is in real estate sales, and Yu teaches. They have their own lives. I never expected them to inherit anything, but sitting here now, looking at these tools, I suddenly realized—these things are about to become useless. Not because they’re broken or lost, but because no one will know how to use them.

This morning, Meifang asked me what I was busy with. I only replied with a grunt. She didn't ask again. She’s used to my silence. After thirty-five years, she’s long known that I’m a man of few words. But when that "mm-hmm" came out today, even I could hear it—it wasn't silence; it was avoidance. I don’t know how to explain to her what I’m doing. I can’t even explain it to myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. My blood sugar is up a bit again. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m in my fifties now; my body is starting to disobey me. I used to think that as long as I kept things under control and followed the plan, nothing would go wrong. But the body doesn't follow a plan. Neither does time.

I’m a little over a year away from retirement. I’ve been waiting for this day, but now that it’s almost here, I’m starting to feel afraid. Afraid of what I’ll do after I retire. Afraid of being idle. Afraid that these tools will just keep sitting here, unused and misunderstood.

The notebook is still open. "Who will ever use this" is still there on page thirty. Tomorrow, I have to keep writing. There are still many tools left to record. I have to get them all down while I can still move.

But even if I record them, what then?

May 18, 2022, 8:45 PM

I had another minor disagreement with Meifang today over the salt. She added an extra spoonful while simmering the soup; I caught the scent and went over immediately to check. She said, "You can't even taste the difference," and I didn't say much back, but I was thinking—her blood pressure is already at 140/92. I saw the last physical exam report clearly. Just one extra spoonful of salt can cause a five-point spike; it’s not a small matter.

I poured her a glass of water and told her to drink it first. Then I told her I’d be putting together a low-sodium meal plan for her this weekend. She didn’t object; she just gave me a smile—that look of someone who’s grown used to my ways.

While sitting in the workshop, I flipped through the tool log again. I’ve already recorded over thirty items this year, each marked with its purpose and maintenance method. Sometimes I write "Who will use this?" in the margins—a question for myself. Hao is selling real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou; neither of them will ever touch these things. I know that.

But I keep recording anyway. While I can still move.

The May physical is coming up. The cardiologist mentioned last time that I need regular follow-ups. I haven't told the family exactly what it's for, just that it's a routine check-up. Meifang asked once, and I said, "Nothing major, just some fluctuations in the data." Honestly, I don't really want to dwell on it myself.

Tonight, I glanced at a WeChat message from Hao. He said the mortgage pressure is a bit heavy this month and asked if I could support him a little more. I transferred five thousand yuan to him without saying much. He replied with a "Thanks, Dad." That was it. I tried to remember the last time he came home—was it three months ago? Or four? I couldn't recall.

I wonder, should I keep doing all this after I retire? Should I keep keeping the accounts? Should I keep polishing the tools? Should I keep managing everything in this house?

I have to get everything done that needs doing, while I still can.

I have to hurry.

Like last time when you were talking to Chen Hao—you clearly wanted to bring up your mechanical crafts, but you ended up just asking about work. Are you worried they won't actually be interested in these "treasures" of yours?

ranked_items.content:
July 14, 2022, 3:00 PM

Just got off the phone with the hospital. I moved my physical exam from July 25th up to tomorrow, July 15th.

The operator asked why I wanted to move it forward. I told her there was no special reason, just that I’m relatively free right now. That was a lie. I knew I was lying, but I couldn't explain the real reason. Maybe there isn't a reason at all, or maybe there are too many.

*The sooner it’s done, the better.* This sentence keeps looping in my head. It’s as if once I finish the check-up tomorrow and get the report, everything will be fine. But that logic doesn't hold up. I know that perfectly well.

My productivity has been low these past two days. I had a quality review meeting this morning and found myself repeating the same things; the expressions on my subordinates' faces told me they noticed it too. When I got back to the office this afternoon, I was supposed to organize some process documentation, but I sat there for half an hour without typing a single word. My appetite isn't great either; I only ate half of my lunch. Li Meifang asked if I was feeling unwell. I said no, I’m just not hungry.

My fingers have been tapping on the desk all afternoon. *Tap, tap, tap.* The rhythm is fast and restless. I can’t stand the sound of it myself, but I can’t stop. This isn't like me. I can usually control these things.

In the evening, I flipped through that tool logbook in the workshop. Looking at those dense notes, it suddenly felt a bit absurd. What am I recording? Who is going to use these tools? My eldest son is selling his house, and my daughter is teaching. Neither of them needs any of this. So why am I still keeping records?

I caught a glimpse of myself in the mirror. I look a bit pale.

The results will be out tomorrow afternoon. My heart is racing, but I tell myself it’s a normal physiological response. Just nerves. But why am I nervous? I should be feeling confident.

Whatever. Time for bed. Waking up at 6:00 AM tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
April 28, 2022, 11:15 PM

I’m sitting in the study. The lights are off. It’s pitch black outside the window.

I’ve been sitting here in the dark for a long time. My hand is pressed against my chest; I can feel my heartbeat. The rhythm is irregular. My breathing is a bit shallow, but I haven't moved. I just sit here, feeling it all.

The physical exam report is still on the desk. Blood pressure 139/88, fasting blood sugar 6.8. There’s a waveform on the ECG that the doctor said needs observation. He used the phrase "requires attention." I heard him loud and clear.

I’m thinking: this is how it begins.

I press my fingers against my pulse and count—76 beats per minute. Within the normal range. But I know it was racing just a moment ago. Why did it speed up? No exercise, nothing special happened. I was just sitting here when I suddenly felt... something I can't put into words.

Hao called today. He said the mortgage pressure is heavy and he might have to pay back a little less this month. I didn't say much; I just noted it down in my ledger. The mortgage, his sales commissions, the fluctuations of Shenzhen’s housing prices. I track all these data points. But what’s the use of tracking? Can I change anything?

The tools in the workshop are still on the shelves. Lately, I’ve been numbering them, writing down their uses and the years they were purchased. Some were bought in 1995, others in 2008. I wonder, if I were suddenly gone, who would know how to use these things? Not Hao. Not Yu. And certainly not Meifang.

They would just become scrap metal. Or be tossed out as junk.

I’ve been sitting in the dark for a long time, without turning on the light.

Tomorrow, I need to schedule a more detailed check-up. Cardiology. I need to know exactly what that abnormal waveform means. I need to establish a long-term monitoring plan. Every month’s data must be recorded so I can see the trends.

I also need to keep supervising Meifang’s blood pressure. She’s started eating salty foods again; I have to remind her once more.

My breathing has settled down a bit now.

But that sense of unease remains.

It’s as if something is slowly coming loose, and I can’t reach out to catch it.

I might be back late in the afternoon, so go ahead and eat without me.

ranked_items.content:
# May 8, 2022, past 10 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Meifang fell asleep a long while ago; I can hear her light snoring drifting in from the bedroom. Her blood pressure was a bit high again today. I saw the reading she took this afternoon, but I didn't tell her.

What happened at the chess room today keeps spinning in my head.

Midway through the game, I suddenly realized I was staring blankly at the board. My opponent asked what I was thinking about, and I said, "I'm getting older; my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." As soon as the words left my mouth, they felt foreign to me. I wouldn't have said that before. I would have said, "This is an interesting move," or I wouldn't have explained at all and just played the piece.

But today, I was just slow. The calculations in my head felt sluggish. I could feel it.

Player A tried to comfort me, saying my game has always been stable. I heard myself reply: "It’s stable because I’m afraid to change."

I regretted saying it immediately. Not because it wasn't true, but because it was *too* true. I’ve never been that blunt before. Stable... yes, I’ve always chased stability. Forty years, the same factory, the same way of working, the same rhythm of life. No job-hopping, no risks, every step planned out.

That’s not stability. That’s stagnation.

I left the chess room halfway through. That has never happened before. My friends were a bit surprised; I told them I was tired and needed to go home. As I turned to leave, I looked back at that place—the familiar boards, the familiar faces, everything so familiar. My eyes stung a little.

Why is this happening?

The tools in the workshop are still there. All these years, I’ve been wiping them down and organizing them, like I’m looking after something precious. But Hao is in real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked what those tools are for. Once, I tried to explain the use of a specific drill bit to Hao, but before I could finish, he said, "Dad, I’ve got a client I need to call back."

I’m recording the purpose of every tool in a notebook. Writing them down, one by one. But halfway through, I asked myself—what’s the point? Who is ever going to look at this notebook?

The physical exam report is still sitting in the drawer. The doctor said those figures "require attention." Every two days, I take it out and look at it, staring at the numbers, trying to find some kind of pattern. As if by doing that, I could control something. But the truth is, I can’t control anything. Blood pressure rises, blood sugar fluctuates, reactions slow down. These things are happening, and all I can do is record them.

Meifang’s blood pressure is rising too. I’m monitoring her diet and pushing her to get checkups. But what if one day I can’t move either? In our thirty-five years together, we’ve said too little. Now, even if I want to make up for it, I can't.

How much time is left?

I don't know. But I can feel time accelerating. It’s as if I used to have plenty of time to do something, and now I suddenly realize it’s gone. Retirement in two years, and then what? Empty days, no work, no one wanting anything from me, no one inheriting anything I know.

I sit here, pen in hand, but I don’t know what to write.

Maybe it’s best to just keep working tomorrow. At least when I’m working, I still know what I’m doing.

Participants: Player A, Player B, Player C

---

**Log:**
Today’s afternoon game. During the second round, my reactions were noticeably slower, and my strategy felt muddled. Player A asked if I’d been overworked lately; I told him no, I was just having an off day. But it’s not about being tired.

I stared at the board for nearly two minutes before making a move—something that never used to happen. My fingers were resting on the piece, but my mind was elsewhere—thinking about those figures in last week’s physical exam report, thinking about the dizzy spells I’ve been having some mornings.

I left early during the third round, claiming I had something to take care of at home. I didn’t. I just didn't want to keep going.

**Flag/Reminder: Contact the doctor next Monday. Ask if this recent sluggishness requires a follow-up. Might need a neurocognitive assessment.**

---

*Note: Review chess performance once a month. If things continue to deteriorate, must take the initiative to go to the hospital.*

**Date**: June 2, 2022, 6:30 AM – 6:50 AM  
**Location**: Community Gym Treadmill  
**Event Type**: Physical Abnormality / Consultation Pending  

---

## Event Log

Started my morning run at 6:30 AM. At the 8-minute mark, the treadmill showed my heart rate suddenly jumping to 128, which didn't feel right. I cross-checked the machine's data by manually counting my pulse at the wrist, and the results were inconsistent. I stopped to rest for 5 minutes and re-measured; the value only then returned to the normal range (68–72).

No obvious chest tightness or difficulty breathing, but I felt my rhythm was completely thrown off.

---

## Points of Concern

- [ ] Check May’s physical exam report for any abnormal heart rate indicators.
- [ ] Ask the doctor: Are heart rate fluctuations during exercise normal, or is further testing required?
- [ ] Verify the accuracy of the treadmill sensors (consider a professional test at the hospital next week).
- [ ] Avoid high-intensity exercise this week; switch to walking or Tai Chi instead.

---

## Related Matters

**Next Physical Exam**: Completed in mid-May; should still have the report on hand.  
**To Mention to Doctor**: This incident, plus recent feelings of slower recovery from fatigue.  
**Li Meifang**: Don't tell her; no need to make her worry again.

---

*Log Time: 10:15 AM*  
*Status: Returned to normal, but requires detailed consultation during the next check-up.*
