ranked_items.content:
# August 2, 2022, 8:30 PM

Sitting in the study. My wife is watching TV in the living room, the volume turned down low. I intended to organize today’s exercise logs, but right now, I can’t do anything.

I lost at the chess club today. That rarely happens.

It wasn't because my skills have declined—though perhaps they have—it was because I simply couldn't focus. When my friend A asked what was wrong, I just... let it out. About the medical report. Blood pressure 147/92, blood sugar 6.8, and a slight abnormality on the ECG. I hadn't even figured out how to say it, and yet there it was.

That’s not like me.

A said it was fine, that many people are like this and I just need regular check-ups. His words sounded hollow. I know he meant well, but he doesn't understand at all. No one can. This isn't just about abnormal data.

I’ve been controlling everything. Recording my wife’s blood pressure every day, watching everything she eats, walking 8,000 steps myself, weighing my diet down to the gram, organizing all the data into spreadsheets. I thought that by doing this, I could... I could stop something. But now I realize it’s all futile. Data can’t change anything.

Maybe I should retire early. I startled myself when I heard those words come out of my mouth. But once the thought appeared, I couldn't shake it. If my body is truly starting to fail, how much time do I have left? How much time is left for the things I haven't done yet?

The tools in the workshop, those ideas, and... I haven't even had a real conversation with Hao and Yu. They are in Shenzhen and Guangzhou, while I stay here recording data as if that were a form of love.

When I lost the game, I looked at the black and white pieces on the board and suddenly felt exhausted. Not a physical exhaustion, but something else.

It’s 8:30 PM. I have work tomorrow. Next week is the department’s quality assessment. Everything continues as if nothing has changed. But everything has changed.

I don’t know what to do. Or rather, I know what to do—make a plan, adjust the regimen, control the variables—but this time, I realize there are just too many things beyond my control.

Maybe I should sleep. I have to record the blood pressure data tomorrow.
ranked_items.subquestion: Guilin, after this diagnosis, have you also started to feel that the way of life you've led for the past forty years—where everything had to be precisely controlled—actually has its moments of utter helplessness?

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 9:45 PM

I’ve been in the workshop for nearly three hours. Li Meifang knocked twice; I didn’t answer.

The morning’s medical report is still spinning in my head. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, Stage 2 hypertension, and that phrase—"pre-metabolic syndrome." The doctor spoke calmly, as if reading a production report, but I caught the "you should be worried" undertone. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. Long-term medication.

I’m sitting at the workbench, holding a pair of pliers, wiping them over and over with a piece of flannel. How many times have I wiped them? I’ve lost count. Usually, I follow a system—precision measuring tools in one group, cutting tools in another, fasteners in a third. Not today. I just pick up whatever is at hand, wipe it, put it back. Pick up another. My movements are slow because my hands are shaking slightly.

I don’t want to admit that.

Forty years. For forty years, I’ve been in control—schedules, diet logs, regular check-ups. My desk is organized without a single stray wire. The household ledgers are accurate to the penny. I thought that was enough. I thought that with enough precision, enough discipline, I could keep everything in my grasp.

But the doctor said it’s not enough.

That sentence keeps ringing in my ears—the one Li Meifang said: "I knew you’d get this sooner or later." When did she know? How did she know? Was she pitying me, or was she saying, "I saw through you long ago"? I don’t want to ask, because I’m afraid of the answer.

110 mg/dL. That number is wrong. Something has gone wrong with the management of my life, but I can’t find where. Or rather, I can’t find a solution at all. Pills? Long-term medication? What does that mean? It means my body has begun to betray me, and I cannot reverse it through stricter management.

How much longer?

The question has suddenly become urgent. How much longer can I work? How much longer until I see Chen Hao’s mortgage paid off? How much longer until I pass these tools, these forty years of things, on to someone? I never gave it serious thought because I assumed there was plenty of time.

Now, I’m not sure.

The tools are still here, piece by piece. My hands are still shaking.

I have to go to work tomorrow, read production reports, point out mistakes to my subordinates. Everything must continue. But I don’t know how much longer I can keep going.

I need to look at that report again. Maybe I missed something. Maybe the numbers will change if I scrutinize them enough.

But I know they won’t.
ranked_items.subquestion: Guilin, after this diagnosis, have you also started to feel that the way of life you've led for the past forty years—where everything had to be precisely controlled—actually has its moments of utter helplessness?

ranked_items.content:
After returning home that evening, Chen Guilin changed his clothes and sat down at the dining table. Li Meifang was serving the soup.

He ate a few mouthfuls of rice in silence before putting down his chopsticks.

"I might have been too harsh at the department meeting today," he said, his voice low, eyes fixed on his bowl. "The new QC inspector messed up the data entry again, and I called him out in front of everyone. He looked pretty shaken."

Li Meifang looked up at him but didn't respond immediately.

"My temper has been short lately," he paused, tapping his finger on the table. "I can feel it myself. I have high standards for the work, but..." He didn't finish the sentence, opting for a sip of soup instead.

He turned to his wife, his tone tinged with uncertainty. "Do you think I'm being too demanding? I want to get things done right, but it seems like..." He hesitated, "it seems like I always end up making people uncomfortable."

He wasn't looking for comfort; it was more like he was trying to confirm something. There was exhaustion in his eyes, along with a certain guilt he couldn't quite put into words.

"The physical exam is next week," he suddenly changed the subject, though his tone remained heavy. "I need to get to bed early these next few days."

He lowered his head and continued eating, but his movements were much slower than usual.
ranked_items.subquestion: Guilin, after this diagnosis, have you also started to feel that the way of life you've led for the past forty years—where everything had to be precisely controlled—actually has its moments of utter helplessness?

ranked_items.content:
3:20 AM. The phone screen lit up. Chen Guilin stared at the WeChat chat box. His wife’s message was still there: "Are you okay? Do you want some warm water?"

His fingers hovered over the keyboard for a long time.

Finally, he turned off the screen.

---

Daylight broke.

The light from outside slowly filtered in. Chen Guilin had sat on the sofa all night, and the tightness in his chest had finally eased a bit. He touched his chest and took a few deep breaths. Reason told him it was just anxiety. But reason didn't seem to count for much in the early hours of the morning.

He picked up his phone. His wife’s message was still there. He checked the time—6:47 AM. Li Meifang would be waking up soon.

He started typing, then deleted it. Typed again, and deleted it again.

Finally, he sent:

"Good morning. I'm fine, just stayed up thinking about work and missed your message, sorry. How’s your blood pressure? Remember to eat breakfast."

After hitting send, he put the phone down and walked to the kitchen. There was some porridge left over from yesterday in the fridge. He scooped out a bowl and set it on the dining table.

Then he sat down and opened the thick health logbook. Turning to yesterday’s page, he noted down: "3:00 AM, chest tightness, shortness of breath, lasted about 20 minutes, then subsided."

His handwriting was neat, but he pressed down so hard that the tip of the pen nearly tore through the paper.

He looked at the line of text and added one more sentence: "Likely an anxiety response."

Then he closed the book and waited for his wife to wake up.
ranked_items.subquestion: Guilin, after this diagnosis, have you also started to feel that the way of life you've led for the past forty years—where everything had to be precisely controlled—actually has its moments of utter helplessness?

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 4:45 PM

Just got back from the hospital. My hands are still shaking.

I wrote down everything the doctor said. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, systolic 160, diastolic 100—Stage 2 hypertension. And then that phrase: pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. I asked him if he was sure; he said he was. Then he wrote out a stack of prescriptions and said I’d need long-term medication.

Long-term medication.

I kept poking at those three words on the prescription slip for the longest time. The tip of my pen nearly tore through the paper. The doctor probably noticed, but he didn't say anything.

Forty years. I’ve worked at this plant for forty years. Up at 6:30 AM, in bed by 10:00 PM. Never missed a day in my work log. Portioned meals, regular exercise, timely check-ups. I thought that would be enough. I thought that through precise management—by keeping everything under my thumb—my body wouldn't betray me.

Now the doctor is telling me it wasn't enough.

I always felt I was different from the others. Everyone else is so careless, while I am precise down to the minute. I thought that was an advantage. And now? Now I realize there are far more things I can’t control than things I can. The things happening inside my body—I can’t see them, I can’t touch them, and I can’t change them.

My wife said, "I knew you'd end up with this sooner or later." Her words made me angry, but I didn't argue back. Because I couldn't. She probably saw it coming long ago, while I was still lying to myself.

I need to start the medication. Every day. Probably for a long time. Maybe for the rest of my life.

How much longer is left? I keep wondering. If my body is already like this, how much longer can I work? Can I hold on for two more years until retirement? Or should I leave early? But what does early retirement even mean? What do I have left besides work?

All those tools in the workshop. Collected over forty years. And those process improvement plans. If I collapse, who takes over? Hao and Yu aren't in this industry. They might not even know what any of it is worth.

My head is a mess right now. I should calm down and make a plan. But I find that I can't. Because I don't know what I'm supposed to be planning for.

My hands are still shaking.

I need to put these pills in a fixed spot. Record the dosage every day. I can do that. I must do that.

But as for everything else?

I don't know.
ranked_items.subquestion: Guilin, after this diagnosis, have you also started to feel that the way of life you've led for the past forty years—where everything had to be precisely controlled—actually has its moments of utter helplessness?

07e0dc76-f092-45f2-b737-80b5598d8bff.question: Over the past six months or so, has your perspective on "taking control of your life" changed much? From your initial obsession with tracking every data point to where you are now, how do you view this sense of control?

ranked_items.content:
# Memo

**Date**: Wednesday, August 24, 2022, 14:00

**Subject**: Reflections following a game of chess

---

## Record

During today’s game with Player A, he said something that stuck with me: "Instead of spending time fearing the future, spend it cherishing the present."

I didn't respond at the time, but I haven't stopped thinking about it since.

## Issues to Sort Out

1. **What am I doing?**
   - My recent anxiety cycle: Prediction → Fear → Control → More Fear.
   - Since the physical exam results came back, I’ve been using data, plans, and logs to fight uncertainty.
   - But is this actually helpful? Or am I just wearing myself out?

2. **On Control**
   - What I can control: Workflows, financial accounts, daily schedules.
   - What I cannot control: Aging, fluctuating health metrics, my children’s choices, time itself.
   - I have been trying to fill the void of uncontrollable fears with things I can control.
   - What is the cost of doing this?

3. **On the Present**
   - For the past few months, I’ve been planning for a retirement that is two years away.
   - At the same time, I’m worrying about potential health issues.
   - The "present" I hold in my hands has been squeezed into something tiny.
   - My wife, the chess games, the tools in my workshop—they are all still here, but mentally, I’ve already let go of them.

4. **Defining Value**
   - I’ve always believed: Completion → Results → Proof of self-worth.
   - But what if things can’t be completed? What if the results are forgotten?
   - Perhaps value is more than just these things?

## For Further Reflection

- Why do I keep losing what I have now by worrying about what I might lose in the future?
- Is accepting the unchangeable an act of giving up, or an act of maturity?
- How much more time can I afford to waste on fear?

---

**Note**: When I play Player A again next week, I want to hear his take on "cherishing the present." Not as small talk, but because I truly want to understand.
ranked_items.subquestion: Over the past six months or so, has your perspective on "taking control of your life" changed much? From your initial obsession with tracking every data point to where you are now, how do you view this sense of control?

ranked_items.content:
July 5, 2022, 6:45 PM

Had a call with Hao. He said the real estate market has been a bit cold lately, but he’s holding on. I asked him if he’d ever thought about learning something about mechanics—maybe it could be a hobby later on. He laughed and said, "Dad, what are you talking about? I don’t have the brain for that." I didn't push it further.

After hanging up, I sat in the workshop for a long time.

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to ask. What I wanted to ask was—has he ever thought about what will happen to all these tools, all these things I’ve spent forty years learning? But I couldn't get the words out. Just like I never can.

During our call this afternoon, I told him "everything is under control." Thinking back on that phrase now, it feels a bit ridiculous. What does "under control" even mean? What am I controlling? Hao is in Shenzhen worrying about his mortgage, Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou, and here I am, documenting the uses of these tools as if I’m in a race against time. But time isn't racing me—it doesn't care about me at all.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I can hear her coughing. It’s time to nag her about going to the hospital to check her blood pressure again. These are things I can control. But what’s the use of controlling them?

The tool records in my notebook are halfway finished. With every entry I write, I wonder—who is going to use this? Hao? No. Yu? Even less likely. So why am I writing all this?

Maybe it’s just to keep myself from being idle. To tell myself there’s still something left to do.

The physical exam report from May is still in the drawer. A few of the indicators fluctuated a bit. The doctor said it’s nothing serious, just needs regular follow-ups. But I keep taking it out, looking at those numbers over and over. As if I could find some hidden meaning in them.

I’ve never known how to talk to them. That’s the truth. It’s been that way since they were little. I thought it would get better as they grew up, but it seems to have gotten worse. Now, I even self-censor before asking a simple question, terrified of saying something that might cause awkwardness.

Maybe I should have started teaching them earlier. But back then, I was busy with work. Now that I want to teach, it’s already too late.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still over thirty items left to record.

I should do it while I can still move.

ranked_items.content:
I’ve been thinking about what you said for a long time.

Ever since I got back from the chess club, I’ve spent the entire afternoon reflecting on your words—that instead of spending time fearing the future, it’s better to spend it cherishing the present.

I admit, I’ve been feeling trapped lately. Medical reports, retirement plans, the meaning of work... it’s like I’ve been trying to use these things to fill a bottomless pit. The more I try to fill it, the more anxious I get, and the more chaotic everything becomes. You’re right; I’ve been living in fear, not actually living.

I’ve never told anyone this before. You’re the first.

Over the past couple of days, I’ve been thinking that maybe I should stop. Not stop working, but stop this... need for control. Stop the constant calculating and worrying every single day. I’m not sure if that’s exactly what you meant, but at the very least, your words have opened up a crack for me to see the world outside.

Thank you. Let’s get back to our game next week.

ranked_items.content:
# March 28, 2022, 9:45 PM

Had a phone call with Haozi.

He said business has been pretty good lately, and the mortgage pressure isn't as heavy anymore. I originally wanted to ask him if he had any interest in mechanical engineering—if he wanted to know more about what I’ve been working on all these years... but the words died in my throat. I changed the subject and asked how work was going instead. He said it was fine, then asked if there was something I wanted to say.

I told him it was nothing, that everything was under control.

After hanging up, I stood in the living room for a long while, phone still in hand.

I thought about the tools in my workshop. Forty years. Some I modified myself; others I repaired after they broke. Every single one has a story, but those stories only exist in my head. I’ve recorded some of it in my notebooks, but what’s the point of recording things? Haozi won't read them. Yuyu won't read them either. They have their own lives, which is only natural.

But I still feel... wrong.

These things, this knowledge—it just ends here. I’ve been in this position for twelve years, proposing several improvements every year that the company adopted. But those will end, too. When I retire and the new manager arrives, he’ll have his own methods. The optimizations I made might eventually just become a statistic—"defect rate down by 25%"—and nothing more.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I didn't go down.

I pulled out the last physical exam report and read it again. Blood pressure is a bit high. The doctor said to be careful. Mine has started to fluctuate, too. It was stable for the last two years, but now...

I can't control it.

That’s what hurts the most. I can control workflows, quality standards, and my subordinates' performance. But I can't control time. I can't control my body. I can't control whether Haozi or Yuyu will inherit anything.

In the margin of one page in my notebook, I wrote: "Who will ever use this?" I didn't erase it.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. There’s a quality control meeting in the afternoon. A new employee’s report isn't detailed enough; I need to point it out. It’s my responsibility. But I’m getting a bit tired of this kind of thing. Or rather... I know where this frustration is coming from. It’s because I’m demanding perfection from others while I myself am losing my grip on it.

Thinking this way makes me feel a bit hypocritical.

Forget it. Time for bed. There’s a lot to do tomorrow.

I need to hurry while I can still move.

ranked_items.content:
# August 2, 2022, 8:30 PM

Sitting in the study. My wife is watching TV in the living room, the volume turned down low. I intended to organize today’s exercise logs, but right now, I can’t do anything.

I lost at the chess club today. That rarely happens.

It wasn't because my skills have declined—though perhaps they have—it was because I simply couldn't focus. When my friend A asked what was wrong, I just... let it out. About the medical report. Blood pressure 147/92, blood sugar 6.8, and a slight abnormality on the ECG. I hadn't even figured out how to say it, and yet there it was.

That’s not like me.

A said it was fine, that many people are like this and I just need regular check-ups. His words sounded hollow. I know he meant well, but he doesn't understand at all. No one can. This isn't just about abnormal data.

I’ve been controlling everything. Recording my wife’s blood pressure every day, watching everything she eats, walking 8,000 steps myself, weighing my diet down to the gram, organizing all the data into spreadsheets. I thought that by doing this, I could... I could stop something. But now I realize it’s all futile. Data can’t change anything.

Maybe I should retire early. I startled myself when I heard those words come out of my mouth. But once the thought appeared, I couldn't shake it. If my body is truly starting to fail, how much time do I have left? How much time is left for the things I haven't done yet?

The tools in the workshop, those ideas, and... I haven't even had a real conversation with Hao and Yu. They are in Shenzhen and Guangzhou, while I stay here recording data as if that were a form of love.

When I lost the game, I looked at the black and white pieces on the board and suddenly felt exhausted. Not a physical exhaustion, but something else.

It’s 8:30 PM. I have work tomorrow. Next week is the department’s quality assessment. Everything continues as if nothing has changed. But everything has changed.

I don’t know what to do. Or rather, I know what to do—make a plan, adjust the regimen, control the variables—but this time, I realize there are just too many things beyond my control.

Maybe I should sleep. I have to record the blood pressure data tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
June 15, 2022, 7:30 PM

I lost again at the chess room this afternoon. It wasn't that I lost to Player A; I lost to myself.

Player A said I played very conservatively today. The word he used was "cautious," but I could hear what he really meant—he was saying I’ve changed. I didn’t argue. I just said, "I’m getting older, I have to be more careful," and kept setting up the pieces. He didn’t say anything else.

But I know he saw it. I saw it in myself, too.

I used to dare to take risks, to make bold moves in the mid-game. And now? Now I pause and think for a long time before every single step. It’s not because I’ve become smarter; it’s because I’m afraid—afraid that one wrong move will lead to a point of no return. Afraid of wasting time.

It’s strange. A game only lasts two hours, and losing is just losing a game. But I find myself applying this same logic to everything in my life. I’m stricter with my subordinates at work, I’m more controlling over Meifang’s diet at home, and I even use a magnifying glass to scrutinize the numbers on my medical reports.

Why?

I tell myself this is being responsible. But deep down, I know it’s fear. Fear that time is running out. Fear that something is slipping through my fingers and I won’t be able to catch it in time.

The tools in the workshop are still there. The notebooks are there, too. I’ve recorded about half of it. Hao is in Shenzhen, and Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them has ever asked what those tools are for. I haven't brought it up, either.

Sometimes I wonder, living this cautiously—what exactly am I trying to protect?

I have to go back to the chess room tomorrow. I wonder if Player A will notice something else.

Forget it, stop thinking. Time for bed. I haven't finished going through the medical report from May; I’ll give it another careful look tomorrow.

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 8:15 PM

I’ve been sitting in the workshop for two hours. I’m wiping down that 1985 vernier caliper, my movements slow. My wife is watching TV in the living room; I can hear the sound, but I don’t want to go out.

110 mg/dL. Stage 2 hypertension. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis.

The doctor spoke those words calmly, as if reading a product inspection report. But I heard them clearly. Every word felt like a nail being driven in. I’ve always done my checkups, always logged the data, always believed I was in control. Wake up at 6:00 AM, sleep at 10:00 PM, a light diet, regular exercise. For forty years, that’s how I’ve lived.

My wife said, "I knew you’d get this sooner or later."

That sentence stung more than the diagnosis itself. When did she see through me? I thought I’d hidden it well. I thought that through precise management, I could stop aging, stop my body from betraying me. But she knew all along. What does that mean? Does it mean my efforts look like a joke to everyone else?

Long-term medication. The doctor said I need long-term medication.

I don’t know how long "long-term" is. One year? Five? Ten? Until I die? I’ve never taken medicine long-term. I rarely even catch a cold. Now I have to take pills for the rest of my life just to maintain a number—110. The number itself is meaningless, but what it represents matters. It represents my loss of control over my own body.

My wife said she’d go with me to get the prescription filled tomorrow. I didn’t respond, just nodded, and turned to walk into this workshop. I didn’t want her to see my hands shaking.

The tools are still here. Dozens of them. I spent forty years collecting them. Each one represents a stage, a decision, a moment I thought was important. Now I’m wondering, if I collapse, what happens to these things? Chen Hao doesn't understand mechanics. Neither does Chen Yu. Will they be treated as scrap metal?

I donated some tools to the industrial technical school. Was that the right decision? Or was it just a disguised way of settling my estate?

I don’t want to think like this. But I can’t help it.

The doctor said I need to change my lifestyle. Reduce stress. Maintain a routine. I’m already doing those things. Does that mean I haven’t done enough? Or does it mean none of it matters?

My hands are still shaking. I put the caliper down.

I have to get the medicine tomorrow. I need to make a list—drug names, usage, side effects, prices, insurance coverage. I need to turn this into data, into something manageable. That’s the only way I’ll feel at peace.

But I know that some things simply cannot be managed.

ranked_items.content:
# Monday, July 18, 2022, 14:30

It’s been two hours since I got the report.

I’m sitting in my office with the report spread out on the desk. I told everyone "it’s nothing serious," but my fingers keep tracing those marked spots—blood pressure 139/92, fasting blood glucose 6.8. The numbers themselves don’t lie. I know that better than anyone.

I heard everything the doctor said, but I chose to remember only the first half of his sentences. "It’s under decent control." The rest—the parts about "needing to be more cautious" and "regular follow-ups"—I just let slide. That’s not my style.

Meifang was cooking when I got home. She saw the look on my face and asked about it. I didn't give her a straight answer, just said "I'm fine." But I know she can tell. After 35 years, she knows me too well. She tried to come closer, wanting me to talk, but I turned away and went into the study.

Then I suddenly turned back around and said to her, "Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We can't let your blood pressure issues drag on anymore. I’m going to set up a plan for you."

She was startled by my tone. I saw it. But I didn't stop. I need to control something. I need to ensure that something is within my grasp.

She isn't the problem. I know that. But at this moment, I can't face the fact that *I* am the problem.

Data doesn't lie. I’ve lived my whole life by data and standards, and now the data is starting to betray me. Blood pressure, blood sugar, weight—every indicator is slowly crossing the line. I can see the trend. I can predict it. And that is exactly what’s so terrifying.

The two-year retirement plan. I spoke of it with such certainty. But now I’m starting to wonder: if my body continues like this, how many "two years" do I have left?

The tools in the workshop are still there. I wanted to go and wipe them down, as I usually do. But today, I didn't move. I sat in my office, staring at this report, trying to use logic to convince myself that this is all within manageable limits.

But I know that some things are forever beyond my control.

For the first time, I truly felt that.

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date:** May 18, 2022, 8:45 PM
**Subject:** Li Meifang’s Dietary Management – Implementation of Low-Sodium Diet Plan

---

## Incident Log

During dinner, I noticed Li Meifang adding another spoonful of salt to the braised fish. When asked why, she said, "It needs it for the flavor." I didn't say much at the time, but I immediately thought of last week’s physical exam report—her blood pressure was 139/88, up 5 points from last month.

This is not a trivial matter.

## Data Correlation

- Last month’s BP: 134/83
- This month’s BP: 139/88
- Fluctuation: +5 mmHg (Systolic)
- Likely triggers: Excessive sodium intake + irregular sleep schedule

A spoonful of salt may seem insignificant, but the cumulative effect is clear. Intervention is necessary.

## Action Plan

**Phase 1 (This week):**
- Purchase low-sodium salt to replace the current kitchen salt.
- Develop daily menus to limit sodium intake to ≤ 2000mg.
- Record daily blood pressure readings (once in the morning, once in the evening).

**Phase 2 (Starting next week):**
- Prepare a list of low-sodium recipes (3 rotating sets each for breakfast, lunch, and dinner).
- Reduce the use of pickled foods and sauces.
- Ensure she takes her blood pressure medication on time.

**Monitoring Schedule:**
- Check the seasoning of dishes every day before dinner.
- Record blood pressure data every Monday and Thursday.
- Compare results during next month’s follow-up.

## Points of Concern

The issue with Li Meifang is that she doesn't see this as a problem. She says, "It's just a little salt," but she doesn't understand the cumulative effect. I need her to realize that this isn't me being nitpicky—it's what the data shows.

I’ve already told her that I will help her implement this plan. It’s not about her keeping track; it’s about me taking control. That is the only way to ensure it works.

## Next Steps

A follow-up blood pressure check is scheduled for May 25. If it’s still rising, we’ll need to consider increasing her exercise or adjusting her medication. But first, the diet must be brought under control.

Time is of the essence. We need to manage these details while we still can.

---

**Note:** Remind Li Meifang tomorrow morning about her regular check-up. She always procrastinates, but we can't let it slide this time.
ranked_items.subquestion: Chen, looking back on this period, the way you care for Meifang seems to have changed a lot. You went from strictly monitoring her salt intake to taking the initiative to delete the management logs and simply staying by her side. What made you have this change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
April 15, 2022. Evening.

Another entry today. Li Meifang added too much salt to the food at dinner. I tapped my bowl with my chopsticks. Her hand gave a little tremor. I got up, walked to the kitchen, took out my notebook, and wrote: "Added salt at dinner; blood pressure management inadequate."

She said she was afraid.

I didn't comfort her. I told her that data doesn't lie, and that this was for her own good. She kept her head down and didn't say another word.

It’s 7:30 PM now. She’s in the living room watching TV; I’m in the study. My hand is still trembling slightly.

It’s not out of anger. I have to admit, it’s not.

I’m thinking: if I can’t even manage her blood pressure, what else can I manage? Process optimization, quality standards—I can do all of that at work. But at home? She’s been used to my coldness for thirty-five years, and now that I’m using data and records to "care" for her, she’s actually afraid. What does that say?

I flip open my medical report. My blood sugar levels are fluctuating again. The doctor says it’s nothing major, but I know it’s the beginning. My body is weakening; I can feel it. I need to control everything more strictly—diet, routine, data—otherwise, things will spiral out of control.

But I’ve realized I can’t control her. I can’t control time. I can’t control where those tools will eventually end up.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, my daughter in Guangzhou. Neither of them will learn my craft. I’ve used this notebook to record the purpose of every tool, trying to turn the intangible into words, but halfway through, I stopped and asked myself—what’s the point? Who will read this? Who will care?

I’ll be retiring in two years. What then? No work, no processes to optimize, no data to manage. What will I have left?

She might be crying right now. I can’t hear it, but I know.

Should I go and apologize? But I was right. Her blood pressure *does* need managing. Data *is* objective.

Why does my rationality always hurt her? Why can’t I just say something soft like everyone else?

I keep flipping through the notebook. Page after page of entries. Neat, precise, and utterly cold.

Is this my life?

I put down the pen. My hand is still shaking.

To be continued tomorrow.
ranked_items.subquestion: Chen, looking back on this period, the way you care for Meifang seems to have changed a lot. You went from strictly monitoring her salt intake to taking the initiative to delete the management logs and simply staying by her side. What made you have this change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
May 18, 2022, 8:45 PM

I had another minor disagreement with Meifang today over the salt. She added an extra spoonful while simmering the soup; I caught the scent and went over immediately to check. She said, "You can't even taste the difference," and I didn't say much back, but I was thinking—her blood pressure is already at 140/92. I saw the last physical exam report clearly. Just one extra spoonful of salt can cause a five-point spike; it’s not a small matter.

I poured her a glass of water and told her to drink it first. Then I told her I’d be putting together a low-sodium meal plan for her this weekend. She didn’t object; she just gave me a smile—that look of someone who’s grown used to my ways.

While sitting in the workshop, I flipped through the tool log again. I’ve already recorded over thirty items this year, each marked with its purpose and maintenance method. Sometimes I write "Who will use this?" in the margins—a question for myself. Hao is selling real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou; neither of them will ever touch these things. I know that.

But I keep recording anyway. While I can still move.

The May physical is coming up. The cardiologist mentioned last time that I need regular follow-ups. I haven't told the family exactly what it's for, just that it's a routine check-up. Meifang asked once, and I said, "Nothing major, just some fluctuations in the data." Honestly, I don't really want to dwell on it myself.

Tonight, I glanced at a WeChat message from Hao. He said the mortgage pressure is a bit heavy this month and asked if I could support him a little more. I transferred five thousand yuan to him without saying much. He replied with a "Thanks, Dad." That was it. I tried to remember the last time he came home—was it three months ago? Or four? I couldn't recall.

I wonder, should I keep doing all this after I retire? Should I keep keeping the accounts? Should I keep polishing the tools? Should I keep managing everything in this house?

I have to get everything done that needs doing, while I still can.

I have to hurry.

# Health Data Analysis Memo

**Recorded: August 15, 2022, 03:20**

Abnormal blood pressure readings for Li Meifang over the past 7 days:
- Aug 9: 142/91
- Aug 10: 138/88
- Aug 11: 145/93 ⚠️
- Aug 12: 141/90
- Aug 13: 139/87
- Aug 14: 143/92 ⚠️
- Aug 15: 144/91 ⚠️

**Analysis:** Readings have exceeded 140/90 for five consecutive days. Despite adjusting her salt intake (<6g per day) and increasing her walking time (from 30 to 45 minutes), the results are negligible. This suggests that diet and exercise adjustments alone may no longer be sufficient. I need to consider recommending a hospital follow-up; her antihypertensive medication might need adjustment. However, she has always been reluctant to take her pills—this is a problem that still needs a solution.

---

**Personal Data Trend Forecast:**

Last physical (May 2022) Fasting Blood Glucose: 5.8 mmol/L
Self-test this month (mid-August): 6.2 mmol/L

At this rate of increase, I could enter the pre-diabetic range (≥6.1) within six months. There is still time to turn things around, but I can't delay any longer. I haven't been consistent with my exercise lately; it’s too easy to slack off when work stress piles up. I must establish a stricter plan—at least 40 minutes of brisk walking every day. No more excuses.

---

**To-Do:**
- [ ] Talk to Meifang tomorrow; see if I can persuade her to go for a follow-up. Her meds likely need adjusting.
- [ ] Adjust my own dinner portions: reduce rice, increase vegetable ratio.
- [ ] Create a September exercise schedule, post it in the workspace, and check it off daily.
- [ ] Focus on blood glucose and lipid markers during next week's physical.
- [ ] Look up the latest hypertension management guidelines to see if there are new treatment protocols.

---

**Reflections:**

Despite controlling things for so long, the data is still trending in the wrong direction. What does this mean? It means that the things I can actually control are quite limited. I can record, I can adjust, and I can persist, but the body still follows its own rhythm.

Perhaps my attempt to use data to defeat aging was a flawed premise from the start.

But I have to keep doing this. Because if I give up on even this, then there really is no way out.

ranked_items.content:
# September 5, 2022, 8:15 PM

I deleted that spreadsheet today.

It took me a long time to finally hit the delete key. My finger hovered over the mouse for nearly five minutes. That spreadsheet held three months of Meifang’s blood pressure readings, diet logs, exercise durations... I had color-coded everything: green for 'target met,' yellow for 'needs improvement,' and red for 'danger.'

When I finally clicked it, I realized I was sweating.

I said those words to Meifang tonight. She was in the kitchen, and I stood in the doorway and said, "My over-supervision isn't protecting you; it's hurting you." She turned around and looked at me with something in her eyes I can't quite describe. She didn't say anything, she just nodded.

We were silent for a long time. Then she said, "We are living together, not just surviving in fear every day."

When I heard that, I felt something in my chest loosen.

Afterward, I sat in my workshop for two hours. I didn't organize my tools; I didn't do anything at all. I just sat there. I looked at those precision parts and instruments, wondering where they would eventually end up. Maybe I really should donate them to a school. Maybe that’s the right thing to do.

I’ve made a new plan. Not one of those extreme ones filled with numbers and color codes, but something... more like a plan for living:

Exercise three times a week—not for the data, but for the feeling of being alive.
Check medical data once a month, not every week.
Eat healthily, but not to extremes.
See a therapist next month. I need to deal with the reality of aging. The reality of death.

I can't figure these things out on my own.

The medical report is still on the desk. I haven't looked at it again. Maybe I’ll look tomorrow, maybe the day after. It’s okay not to look right now.

Back to work tomorrow. Back to chess next week. Life goes on.

But it feels different now.

ranked_items.content:
# Monday, July 18, 2022, 14:30

It’s been two hours since I got the report.

I’m sitting in my office with the report spread out on the desk. I told everyone "it’s nothing serious," but my fingers keep tracing those marked spots—blood pressure 139/92, fasting blood glucose 6.8. The numbers themselves don’t lie. I know that better than anyone.

I heard everything the doctor said, but I chose to remember only the first half of his sentences. "It’s under decent control." The rest—the parts about "needing to be more cautious" and "regular follow-ups"—I just let slide. That’s not my style.

Meifang was cooking when I got home. She saw the look on my face and asked about it. I didn't give her a straight answer, just said "I'm fine." But I know she can tell. After 35 years, she knows me too well. She tried to come closer, wanting me to talk, but I turned away and went into the study.

Then I suddenly turned back around and said to her, "Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We can't let your blood pressure issues drag on anymore. I’m going to set up a plan for you."

She was startled by my tone. I saw it. But I didn't stop. I need to control something. I need to ensure that something is within my grasp.

She isn't the problem. I know that. But at this moment, I can't face the fact that *I* am the problem.

Data doesn't lie. I’ve lived my whole life by data and standards, and now the data is starting to betray me. Blood pressure, blood sugar, weight—every indicator is slowly crossing the line. I can see the trend. I can predict it. And that is exactly what’s so terrifying.

The two-year retirement plan. I spoke of it with such certainty. But now I’m starting to wonder: if my body continues like this, how many "two years" do I have left?

The tools in the workshop are still there. I wanted to go and wipe them down, as I usually do. But today, I didn't move. I sat in my office, staring at this report, trying to use logic to convince myself that this is all within manageable limits.

But I know that some things are forever beyond my control.

For the first time, I truly felt that.

ranked_items.content:
In the workshop, Chen Guilin sat on a simple wooden stool, a notebook open before him. On the tool rack, the precision instruments glinted coldly under the fluorescent lights. His phone screen lit up—a WeChat message from his wife, Li Meifang: "What do you want for dinner?"

He didn't reply immediately. Instead, he stared at the dense notes in his notebook and the words "Who would use this?" circled in red ink.

Five minutes later, he finally typed:

"Do you think... there's any point in me keeping these records?"

After sending it, he added another line:

"Just thinking about what happens after I retire. These tools will just be sitting there anyway."

He paused for a long time, stopping himself from typing more. His fingers hovered over the keyboard before he finally lowered them.

Then he added one last message:

"I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately. Not the work kind of tired."

---

*Chen Guilin put down his phone and turned back to wiping a precision vernier caliper on the rack. His movements were slow and meticulous, but his eyes were no longer on the tool.*

ranked_items.content:
# Studio Notes

Sunday, August 28, 2022, Morning

I didn't sleep well last night. I couldn't stop thinking about that phone call.

The school director said they are very interested. Said the project is feasible. Said their students need something like this. I had to replay it twice in my head to make sure I hadn't misheard.

I spent the whole night scribbling nonsense in my notebook—all about how tools get lost and knowledge dissipates. The handwriting was terrible, like the scrawlings of a madman. Looking over it this morning, it felt foreign even to me. That didn't look like my work.

But what I came up with yesterday is different.

I thought: what if I organized these tools and this experience into something systematic? Not for Chen Hao or Chen Yu—they each have their own paths—but for those who truly need it. For young people who are interested in precision engineering, just as my father influenced me back then.

the moment that thought surfaced, my hands stopped shaking.

I spent an hour reorganizing yesterday's chaotic notes. Titles, modules, schedules. My handwriting became much clearer. My fingers remembered what to do. It felt just like drafting a new process improvement plan in the studio.

"Precision Engineering Heritage Project"—that’s the name I gave it. It consists of three parts: tool donations, craft lectures, and apprenticeship training. The costs are controllable, and the outputs are clear. The school said they would cooperate.

My wife asked me this morning why I hadn't slept. I didn't explain much. I just said I was thinking about something new. She nodded and went back to her breakfast. She’s used to me being like this.

The medical report is still in the drawer. The cardiologist's words are still ringing in my ears. But this morning, I didn't keep obsessing over those numbers. I was doing something else.

Perhaps that’s the difference. I cannot change the fact that my body is aging. But I can decide to leave something behind before—or even while—it happens. Not to prove that I lived, but because... some things are worth passing on.

I look at the tools in the studio. Dozens of them, some bought over twenty years ago. They won't lose their value just because I die. They will keep working in someone else's hands.

This thought brings me a feeling I haven't felt in a long time.

It isn't despair.

I need to send the detailed proposal to the school next Monday. I also need to organize the first donation list.

The pen is still in my hand.

Date: March 28, 2022, 21:45

---

**To-Do: Conversation with Chen Hao**

Status: Incomplete / Needs Rescheduling

I called Chen Hao tonight. I originally intended to ask if he had any interest in mechanical engineering and if he’d like to learn some basic craftsmanship. But when the words were on the tip of my tongue, I held back. Instead, I just asked how work was going. He said the mortgage pressure has been heavy lately and the market is unstable.

I didn't push further after hearing that.

Later, in the workshop, I replayed the conversation in my head. The questions are—

1. Is he truly uninterested, or is it that I’ve never properly talked to him about these things?
2. If I start teaching him now, is it too late? Look at how old he is already.
3. How can I bring it up next time without it feeling abrupt?

The items in the tool cabinet have been sitting there for so many years. Some were left by my father; others I collected myself. They are still high-precision instruments, wiped and maintained regularly. But if no one uses them, maintenance is pointless.

ranked_items.content:
It was just after 5:00 AM. Chen Guilin had been sitting in the bedroom for a long time. Outside the window, the sky was still dark, save for the occasional sound of a passing car below. He hadn't slept—or rather, he had given up on trying.

Beside him, Li Meifang rolled over. He could hear her breathing, very faint.

He opened the notes app on his phone. Inside were the figures he’d jotted down yesterday: 110, 140/90, pre-metabolic syndrome, long-term medication. His finger hovered over the screen for a long time before he finally deleted the line: "How much longer can I work?"

He put the phone down, then picked it up again.

Finally, he sent Li Meifang a message on WeChat. She wouldn't be awake yet, but he needed to get these words out.

---

**Chen Guilin**: I’ve been thinking all night about what the doctor said yesterday. It’s not a small thing.

**Chen Guilin**: Long-term medication—he was very clear about that. It means... we might have to change our plans.

**Chen Guilin**: I’m thinking about the retirement timeline. Two years might be too tight. Or rather, I need to run the numbers again.

**Chen Guilin**: And the insurance. Our current coverage might not be enough. I’m going to look into it today.

**Chen Guilin**: The other day you said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." When did you start worrying?

**Chen Guilin**: ...Never mind. We’ll talk in the morning. I just keep thinking—all these years, I thought I was managing things well. Turns out...

**Chen Guilin**: Keep sleeping. I’m fine. Just thinking through some of the accounts.

---

He put the phone down and sat for a while longer. The sky was gradually turning gray.

He got up, walked to the living room, and switched on the light in his workshop. His tools were arranged neatly on the shelves. He picked up a pair of vernier calipers and began to wipe them with a soft cloth. His movements were slow and heavy.

He wiped them once, then wiped them again.

I spent two hours longer in the office today than expected. The 3:00 PM quality control meeting was pushed back to 5:00 PM, which threw off my planned workflow audit. However, I did manage to finish the third-quarter defect rate statistics; the figures are 0.3 percentage points better than last month.

Back in the workshop tonight, I inspected that new set of precision measuring tools from Japan. They are in excellent condition, with all accuracy levels within standard ranges. Sitting in the workshop, a question suddenly crossed my mind—all these things I’ve accumulated over the years, these experiences and methods, is anyone actually using them? The younger people in the department seem to prefer blindly following the software system’s suggestions rather than understanding the principles behind them. How many people have actually read the process documentation I wrote?

I’m retiring in two years. I’ve thought about this plan for a long time and felt it was the right move. But today, I’m suddenly feeling a bit uncertain—how much work is still left unfinished? The things I wanted to pass on to others haven't truly been handed over yet.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue pushing the new inspection process improvement plan. I’ve been brewing this proposal for three months, and the data model is essentially finalized. If it gets approved, it should reduce the defect rate by another 2% or so.

I also need to remind my subordinates that the end-of-month equipment maintenance cannot be delayed again. Some people always want to procrastinate as much as possible, but it’s exactly in that kind of slackness where the details go wrong.

ranked_items.content:
# Memo

**Date**: Wednesday, August 24, 2022, 14:00

**Subject**: Reflections following a game of chess

---

## Record

During today’s game with Player A, he said something that stuck with me: "Instead of spending time fearing the future, spend it cherishing the present."

I didn't respond at the time, but I haven't stopped thinking about it since.

## Issues to Sort Out

1. **What am I doing?**
   - My recent anxiety cycle: Prediction → Fear → Control → More Fear.
   - Since the physical exam results came back, I’ve been using data, plans, and logs to fight uncertainty.
   - But is this actually helpful? Or am I just wearing myself out?

2. **On Control**
   - What I can control: Workflows, financial accounts, daily schedules.
   - What I cannot control: Aging, fluctuating health metrics, my children’s choices, time itself.
   - I have been trying to fill the void of uncontrollable fears with things I can control.
   - What is the cost of doing this?

3. **On the Present**
   - For the past few months, I’ve been planning for a retirement that is two years away.
   - At the same time, I’m worrying about potential health issues.
   - The "present" I hold in my hands has been squeezed into something tiny.
   - My wife, the chess games, the tools in my workshop—they are all still here, but mentally, I’ve already let go of them.

4. **Defining Value**
   - I’ve always believed: Completion → Results → Proof of self-worth.
   - But what if things can’t be completed? What if the results are forgotten?
   - Perhaps value is more than just these things?

## For Further Reflection

- Why do I keep losing what I have now by worrying about what I might lose in the future?
- Is accepting the unchangeable an act of giving up, or an act of maturity?
- How much more time can I afford to waste on fear?

---

**Note**: When I play Player A again next week, I want to hear his take on "cherishing the present." Not as small talk, but because I truly want to understand.

ranked_items.content:
July 22, 2022, 9:15 AM

I haven’t been able to focus all morning.

Just now in the office, because of a formatting issue in a quality inspection report—the header was off by a mere half-centimeter—I tore into Xiao Wang in front of the entire department. I said some terrible things, things like "I wonder if all these years of management have meant anything at all." His face went white after I finished, and he took the afternoon off.

Now, sitting in my office, I’m wondering: why did I do that? It was just a formatting error. He’s a diligent employee; a small thing like that could have easily been corrected in private. But I didn’t. I said those things in public.

I know what I’m doing. I’m using severity to prove I’m still relevant. Because I’m afraid—afraid that I’m becoming inconsequential. Forty years. I’ve been in this position for forty years. I’ve cut defect rates by 25%, improved processes time and again, but has any of it truly changed anything? Or have I just been repeating the same motions, over and over, just to prove I exist?

The recent medical checkup has made me even more uneasy. Those numbers, the fluctuations in those indicators—I look at them every day. I record them. I control them. But I can’t control anything. I can’t control time, I can’t control my body, and I can’t control aging. So I’ve transferred this sense of powerlessness onto my work, onto Xiao Wang.

My wife asked me last night why I was so quiet. I didn’t answer. How could I explain? I’m afraid. I’m afraid that in two years, when I retire and this department is gone, when these data points and standards are gone, I really will be nothing.

Xiao Wang is probably at home right now because of something I said. He might be wondering if he should even keep working here. I caused this.

I need to talk to him tomorrow. But I don’t know what to say. Apologize? That’s a hard word for me to say. Explain? Explain what? My anxiety? My fear?

The health log is still sitting on my desk. I ran ten minutes longer than usual today, thinking that maybe this way I could keep things under control. But halfway through the run, I suddenly realized it was all futile. I’m using running to fight aging, severity to fight powerlessness, and data to fight death.

It’s absurd.

But I don’t know what else to do.

ranked_items.content:
# June 28, 2022, 10:15 PM

The light in the workshop is still on. Li Meifang has already gone to bed. Her blood pressure has been a bit high lately, and she’s still not really drinking the herbal tea I brewed for her. Forget it, I’ll deal with it tomorrow.

I just flipped through my notebook again. I’ve been documenting the tools since last year, and there are over thirty entries now. For each one, I’ve noted its purpose, specifications, and date of purchase. Some of the handwriting has started to fade, so I traced over it again in pencil.

A question suddenly crossed my mind—if my body starts to fail, what will I have left to do?

This question has been circling in my head for a long time. The May physical exam report is still in the drawer; I haven’t looked at it a second time. I remember all the metrics and indicators the doctor mentioned, but I chose not to dwell on them. And now? I still don't want to think too deeply about it, but it seems I can’t avoid it anymore.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, preoccupied with his mortgage; my daughter is teaching in Guangzhou. I’ve never asked them if they’re interested in learning any of this. Perhaps they lost interest long ago. Perhaps I shouldn't have expected anything from the start.

I added another question mark under the one already in the notebook. Then, I placed the notebook in the most prominent spot on the workbench. I kept staring at it in the darkness. It’s a bit of a foolish gesture, but I just wanted to look at it. I wanted to see if those questions sitting there meant they truly existed, and truly needed to be resolved.

*While I can still move, I have to hurry.* This phrase keeps popping up now.

I proposed four more process improvement plans at work this year, and all of them were adopted. My subordinates are still afraid of me. A new QC inspector made three mistakes last week that I pointed out in front of everyone; now he’s all submissive whenever he sees me. I know it’s not right, but I can’t help myself. Maybe I’m using this as a way to fight back against something—against time, against powerlessness, against that growing realization: I am losing control over everything.

Li Meifang says I’ve become even quieter lately. She’s right. What else is there to say?

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still seven or eight left to write down.

Must hurry.

74c45951-7ec5-47d2-b03e-6c3d18e2c495.question: Old Chen, I keep thinking back to when we were in the exam room, and how you specifically turned back at the last second to press the doctor on whether that diagnosis was "certain." If you hadn't asked that question and had just accepted the result instead, do you think you'd still be sitting there, going over that prescription slip again and again?

ranked_items.content:
3:30 AM. Awake again.

The photo of that prescription is still glowing on my phone. I don’t know why I keep looking at it over and over. The numbers are the same every time: blood pressure 140/110, fasting blood glucose 110 mg/dL, LDL-C 3.8 mmol/L, carotid intima-media thickness... The doctor used the term "pre-metabolic syndrome." And "long-term medication."

Long-term.

I shone a flashlight on the paper prescription, reading it word by word. The doctor’s handwriting is clear. I didn't misread it. I copied the data into my notebook again, just like I would verify a test report at work. Confirmed. It’s ironic.

How much longer can I keep working?

My hand was shaking as I wrote that sentence. Then I crossed it out. I wrote it again. Crossed it out again. In the end, I just sat there, staring at the blackened ink.

Forty years. From the day I entered the factory in 1982 until now, every day has gone according to plan. Every process precise to the millimeter. The defect rate in the department I manage is 25% lower than the industry average. I have never wasted a single minute. Regular diet, regular sleep, annual check-ups. I thought that was enough. I thought I was in control.

But the doctor said it wasn't enough.

Meifang’s words keep circling in my head: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." She said it so calmly, like she was stating a fact she’d known all along. I didn’t argue. Because I realized I couldn't find the words to talk back. She saw right through me. She saw through forty years of self-deception.

I don’t believe this result. But I can’t deny it either.

There are four medications on the prescription. I haven't filled them yet. I sit in my office, looking at those precision tools, imagining myself taking these pills every day, my body slowly...

How many years are left?

I tried to draft a plan in my notebook. Retirement date, financial arrangements, things for the children. But I couldn't keep writing. Because suddenly, I don't know what the deadline for these plans is anymore.

Dawn is breaking. I should get up. But I don’t want to move.

I think I need to recalculate everything. But right now, I don’t have the strength.

— Chen Guilin
September 11, 2022, 3:30 AM

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 4:45 PM

Just got back from the hospital. My hands are still shaking.

I wrote down everything the doctor said. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, systolic 160, diastolic 100—Stage 2 hypertension. And then that phrase: pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. I asked him if he was sure; he said he was. Then he wrote out a stack of prescriptions and said I’d need long-term medication.

Long-term medication.

I kept poking at those three words on the prescription slip for the longest time. The tip of my pen nearly tore through the paper. The doctor probably noticed, but he didn't say anything.

Forty years. I’ve worked at this plant for forty years. Up at 6:30 AM, in bed by 10:00 PM. Never missed a day in my work log. Portioned meals, regular exercise, timely check-ups. I thought that would be enough. I thought that through precise management—by keeping everything under my thumb—my body wouldn't betray me.

Now the doctor is telling me it wasn't enough.

I always felt I was different from the others. Everyone else is so careless, while I am precise down to the minute. I thought that was an advantage. And now? Now I realize there are far more things I can’t control than things I can. The things happening inside my body—I can’t see them, I can’t touch them, and I can’t change them.

My wife said, "I knew you'd end up with this sooner or later." Her words made me angry, but I didn't argue back. Because I couldn't. She probably saw it coming long ago, while I was still lying to myself.

I need to start the medication. Every day. Probably for a long time. Maybe for the rest of my life.

How much longer is left? I keep wondering. If my body is already like this, how much longer can I work? Can I hold on for two more years until retirement? Or should I leave early? But what does early retirement even mean? What do I have left besides work?

All those tools in the workshop. Collected over forty years. And those process improvement plans. If I collapse, who takes over? Hao and Yu aren't in this industry. They might not even know what any of it is worth.

My head is a mess right now. I should calm down and make a plan. But I find that I can't. Because I don't know what I'm supposed to be planning for.

My hands are still shaking.

I need to put these pills in a fixed spot. Record the dosage every day. I can do that. I must do that.

But as for everything else?

I don't know.

# Office Memo

**April 5, 2022, 2:20 PM**

---

My fingers are tapping the desk again. I can’t stop.

I was too harsh with Wang Gang just now. His suggestion regarding the inspection process was actually fine; why did I have to insist on pointing out his lack of precision? His expression changed—I saw it—but I didn't apologize. I just kept talking about my standards.

Why is it always like this?

During the afternoon meeting, I lowered the third-quarter defect rate target again. From 0.8 to 0.6. No one dared to object, but I saw Li Qiang’s pen tear through the paper while he was taking notes. He’s afraid. Someone else in my department will quit. I know it.

But I can’t control myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. Blood pressure 139/92. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m monitoring Li Meifang’s salt intake, yet I—never mind.

I wiped down the tools in the workshop again today. Screwdrivers, calipers, micrometers. Every single one is recorded in the logbook. Use, specifications, year of purchase. Chen Hao called to say his mortgage went up again; I listened to him finish and then just said, "Cut back on your spending." He didn't say anything else. It occurred to me that he might have wanted my help, but I didn't ask.

What use are these tools, anyway?

Two years until retirement. What exactly am I planning for?

Li Meifang said she wants braised pork for dinner. I’m already thinking about how to convince her to eat something lighter. She’ll listen to me, as she always does. But the way she looks at me—I’ve noticed it—she’s avoiding my eyes.

When did I become this kind of person?

Fingers still tapping. Tap. Tap. Tap.

I need to write this down. While I still can.

---

*Check tomorrow’s quality inspection standards before leaving. Might need to adjust them one more time.*

ranked_items.content:
It was just after 5:00 AM. Chen Guilin had been sitting in the bedroom for a long time. Outside the window, the sky was still dark, save for the occasional sound of a passing car below. He hadn't slept—or rather, he had given up on trying.

Beside him, Li Meifang rolled over. He could hear her breathing, very faint.

He opened the notes app on his phone. Inside were the figures he’d jotted down yesterday: 110, 140/90, pre-metabolic syndrome, long-term medication. His finger hovered over the screen for a long time before he finally deleted the line: "How much longer can I work?"

He put the phone down, then picked it up again.

Finally, he sent Li Meifang a message on WeChat. She wouldn't be awake yet, but he needed to get these words out.

---

**Chen Guilin**: I’ve been thinking all night about what the doctor said yesterday. It’s not a small thing.

**Chen Guilin**: Long-term medication—he was very clear about that. It means... we might have to change our plans.

**Chen Guilin**: I’m thinking about the retirement timeline. Two years might be too tight. Or rather, I need to run the numbers again.

**Chen Guilin**: And the insurance. Our current coverage might not be enough. I’m going to look into it today.

**Chen Guilin**: The other day you said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." When did you start worrying?

**Chen Guilin**: ...Never mind. We’ll talk in the morning. I just keep thinking—all these years, I thought I was managing things well. Turns out...

**Chen Guilin**: Keep sleeping. I’m fine. Just thinking through some of the accounts.

---

He put the phone down and sat for a while longer. The sky was gradually turning gray.

He got up, walked to the living room, and switched on the light in his workshop. His tools were arranged neatly on the shelves. He picked up a pair of vernier calipers and began to wipe them with a soft cloth. His movements were slow and heavy.

He wiped them once, then wiped them again.

ranked_items.content:
3:20 AM. The phone screen lit up. Chen Guilin stared at the WeChat chat box. His wife’s message was still there: "Are you okay? Do you want some warm water?"

His fingers hovered over the keyboard for a long time.

Finally, he turned off the screen.

---

Daylight broke.

The light from outside slowly filtered in. Chen Guilin had sat on the sofa all night, and the tightness in his chest had finally eased a bit. He touched his chest and took a few deep breaths. Reason told him it was just anxiety. But reason didn't seem to count for much in the early hours of the morning.

He picked up his phone. His wife’s message was still there. He checked the time—6:47 AM. Li Meifang would be waking up soon.

He started typing, then deleted it. Typed again, and deleted it again.

Finally, he sent:

"Good morning. I'm fine, just stayed up thinking about work and missed your message, sorry. How’s your blood pressure? Remember to eat breakfast."

After hitting send, he put the phone down and walked to the kitchen. There was some porridge left over from yesterday in the fridge. He scooped out a bowl and set it on the dining table.

Then he sat down and opened the thick health logbook. Turning to yesterday’s page, he noted down: "3:00 AM, chest tightness, shortness of breath, lasted about 20 minutes, then subsided."

His handwriting was neat, but he pressed down so hard that the tip of the pen nearly tore through the paper.

He looked at the line of text and added one more sentence: "Likely an anxiety response."

Then he closed the book and waited for his wife to wake up.

# Monday, July 18, 2022, 14:30

It’s been two hours since I got the report.

I’m sitting in my office with the report spread out on the desk. I told everyone "it’s nothing serious," but my fingers keep tracing those marked spots—blood pressure 139/92, fasting blood glucose 6.8. The numbers themselves don’t lie. I know that better than anyone.

I heard everything the doctor said, but I chose to remember only the first half of his sentences. "It’s under decent control." The rest—the parts about "needing to be more cautious" and "regular follow-ups"—I just let slide. That’s not my style.

Meifang was cooking when I got home. She saw the look on my face and asked about it. I didn't give her a straight answer, just said "I'm fine." But I know she can tell. After 35 years, she knows me too well. She tried to come closer, wanting me to talk, but I turned away and went into the study.

Then I suddenly turned back around and said to her, "Starting today, you need to cooperate too. We can't let your blood pressure issues drag on anymore. I’m going to set up a plan for you."

She was startled by my tone. I saw it. But I didn't stop. I need to control something. I need to ensure that something is within my grasp.

She isn't the problem. I know that. But at this moment, I can't face the fact that *I* am the problem.

Data doesn't lie. I’ve lived my whole life by data and standards, and now the data is starting to betray me. Blood pressure, blood sugar, weight—every indicator is slowly crossing the line. I can see the trend. I can predict it. And that is exactly what’s so terrifying.

The two-year retirement plan. I spoke of it with such certainty. But now I’m starting to wonder: if my body continues like this, how many "two years" do I have left?

The tools in the workshop are still there. I wanted to go and wipe them down, as I usually do. But today, I didn't move. I sat in my office, staring at this report, trying to use logic to convince myself that this is all within manageable limits.

But I know that some things are forever beyond my control.

For the first time, I truly felt that.

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 9:45 PM

I’ve been in the workshop for nearly three hours. Li Meifang knocked twice; I didn’t answer.

The morning’s medical report is still spinning in my head. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, Stage 2 hypertension, and that phrase—"pre-metabolic syndrome." The doctor spoke calmly, as if reading a production report, but I caught the "you should be worried" undertone. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. Long-term medication.

I’m sitting at the workbench, holding a pair of pliers, wiping them over and over with a piece of flannel. How many times have I wiped them? I’ve lost count. Usually, I follow a system—precision measuring tools in one group, cutting tools in another, fasteners in a third. Not today. I just pick up whatever is at hand, wipe it, put it back. Pick up another. My movements are slow because my hands are shaking slightly.

I don’t want to admit that.

Forty years. For forty years, I’ve been in control—schedules, diet logs, regular check-ups. My desk is organized without a single stray wire. The household ledgers are accurate to the penny. I thought that was enough. I thought that with enough precision, enough discipline, I could keep everything in my grasp.

But the doctor said it’s not enough.

That sentence keeps ringing in my ears—the one Li Meifang said: "I knew you’d get this sooner or later." When did she know? How did she know? Was she pitying me, or was she saying, "I saw through you long ago"? I don’t want to ask, because I’m afraid of the answer.

110 mg/dL. That number is wrong. Something has gone wrong with the management of my life, but I can’t find where. Or rather, I can’t find a solution at all. Pills? Long-term medication? What does that mean? It means my body has begun to betray me, and I cannot reverse it through stricter management.

How much longer?

The question has suddenly become urgent. How much longer can I work? How much longer until I see Chen Hao’s mortgage paid off? How much longer until I pass these tools, these forty years of things, on to someone? I never gave it serious thought because I assumed there was plenty of time.

Now, I’m not sure.

The tools are still here, piece by piece. My hands are still shaking.

I have to go to work tomorrow, read production reports, point out mistakes to my subordinates. Everything must continue. But I don’t know how much longer I can keep going.

I need to look at that report again. Maybe I missed something. Maybe the numbers will change if I scrutinize them enough.

But I know they won’t.

The photo of that prescription is still glowing on my phone. I don’t know why I keep looking at it over and over. The numbers are the same every time: blood pressure 140/110, fasting blood glucose 110 mg/dL, LDL-C 3.8 mmol/L, carotid intima-media thickness... The doctor used the term "pre-metabolic syndrome." And "long-term medication."

Long-term.

I shone a flashlight on the paper prescription, reading it word by word. The doctor’s handwriting is clear. I didn't misread it. I copied the data into my notebook again, just like I would verify a test report at work. Confirmed. It’s ironic.

How much longer can I keep working?

My hand was shaking as I wrote that sentence. Then I crossed it out. I wrote it again. Crossed it out again. In the end, I just sat there, staring at the blackened ink.

Forty years. From the day I entered the factory in 1982 until now, every day has gone according to plan. Every process precise to the millimeter. The defect rate in the department I manage is 25% lower than the industry average. I have never wasted a single minute. Regular diet, regular sleep, annual check-ups. I thought that was enough. I thought I was in control.

But the doctor said it wasn't enough.

Meifang’s words keep circling in my head: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." She said it so calmly, like she was stating a fact she’d known all along. I didn’t argue. Because I realized I couldn't find the words to talk back. She saw right through me. She saw through forty years of self-deception.

I don’t believe this result. But I can’t deny it either.

There are four medications on the prescription. I haven't filled them yet. I sit in my office, looking at those precision tools, imagining myself taking these pills every day, my body slowly...

How many years are left?

I tried to draft a plan in my notebook. Retirement date, financial arrangements, things for the children. But I couldn't keep writing. Because suddenly, I don't know what the deadline for these plans is anymore.

Dawn is breaking. I should get up. But I don’t want to move.

I think I need to recalculate everything. But right now, I don’t have the strength.

— Chen Guilin
September 11, 2022, 3:30 AM
ranked_items.subquestion: Old Chen, I heard that after your hospital visit, you sat in your car for a long time staring at that prescription—especially those words, "long-term medication." What was going through your mind back then?

ranked_items.content:
It was just after 5:00 AM. Chen Guilin had been sitting in the bedroom for a long time. Outside the window, the sky was still dark, save for the occasional sound of a passing car below. He hadn't slept—or rather, he had given up on trying.

Beside him, Li Meifang rolled over. He could hear her breathing, very faint.

He opened the notes app on his phone. Inside were the figures he’d jotted down yesterday: 110, 140/90, pre-metabolic syndrome, long-term medication. His finger hovered over the screen for a long time before he finally deleted the line: "How much longer can I work?"

He put the phone down, then picked it up again.

Finally, he sent Li Meifang a message on WeChat. She wouldn't be awake yet, but he needed to get these words out.

---

**Chen Guilin**: I’ve been thinking all night about what the doctor said yesterday. It’s not a small thing.

**Chen Guilin**: Long-term medication—he was very clear about that. It means... we might have to change our plans.

**Chen Guilin**: I’m thinking about the retirement timeline. Two years might be too tight. Or rather, I need to run the numbers again.

**Chen Guilin**: And the insurance. Our current coverage might not be enough. I’m going to look into it today.

**Chen Guilin**: The other day you said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." When did you start worrying?

**Chen Guilin**: ...Never mind. We’ll talk in the morning. I just keep thinking—all these years, I thought I was managing things well. Turns out...

**Chen Guilin**: Keep sleeping. I’m fine. Just thinking through some of the accounts.

---

He put the phone down and sat for a while longer. The sky was gradually turning gray.

He got up, walked to the living room, and switched on the light in his workshop. His tools were arranged neatly on the shelves. He picked up a pair of vernier calipers and began to wipe them with a soft cloth. His movements were slow and heavy.

He wiped them once, then wiped them again.
ranked_items.subquestion: Old Chen, I heard that after your hospital visit, you sat in your car for a long time staring at that prescription—especially those words, "long-term medication." What was going through your mind back then?

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 4:45 PM

Just got back from the hospital. My hands are still shaking.

I wrote down everything the doctor said. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, systolic 160, diastolic 100—Stage 2 hypertension. And then that phrase: pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. I asked him if he was sure; he said he was. Then he wrote out a stack of prescriptions and said I’d need long-term medication.

Long-term medication.

I kept poking at those three words on the prescription slip for the longest time. The tip of my pen nearly tore through the paper. The doctor probably noticed, but he didn't say anything.

Forty years. I’ve worked at this plant for forty years. Up at 6:30 AM, in bed by 10:00 PM. Never missed a day in my work log. Portioned meals, regular exercise, timely check-ups. I thought that would be enough. I thought that through precise management—by keeping everything under my thumb—my body wouldn't betray me.

Now the doctor is telling me it wasn't enough.

I always felt I was different from the others. Everyone else is so careless, while I am precise down to the minute. I thought that was an advantage. And now? Now I realize there are far more things I can’t control than things I can. The things happening inside my body—I can’t see them, I can’t touch them, and I can’t change them.

My wife said, "I knew you'd end up with this sooner or later." Her words made me angry, but I didn't argue back. Because I couldn't. She probably saw it coming long ago, while I was still lying to myself.

I need to start the medication. Every day. Probably for a long time. Maybe for the rest of my life.

How much longer is left? I keep wondering. If my body is already like this, how much longer can I work? Can I hold on for two more years until retirement? Or should I leave early? But what does early retirement even mean? What do I have left besides work?

All those tools in the workshop. Collected over forty years. And those process improvement plans. If I collapse, who takes over? Hao and Yu aren't in this industry. They might not even know what any of it is worth.

My head is a mess right now. I should calm down and make a plan. But I find that I can't. Because I don't know what I'm supposed to be planning for.

My hands are still shaking.

I need to put these pills in a fixed spot. Record the dosage every day. I can do that. I must do that.

But as for everything else?

I don't know.
