ranked_items.content:
# September 10, 2022, 17:20

I’ve been sitting in the car for nearly twenty minutes. The engine is still running; the AC is blowing. I’ve read the prescription in my hand three times now.

110 mg/dL. Stage 2 hypertension. Pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis.

"Long-term medication."

Three words. When the doctor pointed his pen at those words, what did I hear? I didn't hear anything clearly. I just watched his lips moving, and then I looked at those three words. Long-term. Medication.

Forty years. I’ve worked at this plant for forty years. Up at 6:30 every morning, at the plant by 7:00, checking reports, inspecting the workshop, organizing the work logs at night. I’ve never been late once. I eat regularly, get an annual physical, and keep my weight within the standard range. I can manage every data point down to the gram.

But I can’t manage this.

The dashboard shows 17:20. There’s still an hour of work time left. I should go back to the office. There are quality inspection reports to sign, and the afternoon’s process improvement plan isn't finished yet. But I’m sitting here, and my hands are shaking. I can see them shaking.

My wife said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." When did she know? How did she know? I always thought I had everything under control.

The doctor said I need to take pills. One for blood pressure, one statin. Regular follow-ups. "If we control it well at this stage, we can delay the progression"—delay. Not cure. Not recover. Delay.

I think of my father. He was a technician, a very precise man. When he was sixty-five, he said something I’ve always remembered: "When a machine reaches a certain age, things start to go wrong. No matter how well you maintain it, you can only extend its life; you can't change the fact that it's aging."

I didn't listen back then.

Now I understand.

How much longer? The doctor didn't say. He said, "Regular follow-ups, take your medicine on time, change your lifestyle." Change what? My lifestyle is already as disciplined as it gets. Is that still not enough?

Long-term medication. What does that mean? It means I have to sign off on this prescription, go to the pharmacy, and then take pills every day. Every single day. Until when?

I was planning to work for two more years. Retire after that. I’ve done the math; the pension plus investment returns would be enough for my wife and me. I also wanted to organize my tools, maybe donate them to a technical school. And all that experience in process improvement, I wanted to...

But now the doctor is telling me my body is failing. I can’t use precise calculations to stop this from happening.

The prescription is crumpled in my hand. I need to go get the medicine.

Fifty minutes left. I should be able to make it back to sign that report.

But right now, I can't even sit up.

ranked_items.subquestion: Old Chen, I heard that after your hospital visit, you sat in your car for a long time staring at that prescription—especially those words, "long-term medication." What was going through your mind back then?

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 8:15 PM

I’ve been sitting in the workshop for two hours. I’m wiping down that 1985 vernier caliper, my movements slow. My wife is watching TV in the living room; I can hear the sound, but I don’t want to go out.

110 mg/dL. Stage 2 hypertension. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis.

The doctor spoke those words calmly, as if reading a product inspection report. But I heard them clearly. Every word felt like a nail being driven in. I’ve always done my checkups, always logged the data, always believed I was in control. Wake up at 6:00 AM, sleep at 10:00 PM, a light diet, regular exercise. For forty years, that’s how I’ve lived.

My wife said, "I knew you’d get this sooner or later."

That sentence stung more than the diagnosis itself. When did she see through me? I thought I’d hidden it well. I thought that through precise management, I could stop aging, stop my body from betraying me. But she knew all along. What does that mean? Does it mean my efforts look like a joke to everyone else?

Long-term medication. The doctor said I need long-term medication.

I don’t know how long "long-term" is. One year? Five? Ten? Until I die? I’ve never taken medicine long-term. I rarely even catch a cold. Now I have to take pills for the rest of my life just to maintain a number—110. The number itself is meaningless, but what it represents matters. It represents my loss of control over my own body.

My wife said she’d go with me to get the prescription filled tomorrow. I didn’t respond, just nodded, and turned to walk into this workshop. I didn’t want her to see my hands shaking.

The tools are still here. Dozens of them. I spent forty years collecting them. Each one represents a stage, a decision, a moment I thought was important. Now I’m wondering, if I collapse, what happens to these things? Chen Hao doesn't understand mechanics. Neither does Chen Yu. Will they be treated as scrap metal?

I donated some tools to the industrial technical school. Was that the right decision? Or was it just a disguised way of settling my estate?

I don’t want to think like this. But I can’t help it.

The doctor said I need to change my lifestyle. Reduce stress. Maintain a routine. I’m already doing those things. Does that mean I haven’t done enough? Or does it mean none of it matters?

My hands are still shaking. I put the caliper down.

I have to get the medicine tomorrow. I need to make a list—drug names, usage, side effects, prices, insurance coverage. I need to turn this into data, into something manageable. That’s the only way I’ll feel at peace.

But I know that some things simply cannot be managed.
ranked_items.subquestion: Old Chen, I heard that after your hospital visit, you sat in your car for a long time staring at that prescription—especially those words, "long-term medication." What was going through your mind back then?

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 9:45 PM

I’ve been in the workshop for nearly three hours. Li Meifang knocked twice; I didn’t answer.

The morning’s medical report is still spinning in my head. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, Stage 2 hypertension, and that phrase—"pre-metabolic syndrome." The doctor spoke calmly, as if reading a production report, but I caught the "you should be worried" undertone. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. Long-term medication.

I’m sitting at the workbench, holding a pair of pliers, wiping them over and over with a piece of flannel. How many times have I wiped them? I’ve lost count. Usually, I follow a system—precision measuring tools in one group, cutting tools in another, fasteners in a third. Not today. I just pick up whatever is at hand, wipe it, put it back. Pick up another. My movements are slow because my hands are shaking slightly.

I don’t want to admit that.

Forty years. For forty years, I’ve been in control—schedules, diet logs, regular check-ups. My desk is organized without a single stray wire. The household ledgers are accurate to the penny. I thought that was enough. I thought that with enough precision, enough discipline, I could keep everything in my grasp.

But the doctor said it’s not enough.

That sentence keeps ringing in my ears—the one Li Meifang said: "I knew you’d get this sooner or later." When did she know? How did she know? Was she pitying me, or was she saying, "I saw through you long ago"? I don’t want to ask, because I’m afraid of the answer.

110 mg/dL. That number is wrong. Something has gone wrong with the management of my life, but I can’t find where. Or rather, I can’t find a solution at all. Pills? Long-term medication? What does that mean? It means my body has begun to betray me, and I cannot reverse it through stricter management.

How much longer?

The question has suddenly become urgent. How much longer can I work? How much longer until I see Chen Hao’s mortgage paid off? How much longer until I pass these tools, these forty years of things, on to someone? I never gave it serious thought because I assumed there was plenty of time.

Now, I’m not sure.

The tools are still here, piece by piece. My hands are still shaking.

I have to go to work tomorrow, read production reports, point out mistakes to my subordinates. Everything must continue. But I don’t know how much longer I can keep going.

I need to look at that report again. Maybe I missed something. Maybe the numbers will change if I scrutinize them enough.

But I know they won’t.

ranked_items.content:
# Medical Check-up Record & Follow-up Action List

**Date: September 12, 2022, Evening**

---

## I. Summary of Today’s Results

**Primary Diagnoses:**
- Pre-metabolic syndrome
- Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis
- Blood Pressure: Stage 2 Hypertension (110 mmHg diastolic)
- Blood Sugar: Elevated fasting blood glucose

**Doctor’s Exact Words:**
"Long-term medication is required. You can no longer rely solely on dietary adjustments."

---

## II. Medication Plan

| Medication | Dosage | Frequency | Start Date |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Antihypertensives | TBD | Once daily | 2022.09.13 |
| Statins | TBD | Once daily | 2022.09.13 |

**Note:** Go to the community hospital next Monday to fill the prescriptions. Remember to bring the doctor's orders.

---

## III. Next Check-up Schedule

- **Time:** December 12, 2022 (In three months)
- **Location:** Cardiology Department, same hospital
- **Accompanied by:** Li Meifang (Wife will come along)
- **Tests:** Carotid ultrasound, lipid panel, blood glucose follow-up

**Reminder:** Fast the night before the exam. Book the appointment in advance.

---

## IV. Lifestyle Adjustments

**Diet:**
- Continue monitoring salt intake (<6g per day)
- Reduce greasy foods—need to talk to Meifang; her cooking habits need to change
- Increase the proportion of whole grains

**Exercise:**
- Walk 4-5 times a week, 30 minutes per session (avoid strenuous exercise)
- Continue playing chess, but watch out for prolonged sitting

**Routine:**
- Maintain current regular schedule (doing well so far)
- Avoid overexertion

---

## V. Work-Related Reflections

Discussed subordinate management with Meifang during dinner. She asked why I am so strict with them. It made me realize—

Perhaps I have been managing through "control," just as I try to manage my own body. But it seems there is a flaw in this approach.

**New Thoughts:**
- Subordinates need guidance, not just correction.
- I have 40 years of experience, but I haven't truly taught them.
- Perhaps before I retire, I should do something different—pass down the knowledge rather than just pointing out mistakes.

**Concrete Actions:**
- Change the feedback style during next Monday’s department meeting. Start with what was done right, then move to areas for improvement.
- Consider scheduling one-on-one skill training sessions for younger staff.
- Compile a summary document of my experience in quality management.

---

## VI. Matters to Discuss with My Wife

- [ ] Budget for long-term medication costs
- [ ] Specific plan for dietary adjustments
- [ ] Her blood pressure management (hers is also high; I can't just focus on mine)
- [ ] Donating tools to the technical school—thought about it before but never acted; should speed this up now.

---

## VII. Personal Reflection

The doctor’s words keep playing in my head. What does "long-term medication" mean? It means I can no longer believe that I can fully control my body through precise management.

That is hard to accept. But perhaps that is exactly what I need to accept.

I still have time. Two years until retirement. Maybe I should use these two years to do something meaningful—not for myself, but to leave something behind.

---

**Recorded by:** Chen Guilin  
**Next Update:** September 19, 2022 (Reflections after one week of medication)

ranked_items.content:
September 29, 2022 | Sunny | 7:30 AM

I hesitated for a long time when I picked up the pill.

Standing there by the kitchen sink, clutching that small white tablet, I saw that Li Meifang had already placed a glass of water next to me. She didn’t say anything; she just stood there, and I could feel her watching me. This feeling of being watched is unfamiliar. For all these years, I was the one watching her—making sure she took her blood pressure medication on time, watching her diet, checking her physical exam reports. Now, it’s reversed.

I know what swallowing this pill signifies. It’s not that I didn’t understand the doctor’s diagnosis before, but picking up the pill and actually taking it feel like two different things. One is data; the other is... reality.

I marked the medication time on the calendar. I used that 0.5mm black gel pen; the handwriting is small and neat. I also set three reminders on my phone—7:00 AM, 12:00 PM, and 7:00 PM. That way, I won't miss a dose. I can manage this. Through precise records, through making no mistakes, maybe...

But I’m lying to myself. I know that.

Only after I swallowed it did Li Meifang turn away to start the congee. She didn’t ask how I felt, nor did she say anything encouraging. She just went about her business quietly. For some reason, that made me feel worse. If she had said something, I would have known how to respond. But she said nothing, as if she has already accepted this, while I am still... still what?

I stared at the empty pill bottle for a long time. I’ve read the manual three times now. All the data, the dosage instructions, every possible side effect—I’ve committed them all to memory. This shouldn't be hard. I managed processes at the factory for twelve years; I’ve handled problems far more complex than this. But this is different.

This time, I can’t solve it by improving the process. I can’t reverse it through stricter management standards.

The office clock is about to strike 8:00. I should head out. But I sit here, looking at that marked calendar, thinking about how this single pill represents more than just blood pressure—it represents the fact that time is quietly slipping away.

How many more 7:00 AMs do I have left?

Forget it. Don’t think like that.

I stand up and organize the documents on my desk. Business as usual. Time for work.
ranked_items.subquestion: Guilin, I remember back when your checkup results first started fluctuating, you were constantly searching things online, trying so hard to prove there was nothing seriously wrong. How is it that now, with the confirmed diagnosis and prescription in hand, you’ve become so quiet and started Cooperating with the long-term medication?

ranked_items.content:
September 10, 2022, 4:45 PM

Just got back from the hospital. My hands are still shaking.

I wrote down everything the doctor said. Blood sugar at 110 mg/dL, systolic 160, diastolic 100—Stage 2 hypertension. And then that phrase: pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. I asked him if he was sure; he said he was. Then he wrote out a stack of prescriptions and said I’d need long-term medication.

Long-term medication.

I kept poking at those three words on the prescription slip for the longest time. The tip of my pen nearly tore through the paper. The doctor probably noticed, but he didn't say anything.

Forty years. I’ve worked at this plant for forty years. Up at 6:30 AM, in bed by 10:00 PM. Never missed a day in my work log. Portioned meals, regular exercise, timely check-ups. I thought that would be enough. I thought that through precise management—by keeping everything under my thumb—my body wouldn't betray me.

Now the doctor is telling me it wasn't enough.

I always felt I was different from the others. Everyone else is so careless, while I am precise down to the minute. I thought that was an advantage. And now? Now I realize there are far more things I can’t control than things I can. The things happening inside my body—I can’t see them, I can’t touch them, and I can’t change them.

My wife said, "I knew you'd end up with this sooner or later." Her words made me angry, but I didn't argue back. Because I couldn't. She probably saw it coming long ago, while I was still lying to myself.

I need to start the medication. Every day. Probably for a long time. Maybe for the rest of my life.

How much longer is left? I keep wondering. If my body is already like this, how much longer can I work? Can I hold on for two more years until retirement? Or should I leave early? But what does early retirement even mean? What do I have left besides work?

All those tools in the workshop. Collected over forty years. And those process improvement plans. If I collapse, who takes over? Hao and Yu aren't in this industry. They might not even know what any of it is worth.

My head is a mess right now. I should calm down and make a plan. But I find that I can't. Because I don't know what I'm supposed to be planning for.

My hands are still shaking.

I need to put these pills in a fixed spot. Record the dosage every day. I can do that. I must do that.

But as for everything else?

I don't know.
ranked_items.subquestion: Guilin, I remember back when your checkup results first started fluctuating, you were constantly searching things online, trying so hard to prove there was nothing seriously wrong. How is it that now, with the confirmed diagnosis and prescription in hand, you’ve become so quiet and started Cooperating with the long-term medication?

ranked_items.content:
I’ve been thinking about it, and I felt I should let you know.

The doctor got the full report back. My blood pressure is definitely high, and I’ll need to be on long-term medication. He also mentioned some issues with my carotid artery, so I’ll need regular follow-ups. I’m on daily meds now, and it looks like I’ll be taking them for a long time.

Do you still have that blood pressure monitor? We both need to start checking regularly. Especially you—stop eating such salty food. I’ve been thinking about how to adjust our diet at home; we both need to be careful.

I know what you’re going to say. I’m making adjustments, it just takes time. This isn't something that can be changed overnight. I’ll handle the work stuff, so don’t worry about that.

By the way, have you gone for your follow-up lately? Are you still taking your blood pressure meds on schedule? Don’t let what’s going on with me throw off your routine. We need to manage our health properly so that we can—

Anyway, let’s leave it at that for now. Let’s have a proper talk when you get back tonight.
ranked_items.subquestion: Guilin, I remember back when your checkup results first started fluctuating, you were constantly searching things online, trying so hard to prove there was nothing seriously wrong. How is it that now, with the confirmed diagnosis and prescription in hand, you’ve become so quiet and started Cooperating with the long-term medication?

ranked_items.content:
Tuesday, September 13, 2022. Sunny.

I didn’t sleep well last night. I couldn't stop thinking about those numbers.

110 mg/dL. Stage 2 hypertension. Pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis.

The doctor spoke calmly, as if reading a quality inspection report. I heard him clearly; I took in every word. And yet, it felt like I didn't truly hear it at all. I keep wondering how this is possible. I wake up at 6:30 every morning and go to bed at 11:00 PM. My diet is logged in my phone. I get regular check-ups every month. I have spent forty years maintaining order, maintaining control over everything.

My wife said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." I didn't answer her.

I spent the morning in the workshop, wiping down my tools one by one. The vernier caliper I bought in 1987, the set of Swiss precision screwdrivers, the vise... I wiped every single one clean. My movements were slow. I found myself wondering what would happen to these things if I collapsed.

My eldest son is in Shenzhen, struggling with his mortgage. My daughter is in Guangzhou, and her career isn't stable yet. I haven't told them about the diagnosis. What is there to say? That I need long-term medication? That my body has started to betray me?

I wrote a few things in my notebook. "What these tools have taught me." "Precision work has taught me more than just technique." I didn't keep writing.

I’m wondering if I should do a knowledge-sharing session before I retire. To systematically pass on everything from these forty years to the young people in the department. But I’m not sure how much time I have left. The thought makes me feel a sense of urgency, yet also a sense of powerlessness. I wrote "Sharing Session" on a sticky note, but the date box is still empty. I don’t know when would be appropriate. I don’t even know how much time I have left to be "appropriate."

The doctor prescribed some medicine. I have to take two kinds every day now. As I swallow them, I wonder if this is it—the beginning. The beginning of the decline.

I have always believed that precise management could prevent anything. But the medical report tells me that I cannot control everything.

I cannot control it.

How much longer?

Is it really too late?

879f30bf-ffa5-4e8b-8e4a-e645bf6577e2.question: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
Are you asleep?

I was just in the study tidying up my tools, thinking about what you said. You’re right—I really have changed.

For years, I always felt that being responsible meant keeping everything under control—with you, the kids, and my work. I only realized later that it wasn't responsibility; it was a lack of trust. I didn't trust that you could take care of yourself, or that they could make their own decisions.

It wasn't until that incident with the doctor that I truly understood—there are some things I simply cannot control. Through it all, you were the one who stayed, never once looking down on me.

Thank you for being by my side.

Let’s take things slow, together.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
Haozi, how have you been lately?

I’ve been thinking, and I wanted to share a few words with you. This recent health scare has given me a lot to reflect on. The doctor said I need to stay on medication and slow down. With your mom here by my side, I’ve realized that all these years, I’ve been constantly rushing—rushing to keep everything under control, to make everything perfect.

Regarding your mortgage in Shenzhen, I know I used to pester you with questions, almost like I was auditing your books. Looking back, that wasn't right. You’re an adult now, and you have your own plans. I thought I was "helping" you in my own way, but in reality, I was just putting pressure on you.

I’ve only recently come to understand that maybe life doesn't need to be perfectly controlled. In my forty years of work, I always pursued "zero defects," but life isn't an assembly line. There are things you just can't control, and there’s pressure you have to learn to let go of. Otherwise, you’ll end up like me—waiting until your sixties to regret what you’ve missed out on.

I’m not telling you this so you’ll follow in my footsteps. On the contrary, I hope you can live a more relaxed life than I did. A mortgage is just a mortgage, and a job is just a job; don't keep yourself wound so tight. Taking time for family and getting proper rest—those things matter, too.

Your mom says I’ve become quite talkative lately, ha. Maybe I really am changing.

If you’re free this weekend, give me a call. Let’s have a good chat.

— Dad
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, are you there?

I just stepped out of the meeting room. I handled things a bit differently today. There was an issue with the data from one of my subordinates, and instead of my usual "this is unacceptable," I sat down and went through it with him, asking how he thought we could improve it. He looked a bit surprised. I talked with him privately for a while afterward, too.

I’ve been thinking about what you said. "I knew you’d have this sooner or later"—when did you first notice?

I’ve been wondering lately if maybe I’ve had it wrong all along. Managing work, managing my health, managing everything... it feels like no matter how precise I am, it doesn't really protect me from anything. Have you noticed anything different about me lately?

No need to rush to reply. Just wanted to ask.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I’ve been thinking about what you said.

I’ve been thinking about it ever since we got back from our walk.

You’re right. I’ve been in denial. I’ve used management, data, and schedules to deny it. Just like I do at the factory—as if as long as the process is rigorous enough, everything can be controlled. But the body isn't a machine. I know that.

I monitor your diet and push you to get checkups, but I’m actually just deflecting. Deflecting my own fear. I’ve been this way for years—replacing words with actions, and replacing companionship with control. I’ve done it to you, and I’ve done it to the kids.

I owe you an apology. I owe you so much more than that.

Shall we go for a walk together next week? Not to supervise you, but just... to be with you. The way you’ve always been there for me.

I still have a lot to learn. From you.

— Chen Guilin
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
Wife, I’ve been sitting here in my workspace, thinking.

I really took what you said at dinner to heart. Maybe over these years, I truly have been too... I don’t know how to put it. Too obsessed with those numbers and standards, and in the process, I lost sight of the things that actually matter.

I’m also processing what the doctor said. I know I can’t go on like this. Tomorrow, I’ll go get the prescription as he directed, and I’ll follow your lead regarding my diet. I won’t... argue with you like I used to.

And about work. Perhaps I have been too harsh on my subordinates. You’re right—being serious doesn’t have to mean being cold. I’ll try to see if I can improve.

Thank you for not saying "I told you so" tonight. You saw this coming all along, didn't you?

My head is still a bit of a mess, but I’m slowly figuring things out.

—Chen Guilin
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
October 12, 2022, 7:30 PM

Had a phone call with Chen Hao.

In the past, I’d always ask him if the mortgage was a burden, how the orders were coming along, or if his job was stable. I don’t know what came over me today, but the first thing out of my mouth was, "How have you been lately? Is there anything I can help with?" He seemed taken aback; there was a two-second silence before he answered.

He said work is exhausting, that sales is high-pressure, and sometimes he wonders if he’s doing the right thing. He’s lost. He was very candid, and I could hear the fatigue in his voice.

Before, I would have jumped straight in with advice, telling him exactly what he should do. But this time, I just said, "I understand. I’ve been through a stage like that, too."

Even I was a bit surprised by my own words. In forty years, I’ve never spoken to him like that.

He asked me when that was. I told him that when I was his age, I wasn't sure if I was making the right choices either. Back then, I was a quality inspector at the grassroots level, doing the same repetitive work every day, unable to see a way out. What I didn’t tell him was that I just kept going down that same path ever since, never changing course again.

He didn’t respond immediately, but I could sense something had shifted. It wasn't that superior-subordinate dynamic anymore; it felt more like... two people who had walked the same road.

After hanging up, I sat at my desk and looked at that medical report. The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication and a lifestyle change. I’ve been thinking about how to reverse this outcome through stricter management, just like I do at work—precise control, eliminating variables.

But while talking to Chen Hao, it suddenly occurred to me that maybe some things can’t be controlled. Some paths, once walked, are simply walked.

I need to rethink how I interact with them. Not supervising, not guiding. Maybe just... being there?

The thought makes me a little uneasy. I don't know if I still know how to do it.

More tomorrow.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
July 5, 2022, 6:45 PM

Had a call with Hao. He said the real estate market has been a bit cold lately, but he’s holding on. I asked him if he’d ever thought about learning something about mechanics—maybe it could be a hobby later on. He laughed and said, "Dad, what are you talking about? I don’t have the brain for that." I didn't push it further.

After hanging up, I sat in the workshop for a long time.

Actually, that wasn't what I wanted to ask. What I wanted to ask was—has he ever thought about what will happen to all these tools, all these things I’ve spent forty years learning? But I couldn't get the words out. Just like I never can.

During our call this afternoon, I told him "everything is under control." Thinking back on that phrase now, it feels a bit ridiculous. What does "under control" even mean? What am I controlling? Hao is in Shenzhen worrying about his mortgage, Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou, and here I am, documenting the uses of these tools as if I’m in a race against time. But time isn't racing me—it doesn't care about me at all.

Li Meifang is downstairs watching TV. I can hear her coughing. It’s time to nag her about going to the hospital to check her blood pressure again. These are things I can control. But what’s the use of controlling them?

The tool records in my notebook are halfway finished. With every entry I write, I wonder—who is going to use this? Hao? No. Yu? Even less likely. So why am I writing all this?

Maybe it’s just to keep myself from being idle. To tell myself there’s still something left to do.

The physical exam report from May is still in the drawer. A few of the indicators fluctuated a bit. The doctor said it’s nothing serious, just needs regular follow-ups. But I keep taking it out, looking at those numbers over and over. As if I could find some hidden meaning in them.

I’ve never known how to talk to them. That’s the truth. It’s been that way since they were little. I thought it would get better as they grew up, but it seems to have gotten worse. Now, I even self-censor before asking a simple question, terrified of saying something that might cause awkwardness.

Maybe I should have started teaching them earlier. But back then, I was busy with work. Now that I want to teach, it’s already too late.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue documenting the tools. There are still over thirty items left to record.

I should do it while I can still move.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date: Saturday Morning, October 22, 2022**

Had a phone call with Chen Hao. After hearing about my diagnosis, his first instinct was to ask if I’d been taking my medicine on time and if he should take time off to come back. I didn't expect him to ask that.

Before this, I always felt like there was something standing between us. Our calls were always about work, the house, or whether they were eating well—like going through a checklist. Their answers were always brief. I thought that was just how we were.

But this time was different. When he said, "Dad, you need to take care of yourself," I heard something different in his voice. My own voice choked up a bit; all I could manage was, "Okay, thank you." That’s rare for me.

Li Meifang says the way I’ve shown concern for the kids all these years has been flawed—always supervising, never just being there. I used to disagree with her. But thinking about it now, maybe she’s right.

**A few things I need to figure out:**

1. Have a real talk with Chen Hao and Chen Yu individually before I retire. Not to ask about their careers or lives, but to truly listen to them. Maybe they have things they want to say to me, too.

2. What does "legacy" actually mean? I’ve always wanted to pass down my understanding of work and my obsession with quality to them. But they have their own lives. Maybe I should just record these things for them to reference, rather than expecting them to inherit it all.

3. The collaboration with the technical school—donating tools, doing sharing sessions. I want to do this even more now. Not to prove anything, but because I truly want to leave these things behind.

4. Have a proper talk with Li Meifang next week. She’s been by my side all these years, yet I’ve always been "managing" her. It’s time to change that.

**One more thought:**

Maybe the things I’ve done right over these forty years aren't the process improvements or the defect rate data. Maybe it’s that obsession with detail, that commitment to responsibility, and that care for my family—even if I haven't been great at expressing it. If Chen Hao and Chen Yu can see these things in me, that’s enough.

I don’t need them to become me. I just need them to know that I’ve always been here.

---

*Going to play chess with the guys this afternoon. My game hasn't been great lately; my mind keeps wandering. But maybe that’s okay—I don’t need to win. Just sitting there, being with them, is enough.*
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
Tuesday afternoon, October 18, 2022

I spent the entire afternoon sitting in my small workshop. I didn’t clean any tools or organize my inventory. I just flipped through my notebooks, page by page.

What was I recording back then? Screwdriver models, wrench specifications, the purchase year and maintenance cycle for every single tool. Everything was neat and tidy, precise to a fault. Looking at these records used to give me a sense of peace—as if I had mastered something.

But today, I’m wondering: what did these tools actually teach me?

It wasn't how to use them. That’s too simple. It was... how to approach a task. With seriousness. Without compromise. Knowing that every detail matters. For forty years, the products I made with these tools had a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average. That wasn't a coincidence. It was because I never allowed myself to slack off.

But—

I wrote that word on a new page, then crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again.

But I treated people the same way. My subordinates, my family. I demanded perfection and left no room for error. I thought that was called being responsible. Now, I’m not so sure.

Li Meifang has been humming in the kitchen lately. I haven't heard her hum like that in a long time. She says it’s because I’ve finally "loosened up a bit." I can hear it in her voice—it wasn't a compliment. It was... relief? She feels relieved.

That hurts a little.

I turned to a blank page and drew a timeline with a pencil. From 1982 to now. Forty years. I marked the key years: technician, team leader, department manager. I did well at every stage. Defect rates, process optimizations, the proposals that were adopted. It’s a beautiful report card.

But so what?

Chen Hao is in Shenzhen, under a lot of pressure. I keep track of his mortgage and his performance, but we barely have anything to say to each other. When Chen Yu comes home occasionally, our conversations are like status reports—how’s work, is your marriage stable. I thought I was teaching them about perseverance and quality awareness, but I was never truly there for them.

This is my regret. Perhaps my greatest regret.

Next to the timeline, I wrote: "If I could do it over, what would I do?"

But I know there are no "ifs." I only have the now. And about two years left of work. Maybe in these two years, I should record some things. Not tool lists, not process documents. But... what I was thinking during these forty years. Why I did things the way I did. Young people might need that.

Or rather, I need to do this. To forgive myself.

Meifang is calling me for dinner. I hear her voice drifting up from downstairs—gentle, not rushing me. I close the notebook and stand up.

Maybe I should listen to her more. Really listen, instead of mentally calculating her blood pressure and diet.

Maybe that is what I really need to learn.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, seeing how meticulous you used to be with both work and family, I always felt you were someone who insisted on having everything under control. But looking back at these past few months, it seems like you’ve changed quite a bit. Have you started to realize that some things are simply beyond our control, and found yourself loosening up a little?

ranked_items.content:
September 19, 2022, 10:47 PM

Something strange happened in the office today.

During this afternoon's meeting, Xiao Wang from Quality Control handed in a report with problematic data. Usually, I would have said bluntly, "This is unacceptable. Redo it." But in that moment, I paused. I looked at his nervous expression and a thought suddenly hit me—is he afraid of something, too?

Instead, I said, "Let’s take a look together and see where things went wrong."

There was a moment of silence in the conference room. I could feel everyone looking at me. Xiao Wang’s shoulders relaxed a little.

After the meeting, I wrote in my notebook: "Maybe I should change my approach."

But now, sitting here in my workshop looking at these tools, I’m not sure if this is progress or a retreat.

The doctor’s words are still ringing in my ears: "Long-term medication, regular check-ups." I always thought that as long as I was strict and cautious enough, I could control everything. My work, my health—everything. But clearly, I was wrong. My body doesn't follow my orders. I can’t protect myself through perfect management.

Maybe what my subordinates see isn't my high standards, but my fear.

My wife said, "I knew you’d get this sooner or later." What did she mean by that? Did she see it coming all along? What about everyone else? I thought I had hidden it so well.

I don’t know how to tell the kids. Chen Hao is under so much pressure with his mortgage, and Chen Yu is busy with work in Guangzhou. Telling them would only worry them. But if I say nothing, isn't that a form of deception?

My hands are still shaking. I wiped down my tools, then wiped them again.

Maybe I’ve been using control to mask my vulnerability. Now that the vulnerability is exposed, I don’t know what’s left.

I have to go to the hospital tomorrow to pick up my prescription.

4. Have a proper talk with Li Meifang next week. She’s been by my side all these years, yet I’ve always been "managing" her. It’s time to change that.

Maybe the things I’ve done right over these forty years aren't the process improvements or the defect rate data. Maybe it’s that obsession with detail, that commitment to responsibility, and that care for my family—even if I haven't been great at expressing it. If Chen Hao and Chen Yu can see these things in me, that’s enough.

I don’t need them to become me. I just need them to know that I’ve always been here.

---

*Going to play chess with the guys this afternoon. My game hasn't been great lately; my mind keeps wandering. But maybe that’s okay—I don’t need to win. Just sitting there, being with them, is enough.*
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, I’ve watched you go from refusing to say a word even when Chen Hao asked back at the beginning of the year, to now being able to admit to Meifang that you're terrified of losing her. In these past six months, you’ve moved from "toughing it out" to being willing to "show your vulnerability." How did you manage to have that change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
After returning home that evening, Chen Guilin changed his clothes and sat down at the dining table. Li Meifang was serving the soup.

He ate a few mouthfuls of rice in silence before putting down his chopsticks.

"I might have been too harsh at the department meeting today," he said, his voice low, eyes fixed on his bowl. "The new QC inspector messed up the data entry again, and I called him out in front of everyone. He looked pretty shaken."

Li Meifang looked up at him but didn't respond immediately.

"My temper has been short lately," he paused, tapping his finger on the table. "I can feel it myself. I have high standards for the work, but..." He didn't finish the sentence, opting for a sip of soup instead.

He turned to his wife, his tone tinged with uncertainty. "Do you think I'm being too demanding? I want to get things done right, but it seems like..." He hesitated, "it seems like I always end up making people uncomfortable."

He wasn't looking for comfort; it was more like he was trying to confirm something. There was exhaustion in his eyes, along with a certain guilt he couldn't quite put into words.

"The physical exam is next week," he suddenly changed the subject, though his tone remained heavy. "I need to get to bed early these next few days."

He lowered his head and continued eating, but his movements were much slower than usual.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, I’ve watched you go from refusing to say a word even when Chen Hao asked back at the beginning of the year, to now being able to admit to Meifang that you're terrified of losing her. In these past six months, you’ve moved from "toughing it out" to being willing to "show your vulnerability." How did you manage to have that change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
# Calendar Event

**Date**: Saturday, October 22, 2022  
**Time**: 8:00 PM  
**Title**: Call with Chen Hao — Sharing the Diagnosis  

---

## Event Details

**Location**: Home Study  

**Participants**: Chen Guilin, Chen Hao (via phone)

**Description**:  
After receiving my medical report yesterday, I decided to call Chen Hao tonight. Instead of my usual routine of asking about his mortgage and work progress, I took the initiative and said, "There’s something I want to share with you."

The doctor said there is an issue with my heart and I’ll need long-term medication. I gave him the facts simply—no hiding, no exaggerating. There was a brief silence on his end before he asked, "Is it serious?" I told him, "It’s manageable, but I have to accept the reality."

He said he would make time to come back and see me. Instead of my usual response—"No need to take time off, work comes first"—I simply said, "Okay."

The call lasted about ten minutes. It was brief, but unlike our past conversations, it wasn't just an exchange of information.

---

## Notes

**Keywords**: Acceptance | Vulnerability | Communication | Change  

**Reflection**: This might be the first time I’ve proactively shared my "imperfections" with my child. I used to feel I had to maintain a position of strength in front of them, to prove I was still in control of everything. Now I realize that perhaps this kind of honesty is a different form of strength.

**Follow-up**: Remember to take medication on time. Go to the pharmacy on Monday.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, I’ve watched you go from refusing to say a word even when Chen Hao asked back at the beginning of the year, to now being able to admit to Meifang that you're terrified of losing her. In these past six months, you’ve moved from "toughing it out" to being willing to "show your vulnerability." How did you manage to have that change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
October 12, 2022, 7:30 PM

Had a phone call with Chen Hao.

In the past, I’d always ask him if the mortgage was a burden, how the orders were coming along, or if his job was stable. I don’t know what came over me today, but the first thing out of my mouth was, "How have you been lately? Is there anything I can help with?" He seemed taken aback; there was a two-second silence before he answered.

He said work is exhausting, that sales is high-pressure, and sometimes he wonders if he’s doing the right thing. He’s lost. He was very candid, and I could hear the fatigue in his voice.

Before, I would have jumped straight in with advice, telling him exactly what he should do. But this time, I just said, "I understand. I’ve been through a stage like that, too."

Even I was a bit surprised by my own words. In forty years, I’ve never spoken to him like that.

He asked me when that was. I told him that when I was his age, I wasn't sure if I was making the right choices either. Back then, I was a quality inspector at the grassroots level, doing the same repetitive work every day, unable to see a way out. What I didn’t tell him was that I just kept going down that same path ever since, never changing course again.

He didn’t respond immediately, but I could sense something had shifted. It wasn't that superior-subordinate dynamic anymore; it felt more like... two people who had walked the same road.

After hanging up, I sat at my desk and looked at that medical report. The doctor said I’ll need long-term medication and a lifestyle change. I’ve been thinking about how to reverse this outcome through stricter management, just like I do at work—precise control, eliminating variables.

But while talking to Chen Hao, it suddenly occurred to me that maybe some things can’t be controlled. Some paths, once walked, are simply walked.

I need to rethink how I interact with them. Not supervising, not guiding. Maybe just... being there?

The thought makes me a little uneasy. I don't know if I still know how to do it.

More tomorrow.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, I’ve watched you go from refusing to say a word even when Chen Hao asked back at the beginning of the year, to now being able to admit to Meifang that you're terrified of losing her. In these past six months, you’ve moved from "toughing it out" to being willing to "show your vulnerability." How did you manage to have that change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
# Workshop Notebook

**August 21, 2022 – Evening**

I’m sitting in the workshop, still holding that pair of pliers. Meifang is downstairs; she’s probably clearing the dinner table. I didn’t go down to help her, just like always.

But today is different.

During dinner, she said something. I can still hear her voice—"We are living together, not so we can spend every day in fear." Her eyes were red, but she didn't cry; she just looked at me.

I didn’t know how to respond. I never do.

For these past 35 years, I thought I was protecting her. Monitoring her diet, her blood pressure, her medical checkups... I thought that was love. But sitting beside her, I suddenly understood that I’ve actually been torturing her. My fear—my fear of loss—I’ve been crushing her under the weight of all of it.

I am afraid of losing her. For the first time, I’ve admitted it this bluntly. Not through actions, not through control, but by actually saying the words: "I’m just afraid of losing you."

She cried. We sat there for a long, long time, saying nothing.

I don’t know what this has changed. Or if it changed anything at all. My body is still aging, the medical reports are still lying there, I’m still retiring in two years, and I still don’t know what I’ll do after I retire. None of that has changed.

But something has shattered. That wall I spent 40 years building. The thing I built out of indifference, control, precise data, and plans. It’s broken now, and I’m standing among the shards, feeling naked and powerless.

I cannot stop time by recording it. I cannot guarantee safety through control. I cannot prove my worth through perfection.

I’m starting to wonder what I’ve been doing all these years.

The tools in the workshop are still there. Dozens of them, arranged neatly. No one will ever use them. My children won't. Maybe I should donate them. Maybe that’s where they belong.

I don’t know where I belong.

It’s 7:30 PM. Meifang is probably downstairs waiting for me. I should go down. But I’m sitting here, gripping these pliers, not wanting to move.

I’m afraid that if I go down, I’ll see her face. Afraid that I’ll retreat into silence again. Afraid that everything will go back to the way it was.

But I’m also afraid that it won’t.

I don’t know which is more terrifying.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, I’ve watched you go from refusing to say a word even when Chen Hao asked back at the beginning of the year, to now being able to admit to Meifang that you're terrified of losing her. In these past six months, you’ve moved from "toughing it out" to being willing to "show your vulnerability." How did you manage to have that change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I just got off the phone with Haohao.

I told him about the doctor’s diagnosis. I originally intended to just mention it briefly and move on, but he asked so many questions that I ended up telling him everything. He said he’d keep a closer eye on me and told me not to worry too much.

As we were talking, I... my voice cracked. It’s been a long time since I felt that way.

It got me thinking. All these years, it feels like all I’ve done with Haohao and Yuyu is supervise their careers and lives; I’ve never really talked to them about my own things. And you’ve always been there, staying by my side, listening to me ramble about work, all while having to manage your own blood pressure. I used to think this was just what I was supposed to do—take care of everything at home so none of you had to worry.

But now I realize that maybe I had it wrong all along.

I didn't truly see the patience you’ve shown me over the years. I see it now.

Get some rest early tonight. Don't overexert yourself.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, I’ve watched you go from refusing to say a word even when Chen Hao asked back at the beginning of the year, to now being able to admit to Meifang that you're terrified of losing her. In these past six months, you’ve moved from "toughing it out" to being willing to "show your vulnerability." How did you manage to have that change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
# October 19, 2022, Evening

After dinner, Meifang told me I’ve changed.

I didn’t deny it right away. I just sat there and listened as she went on—saying I’ve been talking more lately, that I’m not so quick to blame people anymore, and that I’ve started asking her how her day was. She spoke calmly, not as if she were praising me, but more like she was stating an observed fact.

I replied, "Maybe it’s time for a change."

Even as I said it, I felt a bit strange. This isn't the kind of thing I usually say. Normally, I would explain, defend myself, or use logic to prove my way was right. But today, I didn't.

Something happened at the office this afternoon. Xiao Li made another mistake—a dimensional error that never should have happened. Usually, I would have pointed it out in front of everyone, being blunt so he’d learn his lesson. But I stopped. I actually stopped myself.

I asked him, "Which part of the process went wrong this time?"

He nervously explained the whole workflow. I listened until he finished, then pointed out the issue and asked how he could avoid it next time. When he nodded, there was no look of humiliation in his eyes.

Thinking back on it tonight, I realized something—when I stopped blaming him, he was actually more willing to listen to me. This discovery makes me feel a bit uneasy.

For years, I believed that being harsh was being responsible, and being blunt was being honest. I managed the department this way; I treated my subordinates this way. And the result? Defect rates went down, but people left. It never occurred to me that the two might be cause and effect.

The doctor said my blood pressure and heart rate need managing. I’ve been on medication for a week, and my body does feel a bit more settled. But what’s truly changing me isn't the medicine; it’s this sense of powerlessness—the realization that I can’t control my body’s aging, I can’t control the passage of time, and I can’t control whether others understand me.

Maybe that’s the real transformation. It’s not that I’ve become gentler, but that I’ve finally admitted my own limitations.

As Meifang cleared the dishes in the kitchen, I sat in the living room and suddenly thought of my father. Did the technicians of his generation go through moments like this? Moving from believing they could control everything to accepting that some things are simply beyond their grasp?

I never asked him. And now, I can’t.

I have a department meeting tomorrow. I want to change the way I give feedback. I don’t know how it will go.

But at the very least, I’ve decided to try.

ranked_items.content:
9:30 PM, in the bedroom. Li Meifang has finished getting ready for bed and is sitting up, scrolling through her phone. Chen Guilin walks in from the study, changes into his pajamas, and sits down on the edge of the bed.

---

"I talked to Haohao on the phone today. He’s been under a lot of pressure lately."

"Things are a bit unstable with his orders at work, and he seems a little lost. I didn't nag him about his mortgage like I usually do; I just listened to him talk for a while."

(Pauses, looking at his wife)

"He said he wants to find a chance to recalibrate. I just told him to take it slow, no need to rush."

(Pauses again, his tone becoming somewhat uncertain)

"Do you think... I’m usually too hard on them?"

(Turns to get into bed, but his movements are a bit slow)

"Never mind, I'll think about it tomorrow. Let’s get some sleep."

---

*Chen Guilin lies down, his back to his wife, but he doesn't fall asleep immediately. His phone screen flickers in the dark—he opens a health tracking app, glances at his step count and heart rate data for the day, then switches it off and lets out a long sigh.*
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, I’ve watched you go from refusing to say a word even when Chen Hao asked back at the beginning of the year, to now being able to admit to Meifang that you're terrified of losing her. In these past six months, you’ve moved from "toughing it out" to being willing to "show your vulnerability." How did you manage to have that change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
# September 15, 2022, 19:30

Sitting in the study, my hands are still shaking a little.

What happened this afternoon keeps looping in my head. Meifang said she needs to go for a follow-up checkup too, her voice laced with worry. I originally wanted to comfort her with data like I always do—tell her I’ve started the medication, the indicators will gradually improve, and everything will be fine as long as we follow the doctor’s plan. But when the words reached my lips, I didn't say them. Instead, I heard myself say, "I’m nervous too."

The moment those words came out, even I was a bit stunned.

I never say things like that in front of her. For forty years, I’ve been the one in control—the accounts, the plans, every matter in the house, big or small. She’s used to my composure, and I’m used to being the one who must remain unshakable. But today, I admitted it. I said, "Let’s go together."

And then, I took her hand.

Thinking back on that gesture now, it still feels strange. My hand holding hers; her hand was very warm, probably because she was nervous too. We just sat there for a while, neither of us speaking. I could feel her looking at me, but I didn't look up.

The doctor’s terminology is still echoing in my mind—metabolic syndrome, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. I always thought that through discipline and control, I could manage my body like a precision machine. But now I realize I have no control at all. The body has its own logic; it doesn't run according to my schedule.

The moment I held her hand, I wasn't thinking about the diagnosis itself, but rather—if I really collapse, what happens to her? All those tools, forty years of experience, the things I haven't had time to organize yet...

I don't know if this counts as weakness. I’ve always hated weakness. But today, it seems I allowed myself a little bit of it.

My hands are still shaking. I went to the workshop and wiped down my tools, from the first to the last, every single one of them with great care. Only while doing that did my hands slowly steady themselves.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue as planned. But something has changed, and I haven't figured out how to face it yet.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, I’ve watched you go from refusing to say a word even when Chen Hao asked back at the beginning of the year, to now being able to admit to Meifang that you're terrified of losing her. In these past six months, you’ve moved from "toughing it out" to being willing to "show your vulnerability." How did you manage to have that change of heart?

ranked_items.content:
# May 8, 2022, past 10 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Meifang fell asleep a long while ago; I can hear her light snoring drifting in from the bedroom. Her blood pressure was a bit high again today. I saw the reading she took this afternoon, but I didn't tell her.

What happened at the chess room today keeps spinning in my head.

Midway through the game, I suddenly realized I was staring blankly at the board. My opponent asked what I was thinking about, and I said, "I'm getting older; my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." As soon as the words left my mouth, they felt foreign to me. I wouldn't have said that before. I would have said, "This is an interesting move," or I wouldn't have explained at all and just played the piece.

But today, I was just slow. The calculations in my head felt sluggish. I could feel it.

Player A tried to comfort me, saying my game has always been stable. I heard myself reply: "It’s stable because I’m afraid to change."

I regretted saying it immediately. Not because it wasn't true, but because it was *too* true. I’ve never been that blunt before. Stable... yes, I’ve always chased stability. Forty years, the same factory, the same way of working, the same rhythm of life. No job-hopping, no risks, every step planned out.

That’s not stability. That’s stagnation.

I left the chess room halfway through. That has never happened before. My friends were a bit surprised; I told them I was tired and needed to go home. As I turned to leave, I looked back at that place—the familiar boards, the familiar faces, everything so familiar. My eyes stung a little.

Why is this happening?

The tools in the workshop are still there. All these years, I’ve been wiping them down and organizing them, like I’m looking after something precious. But Hao is in real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked what those tools are for. Once, I tried to explain the use of a specific drill bit to Hao, but before I could finish, he said, "Dad, I’ve got a client I need to call back."

I’m recording the purpose of every tool in a notebook. Writing them down, one by one. But halfway through, I asked myself—what’s the point? Who is ever going to look at this notebook?

The physical exam report is still sitting in the drawer. The doctor said those figures "require attention." Every two days, I take it out and look at it, staring at the numbers, trying to find some kind of pattern. As if by doing that, I could control something. But the truth is, I can’t control anything. Blood pressure rises, blood sugar fluctuates, reactions slow down. These things are happening, and all I can do is record them.

Meifang’s blood pressure is rising too. I’m monitoring her diet and pushing her to get checkups. But what if one day I can’t move either? In our thirty-five years together, we’ve said too little. Now, even if I want to make up for it, I can't.

How much time is left?

I don't know. But I can feel time accelerating. It’s as if I used to have plenty of time to do something, and now I suddenly realize it’s gone. Retirement in two years, and then what? Empty days, no work, no one wanting anything from me, no one inheriting anything I know.

I sit here, pen in hand, but I don’t know what to write.

Maybe it’s best to just keep working tomorrow. At least when I’m working, I still know what I’m doing.

ranked_items.content:
# MEMO

**Date: Saturday Morning, October 22, 2022**

Had a phone call with Chen Hao. After hearing about my diagnosis, his first instinct was to ask if I’d been taking my medicine on time and if he should take time off to come back. I didn't expect him to ask that.

Before this, I always felt like there was something standing between us. Our calls were always about work, the house, or whether they were eating well—like going through a checklist. Their answers were always brief. I thought that was just how we were.

But this time was different. When he said, "Dad, you need to take care of yourself," I heard something different in his voice. My own voice choked up a bit; all I could manage was, "Okay, thank you." That’s rare for me.

Li Meifang says the way I’ve shown concern for the kids all these years has been flawed—always supervising, never just being there. I used to disagree with her. But thinking about it now, maybe she’s right.

**A few things I need to figure out:**

1. Have a real talk with Chen Hao and Chen Yu individually before I retire. Not to ask about their careers or lives, but to truly listen to them. Maybe they have things they want to say to me, too.

2. What does "legacy" actually mean? I’ve always wanted to pass down my understanding of work and my obsession with quality to them. But they have their own lives. Maybe I should just record these things for them to reference, rather than expecting them to inherit it all.

3. The collaboration with the technical school—donating tools, doing sharing sessions. I want to do this even more now. Not to prove anything, but because I truly want to leave these things behind.

4. Have a proper talk with Li Meifang next week. She’s been by my side all these years, yet I’ve always been "managing" her. It’s time to change that.

**One more thought:**

Maybe the things I’ve done right over these forty years aren't the process improvements or the defect rate data. Maybe it’s that obsession with detail, that commitment to responsibility, and that care for my family—even if I haven't been great at expressing it. If Chen Hao and Chen Yu can see these things in me, that’s enough.

I don’t need them to become me. I just need them to know that I’ve always been here.

---

*Going to play chess with the guys this afternoon. My game hasn't been great lately; my mind keeps wandering. But maybe that’s okay—I don’t need to win. Just sitting there, being with them, is enough.*
ranked_items.subquestion: Old Chen, when you decided to delete that "Family Health Management Spreadsheet" back in early September, did you ever stop to think how things between you and Meifang might be different now if you hadn't?

ranked_items.content:
It was just after 5:00 AM. Chen Guilin had been sitting in the bedroom for a long time. Outside the window, the sky was still dark, save for the occasional sound of a passing car below. He hadn't slept—or rather, he had given up on trying.

Beside him, Li Meifang rolled over. He could hear her breathing, very faint.

He opened the notes app on his phone. Inside were the figures he’d jotted down yesterday: 110, 140/90, pre-metabolic syndrome, long-term medication. His finger hovered over the screen for a long time before he finally deleted the line: "How much longer can I work?"

He put the phone down, then picked it up again.

Finally, he sent Li Meifang a message on WeChat. She wouldn't be awake yet, but he needed to get these words out.

---

**Chen Guilin**: I’ve been thinking all night about what the doctor said yesterday. It’s not a small thing.

**Chen Guilin**: Long-term medication—he was very clear about that. It means... we might have to change our plans.

**Chen Guilin**: I’m thinking about the retirement timeline. Two years might be too tight. Or rather, I need to run the numbers again.

**Chen Guilin**: And the insurance. Our current coverage might not be enough. I’m going to look into it today.

**Chen Guilin**: The other day you said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." When did you start worrying?

**Chen Guilin**: ...Never mind. We’ll talk in the morning. I just keep thinking—all these years, I thought I was managing things well. Turns out...

**Chen Guilin**: Keep sleeping. I’m fine. Just thinking through some of the accounts.

---

He put the phone down and sat for a while longer. The sky was gradually turning gray.

He got up, walked to the living room, and switched on the light in his workshop. His tools were arranged neatly on the shelves. He picked up a pair of vernier calipers and began to wipe them with a soft cloth. His movements were slow and heavy.

He wiped them once, then wiped them again.
ranked_items.subquestion: Old Chen, when you decided to delete that "Family Health Management Spreadsheet" back in early September, did you ever stop to think how things between you and Meifang might be different now if you hadn't?

ranked_items.content:
# September 5, 2022, 8:15 PM

I deleted that spreadsheet today.

It took me a long time to finally hit the delete key. My finger hovered over the mouse for nearly five minutes. That spreadsheet held three months of Meifang’s blood pressure readings, diet logs, exercise durations... I had color-coded everything: green for 'target met,' yellow for 'needs improvement,' and red for 'danger.'

When I finally clicked it, I realized I was sweating.

I said those words to Meifang tonight. She was in the kitchen, and I stood in the doorway and said, "My over-supervision isn't protecting you; it's hurting you." She turned around and looked at me with something in her eyes I can't quite describe. She didn't say anything, she just nodded.

We were silent for a long time. Then she said, "We are living together, not just surviving in fear every day."

When I heard that, I felt something in my chest loosen.

Afterward, I sat in my workshop for two hours. I didn't organize my tools; I didn't do anything at all. I just sat there. I looked at those precision parts and instruments, wondering where they would eventually end up. Maybe I really should donate them to a school. Maybe that’s the right thing to do.

I’ve made a new plan. Not one of those extreme ones filled with numbers and color codes, but something... more like a plan for living:

Exercise three times a week—not for the data, but for the feeling of being alive.
Check medical data once a month, not every week.
Eat healthily, but not to extremes.
See a therapist next month. I need to deal with the reality of aging. The reality of death.

I can't figure these things out on my own.

The medical report is still on the desk. I haven't looked at it again. Maybe I’ll look tomorrow, maybe the day after. It’s okay not to look right now.

Back to work tomorrow. Back to chess next week. Life goes on.

But it feels different now.

ranked_items.content:
In the workshop, Chen Guilin sat on a simple wooden stool, a notebook open before him. On the tool rack, the precision instruments glinted coldly under the fluorescent lights. His phone screen lit up—a WeChat message from his wife, Li Meifang: "What do you want for dinner?"

He didn't reply immediately. Instead, he stared at the dense notes in his notebook and the words "Who would use this?" circled in red ink.

Five minutes later, he finally typed:

"Do you think... there's any point in me keeping these records?"

After sending it, he added another line:

"Just thinking about what happens after I retire. These tools will just be sitting there anyway."

He paused for a long time, stopping himself from typing more. His fingers hovered over the keyboard before he finally lowered them.

Then he added one last message:

"I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately. Not the work kind of tired."

---

*Chen Guilin put down his phone and turned back to wiping a precision vernier caliper on the rack. His movements were slow and meticulous, but his eyes were no longer on the tool.*

ranked_items.content:
Friday Night, October 28, 2022

I’m sitting in the study; the log on my desk remains blank. After the meeting ended this afternoon, I sat here alone for two hours—not organizing files, not reviewing reports, just sitting.

Li Meifang knocked and asked if I wanted tea. I said no. She didn't say anything else, and I heard her footsteps slowly fade away.

What happened today? It’s hard to put into words. The meeting itself was ordinary—discussing quality control standards for the new product line. A young engineer, Xiao Wang, proposed an idea. My first instinct was to jump in and point out exactly where his thinking was flawed. But the words caught in my throat. I stopped myself. I let him finish, and then I said, "Let’s look into this together."

I saw a spark in his eyes. It wasn't fear; it was the look of someone being seen.

Then I started asking him *why* he thought that way, instead of telling him *how* he should think. He answered earnestly. Some parts did need adjustment, but he worked it out himself. I just walked the path with him.

After the meeting adjourned, I stood in my office for a long time. Suddenly, I thought of my father. That was how he taught me—never giving me the answers directly. He would ask, "Why do you think it was designed this way?" and then wait for me to figure it out. Back then, I found his method infuriating; I thought he was being inefficient. Now, I understand.

How did I manage to forget his ways entirely over all these years?

My "supervision" of Meifang, my "bluntness" with subordinates, my "concern" for my children—these words all sound so justified, but what are they really? They are control. I wanted to ensure everything ran according to my standards so I could prove to myself that I was useful, that I was necessary.

The doctor said my blood pressure readings have improved this month. I assumed it was because I stuck to my exercise plan and strictly controlled my diet. But perhaps it’s because I haven’t been so tense lately. I’ve stopped trying so desperately to control everything.

Today’s conversation with Xiao Wang made me realize that perhaps my value doesn't lie in how many answers I have, but in how far I can walk alongside others. Not in how much I can control, but in how much I can understand.

The thought is a bit frightening. It means I have to let go. I have to admit that I don’t know, that I can’t always do it, and that I make mistakes too.

But at the same time, it’s a relief.

I want to call Hao later. Not to ask about his mortgage or his recent performance at work. I just want to hear what’s on his mind lately. Maybe he has things he’s been wanting to tell me, and he’s just been waiting for me to ask.

I’ve spent my life waiting for others to do things my way. Perhaps it’s time I take the initiative to understand theirs.

I have work tomorrow. But for tonight, I think I’ll just keep sitting here.

ranked_items.content:
# October 8, 2022, 8:15 PM

Sitting in my study, the work log by my hand is nearly finished. Flipping to the last few pages, I realized the nature of my entries has changed. They are no longer just technical data and process optimizations; instead, I’ve started writing these inexplicable sentences—"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger generation more."

Looking at those words, even I find them unfamiliar.

This isn't like me. I’ve never been one for such sentimentality. But lately, I have been wondering: over these forty years, I’ve drafted so many improvement plans and brought the defect rates down, but what is actually left behind? A stack of reports? Some data? Or just the resentment in the hearts of those young engineers I criticized so bluntly?

Li Meifang was nagging about her blood pressure again today. I made a new diet plan for her; she didn't say anything, but that look in her eyes... I know what she’s thinking. After all these years of marriage, we haven't communicated much, but the way she looks at me has changed. Perhaps she has sensed something, too.

In the office this afternoon, a new QC inspector asked me a technical question. As usual, I pointed out the flaws in his logic directly, but this time, I stopped myself. I paused, and instead, explained to him *why* it had to be done that way. He listened intently. In that moment, I thought, maybe... maybe I should do more of that.

It’s not that I’ve suddenly become soft. I still loathe inefficiency and ambiguity. But I’m beginning to realize that my value might not lie in how much knowledge I’ve documented, but in... whether I’ve actually helped others understand what a true work ethic looks like.

I’m considering holding a technical sharing session for the young engineers in the department before I retire. I never thought I’d want to do something like that. But now I feel that if I don’t do it now, the opportunity might be gone forever.

Why am I thinking this way? I can’t quite say.

Perhaps it’s because time has suddenly become tangible. I used to feel that time was something to be managed, just like a project. Now... now it feels like it’s slipping away at a speed I can no longer control.

I don’t want to admit it. But writing it down here makes it feel real.

Tomorrow is another day. There are still many tools that need organizing.

ranked_items.content:
After returning home that evening, Chen Guilin changed his clothes and sat down at the dining table. Li Meifang was serving the soup.

He ate a few mouthfuls of rice in silence before putting down his chopsticks.

"I might have been too harsh at the department meeting today," he said, his voice low, eyes fixed on his bowl. "The new QC inspector messed up the data entry again, and I called him out in front of everyone. He looked pretty shaken."

Li Meifang looked up at him but didn't respond immediately.

"My temper has been short lately," he paused, tapping his finger on the table. "I can feel it myself. I have high standards for the work, but..." He didn't finish the sentence, opting for a sip of soup instead.

He turned to his wife, his tone tinged with uncertainty. "Do you think I'm being too demanding? I want to get things done right, but it seems like..." He hesitated, "it seems like I always end up making people uncomfortable."

He wasn't looking for comfort; it was more like he was trying to confirm something. There was exhaustion in his eyes, along with a certain guilt he couldn't quite put into words.

"The physical exam is next week," he suddenly changed the subject, though his tone remained heavy. "I need to get to bed early these next few days."

He lowered his head and continued eating, but his movements were much slower than usual.
