ranked_items.content:
October 30, 2022, 8:00 PM

Li Meifang is already asleep. I’m sitting in the study; on the desk lies today’s medical report and a cup of tea that went cold long ago.

I said something to her today that left even me a bit stunned. I said, "Perhaps the meaning of life isn't in perfect control, but in genuine connection." Hearing those words come out of my mouth felt like someone was impersonating me. And yet, it really is what I was thinking.

Since I started the medication these past few days, the physical side effects haven't been as bad as I imagined. The doctor says it’s a good sign. But what’s stranger is that I’ve actually started to relax. I used to check my medical reports three times a day, recording every single number in a notebook, trying to force them back to "normal" through sheer willpower. Now... I still look at the reports, but I no longer feel the need to "subdue" them.

Last night, Meifang took my hand. She does this often, and I used to pull away after a moment to get back to my accounts or the news. But this time, I didn't. I let her hold it—maybe for five or six seconds. She didn’t say anything, and neither did I. We just held hands. Eventually, she let go gently and went to the kitchen to pour me some water.

I realize now that I owe her a great deal. For thirty-five years, I’ve used excuses like "monitoring her blood pressure" or "managing the family finances" to package my need for control as "responsibility." In truth, it was just my way of proving I was useful, that I deserved to be needed—by controlling everything. She saw through it long ago, but she never said a word. She just stayed by my side, waiting.

Chen Hao called recently saying his mortgage is a heavy burden. In the past, I would have immediately started analyzing his finances, giving him advice, even feeling a bit dissatisfied with his "poor planning." This time, I just listened, then told him, "It’s okay, take your time." There was a pause on the other end of the line; he didn’t seem used to me responding like that. I’m not used to it either. But it felt... more real.

Then there’s that idea about donating my tools to the technical school. Initially, I thought of it as "legacy"—wanting young people to learn what I’ve accumulated over forty years. But today I realized that was just another form of control; I wanted their learning to prove that my value wouldn't vanish with retirement. I still want to donate them, but the reason has changed. I just want to put those tools where they can continue to be used and cherished. As for who uses them or what they learn, that’s not for me to decide, nor does it need to be.

It’s strange. I always thought letting go of control would make me panic. But actually, it makes me feel... light?

My subordinates at the office are still a bit afraid of me. I know that. But lately, I’ve been thinking that maybe I could try a different way. Not by lowering standards, but... by changing how I communicate. Letting them know my expectations are high because I believe they can do well, not because I’m disappointed in them. This will require me to say more, and I’ve never been good at that. But perhaps it’s worth learning.

Tomorrow is Monday. Another week begins. I’ll keep taking the medicine, keep working, and keep being there for Meifang. And maybe, at the right moment, I’ll sit with her—saying nothing, just holding her hand.

That is enough.

ranked_items.content:
It was just after 5:00 AM. Chen Guilin had been sitting in the bedroom for a long time. Outside the window, the sky was still dark, save for the occasional sound of a passing car below. He hadn't slept—or rather, he had given up on trying.

Beside him, Li Meifang rolled over. He could hear her breathing, very faint.

He opened the notes app on his phone. Inside were the figures he’d jotted down yesterday: 110, 140/90, pre-metabolic syndrome, long-term medication. His finger hovered over the screen for a long time before he finally deleted the line: "How much longer can I work?"

He put the phone down, then picked it up again.

Finally, he sent Li Meifang a message on WeChat. She wouldn't be awake yet, but he needed to get these words out.

---

**Chen Guilin**: I’ve been thinking all night about what the doctor said yesterday. It’s not a small thing.

**Chen Guilin**: Long-term medication—he was very clear about that. It means... we might have to change our plans.

**Chen Guilin**: I’m thinking about the retirement timeline. Two years might be too tight. Or rather, I need to run the numbers again.

**Chen Guilin**: And the insurance. Our current coverage might not be enough. I’m going to look into it today.

**Chen Guilin**: The other day you said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." When did you start worrying?

**Chen Guilin**: ...Never mind. We’ll talk in the morning. I just keep thinking—all these years, I thought I was managing things well. Turns out...

**Chen Guilin**: Keep sleeping. I’m fine. Just thinking through some of the accounts.

---

He put the phone down and sat for a while longer. The sky was gradually turning gray.

He got up, walked to the living room, and switched on the light in his workshop. His tools were arranged neatly on the shelves. He picked up a pair of vernier calipers and began to wipe them with a soft cloth. His movements were slow and heavy.

He wiped them once, then wiped them again.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, if you hadn't told Meifang about the "long-term medication" after reading the prescription in the car that day, how much longer do you think you could have toughed it out on your own?

ranked_items.content:
September 29, 2022 | Sunny | 7:30 AM

I hesitated for a long time when I picked up the pill.

Standing there by the kitchen sink, clutching that small white tablet, I saw that Li Meifang had already placed a glass of water next to me. She didn’t say anything; she just stood there, and I could feel her watching me. This feeling of being watched is unfamiliar. For all these years, I was the one watching her—making sure she took her blood pressure medication on time, watching her diet, checking her physical exam reports. Now, it’s reversed.

I know what swallowing this pill signifies. It’s not that I didn’t understand the doctor’s diagnosis before, but picking up the pill and actually taking it feel like two different things. One is data; the other is... reality.

I marked the medication time on the calendar. I used that 0.5mm black gel pen; the handwriting is small and neat. I also set three reminders on my phone—7:00 AM, 12:00 PM, and 7:00 PM. That way, I won't miss a dose. I can manage this. Through precise records, through making no mistakes, maybe...

But I’m lying to myself. I know that.

Only after I swallowed it did Li Meifang turn away to start the congee. She didn’t ask how I felt, nor did she say anything encouraging. She just went about her business quietly. For some reason, that made me feel worse. If she had said something, I would have known how to respond. But she said nothing, as if she has already accepted this, while I am still... still what?

I stared at the empty pill bottle for a long time. I’ve read the manual three times now. All the data, the dosage instructions, every possible side effect—I’ve committed them all to memory. This shouldn't be hard. I managed processes at the factory for twelve years; I’ve handled problems far more complex than this. But this is different.

This time, I can’t solve it by improving the process. I can’t reverse it through stricter management standards.

The office clock is about to strike 8:00. I should head out. But I sit here, looking at that marked calendar, thinking about how this single pill represents more than just blood pressure—it represents the fact that time is quietly slipping away.

How many more 7:00 AMs do I have left?

Forget it. Don’t think like that.

I stand up and organize the documents on my desk. Business as usual. Time for work.

ranked_items.content:
[Phone Memo]

09-12 Mon 09:30

---

110 mg/dL
Stage 2 Hypertension
Pre-metabolic syndrome
Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis

—The doctor’s exact words. I’ve noted them down.

Long-term medication. That phrase has been circling in my head all night. Long-term. What does that even mean?

---

In today’s meeting, Wang Lei’s data was wrong again. I was going to say, "This is unacceptable," but the words came out as, "Let’s look at how we can improve this together."

He froze for a second. So did I.

Why did I say that?

---

Talked to him privately for ten minutes after work. Explaining, not accusing. He said, "Manager Chen, you seem different today."

I didn't answer.

---

How much longer?

I find myself asking this every day now.

It’s been 40 years. From 1982 until now. How many working years are left? How many years left to pass these things—the tools, the experience, the standards—on to someone?

My wife said, "I knew you’d have this sooner or later."

She’s right. But I didn't want to hear it.

---

The precision tools room. Need to organize it this weekend. Not sure why that suddenly came to mind.

Maybe I should take photos to document them. Maybe I should make a list.

Maybe I should...

I don't know.

---

Going back for a follow-up checkup this afternoon. The doctor wants me to log my blood pressure.

Once every morning and evening.

Yet another new routine to maintain.

Weren't the old routines enough?

---

Chess buddy Jia said we’re playing Friday as usual. I said okay.

But I wonder, when I’m sitting at the chessboard, what will I be thinking about?

---

My hands are shaking. I noticed it just now while wiping down the tools.

I didn't notice when it started.

---

Need to monitor Li Meifang’s blood pressure too. She’s been sneaking pickles again lately.

I need to be stricter. Or...

No. It is what it is.

---

110 mg/dL

Is this number going to be here forever?

---

[End of Memo]

ranked_items.content:
# Calendar Event

**Date**: Saturday, October 22, 2022  
**Time**: 8:00 PM  
**Title**: Call with Chen Hao — Sharing the Diagnosis  

---

## Event Details

**Location**: Home Study  

**Participants**: Chen Guilin, Chen Hao (via phone)

**Description**:  
After receiving my medical report yesterday, I decided to call Chen Hao tonight. Instead of my usual routine of asking about his mortgage and work progress, I took the initiative and said, "There’s something I want to share with you."

The doctor said there is an issue with my heart and I’ll need long-term medication. I gave him the facts simply—no hiding, no exaggerating. There was a brief silence on his end before he asked, "Is it serious?" I told him, "It’s manageable, but I have to accept the reality."

He said he would make time to come back and see me. Instead of my usual response—"No need to take time off, work comes first"—I simply said, "Okay."

The call lasted about ten minutes. It was brief, but unlike our past conversations, it wasn't just an exchange of information.

---

## Notes

**Keywords**: Acceptance | Vulnerability | Communication | Change  

**Reflection**: This might be the first time I’ve proactively shared my "imperfections" with my child. I used to feel I had to maintain a position of strength in front of them, to prove I was still in control of everything. Now I realize that perhaps this kind of honesty is a different form of strength.

**Follow-up**: Remember to take medication on time. Go to the pharmacy on Monday.
ranked_items.subquestion: Brother Chen, if you hadn't told Meifang about the "long-term medication" after reading the prescription in the car that day, how much longer do you think you could have toughed it out on your own?

ranked_items.content:
3:30 AM. Awake again.

The photo of that prescription is still glowing on my phone. I don’t know why I keep looking at it over and over. The numbers are the same every time: blood pressure 140/110, fasting blood glucose 110 mg/dL, LDL-C 3.8 mmol/L, carotid intima-media thickness... The doctor used the term "pre-metabolic syndrome." And "long-term medication."

Long-term.

I shone a flashlight on the paper prescription, reading it word by word. The doctor’s handwriting is clear. I didn't misread it. I copied the data into my notebook again, just like I would verify a test report at work. Confirmed. It’s ironic.

How much longer can I keep working?

My hand was shaking as I wrote that sentence. Then I crossed it out. I wrote it again. Crossed it out again. In the end, I just sat there, staring at the blackened ink.

Forty years. From the day I entered the factory in 1982 until now, every day has gone according to plan. Every process precise to the millimeter. The defect rate in the department I manage is 25% lower than the industry average. I have never wasted a single minute. Regular diet, regular sleep, annual check-ups. I thought that was enough. I thought I was in control.

But the doctor said it wasn't enough.

Meifang’s words keep circling in my head: "I knew you’d have this sooner or later." She said it so calmly, like she was stating a fact she’d known all along. I didn’t argue. Because I realized I couldn't find the words to talk back. She saw right through me. She saw through forty years of self-deception.

I don’t believe this result. But I can’t deny it either.

There are four medications on the prescription. I haven't filled them yet. I sit in my office, looking at those precision tools, imagining myself taking these pills every day, my body slowly...

How many years are left?

I tried to draft a plan in my notebook. Retirement date, financial arrangements, things for the children. But I couldn't keep writing. Because suddenly, I don't know what the deadline for these plans is anymore.

Dawn is breaking. I should get up. But I don’t want to move.

I think I need to recalculate everything. But right now, I don’t have the strength.

— Chen Guilin
September 11, 2022, 3:30 AM

# Healthcare Management Plan

**September 10, 2022 – September 30, 2022**

## 🔴 Immediate Actions

1. [ ] Go to the community health center before 8:00 AM tomorrow to refill prescriptions—hypertension medication and statins; ensure everything on the script is covered.
2. [ ] Tell Li Meifang the test results tonight—explain the doctor's advice clearly, but don't let her worry too much.
3. [ ] Set up a blood pressure log—measure once every morning and evening, and record it in the notebook (Format: Date/Time/Systolic/Diastolic).

## 📋 To Be Completed This Week

4. [ ] Take a photo of the prescription list before work on Monday and save it on the phone for easy reference of dosage and usage.
5. [ ] Go to the hospital at noon on Wednesday to book the 3-month follow-up appointment (Cardiology); mark the date on the calendar.
6. [ ] Organize the household grocery list—list high-oil and high-salt foods and tell Li Meifang which ones to cut back on.
7. [ ] Check the company's health insurance coverage to see if long-term medications require out-of-pocket reimbursement.

## 💪 Lifestyle Adjustments

8. [ ] Create a weekly exercise schedule—30-minute walk after dinner every day, plus one extra session on weekends (log on the calendar).
9. [ ] Reduce social dinners—inform the supervisor that I need to focus on my health for a while and decline unnecessary dinner invitations.

## 🏠 Family Related

10. [ ] Update the family financial spreadsheet—set aside a budget for medical expenses and adjust the investment portfolio (reduce risk).
11. [ ] Review Li Meifang’s physical exam report—her blood pressure issues also need attention; manage them together.

## 📝 Notes

- Medication must be taken on time without interruption; this is non-negotiable.
- Summarize blood pressure data once a week to check for improvement trends.
- Prepare one week before the next follow-up; organize recent data to show the doctor.
- Do not tell Chen Hao and Chen Yu—there's no need to worry them for now; focus on stabilizing my own condition first.

# Health Management Plan - September 2022

**Date of Record:** September 29, 2022  
**Date of Diagnosis:** September 23, 2022  
**Attending Physician:** Cardiologist  

---

## I. Summary of Diagnosis

- Hypertension (Stage II)
- Signs of Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (LVH)
- Long-term medication management required
- Doctor's Recommendation: Immediate commencement of medication + lifestyle intervention

---

## II. Prescribed Medication and Dosage Schedule

| Medication | Dosage | Frequency | Time | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Amlodipine | 5mg | Once daily | 07:00 | Take in the morning |
| Valsartan | 80mg | Once daily | 19:00 | Take in the evening |

**Mobile Reminders:** ✓ Set  
**Storage:** Bedroom nightstand; keep in a cool, dry place away from light.

---

## III. Lifestyle Modification Plan

### A. Dietary Adjustments
- [ ] Daily salt intake < 6g (Li Meifang informed)
- [ ] Reduce intake of high-fat foods
- [ ] Increase proportion of vegetables and whole grains
- [ ] Limit pickled foods and processed meats
- [ ] Alcohol cessation (previously an occasional social drinker; now stopped)

### B. Exercise Plan
- Aerobic exercise 3–4 times per week, 30 minutes per session
- Primarily walking (Monday, Wednesday, Friday evenings after 6:00 PM)
- Avoid strenuous exercise and sudden physical exertion
- Continue Go (Weiqi) activities, but be mindful of excessive stress/tension

### C. Weight Management
- Target BMI: Under 24
- Current Weight: To be measured and recorded this week

### D. Stress Management
- Continue playing Go; it remains the most effective way to relax
- Maintain existing work pace; do not take on additional pressure
- Evening professional journal reading time can remain unchanged

---

## IV. Medical Check-up and Follow-up Schedule

| Item | First Follow-up | Subsequent Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Blood Pressure Monitoring | In 2 weeks (Oct 13) | Every 2 weeks |
| Blood Tests | In 1 month (Oct 29) | Every 3 months |
| ECG | In 3 months (Dec 29) | Every 6 months |
| Echocardiogram | In 6 months (Mar 29, 2023) | Annually |

**Note:** All follow-ups must be booked in advance and documented.

---

## V. Symptom Monitoring Checklist

**Seek immediate medical attention for:**
- Chest pain or discomfort
- Severe dizziness or vertigo
- Shortness of breath
- Blurred vision
- Slurred speech

**Daily Monitoring Items (to be recorded daily):**
- Morning blood pressure (use electronic monitor; record in log)
- Presence of dizziness or headaches
- Sleep quality
- Fatigue levels

---

## VI. Home Blood Pressure Log

**Purchase Plan:** Buy a home electronic blood pressure monitor this weekend (Model TBD)

| Date | Morning BP | Evening BP | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sept 29 | - | - | Awaiting equipment purchase |
| | | | |

---

## VII. Key Information

**Doctor’s Contact:** Saved in phone notes  
**Pharmacy Location:** 1st Floor Pharmacy, hospital near home  
**Emergency Contact:** Li Meifang (Wife)

---

## VIII. Personal Reflections

The doctor said this diagnosis isn't an end, but a new beginning. I believe that by executing this plan with precision, I can keep the situation under control. I have managed workflows and quality standards for 40 years in my career; managing my own health should be well within my capabilities.

The keys are **consistency** and **data**. I need to approach this like my work—every detail must be recorded, and every metric must be tracked.

I haven't told Chen Hao or Chen Yu yet. They have their own pressures right now, and there's no need to add to their burden. Meifang already knows; she has been more cautious than usual these past two days, and I can sense her worry. I need her to see that I have everything under control so she can feel at ease.

Next step: Purchase the blood pressure monitor this weekend; first follow-up on October 13.

---

ranked_items.content:
# Workshop Notebook

**August 21, 2022 – Evening**

I’m sitting in the workshop, still holding that pair of pliers. Meifang is downstairs; she’s probably clearing the dinner table. I didn’t go down to help her, just like always.

But today is different.

During dinner, she said something. I can still hear her voice—"We are living together, not so we can spend every day in fear." Her eyes were red, but she didn't cry; she just looked at me.

I didn’t know how to respond. I never do.

For these past 35 years, I thought I was protecting her. Monitoring her diet, her blood pressure, her medical checkups... I thought that was love. But sitting beside her, I suddenly understood that I’ve actually been torturing her. My fear—my fear of loss—I’ve been crushing her under the weight of all of it.

I am afraid of losing her. For the first time, I’ve admitted it this bluntly. Not through actions, not through control, but by actually saying the words: "I’m just afraid of losing you."

She cried. We sat there for a long, long time, saying nothing.

I don’t know what this has changed. Or if it changed anything at all. My body is still aging, the medical reports are still lying there, I’m still retiring in two years, and I still don’t know what I’ll do after I retire. None of that has changed.

But something has shattered. That wall I spent 40 years building. The thing I built out of indifference, control, precise data, and plans. It’s broken now, and I’m standing among the shards, feeling naked and powerless.

I cannot stop time by recording it. I cannot guarantee safety through control. I cannot prove my worth through perfection.

I’m starting to wonder what I’ve been doing all these years.

The tools in the workshop are still there. Dozens of them, arranged neatly. No one will ever use them. My children won't. Maybe I should donate them. Maybe that’s where they belong.

I don’t know where I belong.

It’s 7:30 PM. Meifang is probably downstairs waiting for me. I should go down. But I’m sitting here, gripping these pliers, not wanting to move.

I’m afraid that if I go down, I’ll see her face. Afraid that I’ll retreat into silence again. Afraid that everything will go back to the way it was.

But I’m also afraid that it won’t.

I don’t know which is more terrifying.
ranked_items.subquestion: Looking back at the transformation in your relationship with your wife over the past two months, you used to always rely on data and protocols to show you cared. What made you willing to start telling Meifang so directly, "I'm afraid of losing you"?

ranked_items.content:
# October 1, 2022, 6:45 PM

Just got back from a walk with Meifang. The same old route—from the front door to the park and back, about thirty minutes.

I’ve been thinking about what she said. "I knew this day would come for you. I’ve been waiting for you to admit that you, too, will grow old, and you, too, will get sick."

When did she see it? I keep wondering. Maybe she saw it a long time ago. Maybe from the moment I started monitoring her blood pressure and her diet, she saw right through me—that I was actually afraid. I always thought I was caring for her, but in reality... I was controlling. Controlling her in the name of care.

This realization is hard to swallow.

During our walk, I told her, "I’ve been monitoring you all this time, but I never once asked how you felt." She didn’t answer immediately; she just kept walking. After a while, she said, "It’s okay. I’m used to it." That sentence hurt more than any accusation could.

I wonder if I’ve been like this for the past forty years. Being blunt with my subordinates at work, and doing the same with my wife and children at home—demanding, managing, and dismissing them under the guise of "it’s for your own good." My eldest son’s mortgage pressure, my daughter’s marriage—I’ve been paying attention to all of it, but I’ve never truly asked them what they want.

Lately, I’ve been obsessed with this health management plan. Logging blood pressure, recording meals, drafting exercise routines. I told myself that as long as I managed it well enough, I could reverse the doctor’s diagnosis. But during the walk today, it hit me—maybe I’m not managing my health; I’m in denial. Using data and schedules to deny that diagnosis.

She said, "you, too, will grow old, and you, too, will get sick." I’ve never wanted to admit that. I thought that if I were disciplined enough, precise enough, I could be the exception. But maybe there are no exceptions.

At dinner, she was the one who asked if I wanted to go for another walk. I said yes. For the first time, she was the one asking me, rather than me finding excuses to refuse.

I haven't told the children yet. What am I waiting for? Maybe I’m waiting until I’m ready to admit that this isn’t a problem that can be "managed" away.

Work tomorrow. Three process improvement plans to finish. Still so much to do.

But tonight, sitting here, none of that is on my mind.
ranked_items.subquestion: Looking back at the transformation in your relationship with your wife over the past two months, you used to always rely on data and protocols to show you cared. What made you willing to start telling Meifang so directly, "I'm afraid of losing you"?

ranked_items.content:
# September 15, 2022, 19:30

Sitting in the study, my hands are still shaking a little.

What happened this afternoon keeps looping in my head. Meifang said she needs to go for a follow-up checkup too, her voice laced with worry. I originally wanted to comfort her with data like I always do—tell her I’ve started the medication, the indicators will gradually improve, and everything will be fine as long as we follow the doctor’s plan. But when the words reached my lips, I didn't say them. Instead, I heard myself say, "I’m nervous too."

The moment those words came out, even I was a bit stunned.

I never say things like that in front of her. For forty years, I’ve been the one in control—the accounts, the plans, every matter in the house, big or small. She’s used to my composure, and I’m used to being the one who must remain unshakable. But today, I admitted it. I said, "Let’s go together."

And then, I took her hand.

Thinking back on that gesture now, it still feels strange. My hand holding hers; her hand was very warm, probably because she was nervous too. We just sat there for a while, neither of us speaking. I could feel her looking at me, but I didn't look up.

The doctor’s terminology is still echoing in my mind—metabolic syndrome, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. I always thought that through discipline and control, I could manage my body like a precision machine. But now I realize I have no control at all. The body has its own logic; it doesn't run according to my schedule.

The moment I held her hand, I wasn't thinking about the diagnosis itself, but rather—if I really collapse, what happens to her? All those tools, forty years of experience, the things I haven't had time to organize yet...

I don't know if this counts as weakness. I’ve always hated weakness. But today, it seems I allowed myself a little bit of it.

My hands are still shaking. I went to the workshop and wiped down my tools, from the first to the last, every single one of them with great care. Only while doing that did my hands slowly steady themselves.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue as planned. But something has changed, and I haven't figured out how to face it yet.

# Calendar Event

**Event Title:** Heart-to-heart with Meifang – A New Way of Being Together

**Date:** Tuesday, October 26, 2022

**Time:** 20:00 – 21:00

**Location:** Living Room

**Reminder:** 1 day before

---

**Notes:**

I need to have a proper talk with Meifang this week. Not the usual monitoring or correcting, but a real conversation.

The doctor said I need to adjust my lifestyle, and I’ve seen clearly how much she’s been there for me all these years. I used to feel like I had to control everything—her diet, her checkups, the household accounts—as if that was the only way to prove I was a responsible husband. Looking back, it only wore her down.

This time, I want to make a few things clear:
- I’ll manage my own medication; she doesn't need to remind me.
- We’ll manage her blood pressure together, rather than me just making demands.
- We can go for walks on weekends instead of me just staying home organizing tools.

Maybe I should also talk to her about retirement plans. I used to think I should just plan everything myself, but now I feel I should hear what she thinks. It’s been 35 years; it’s time I truly listened to her.

This isn't some major event, but it’s important to me. I have to remind myself not to slip back into that "work report" tone.

---

- [ ] Prepare a few specific ideas, but don't show her a list.
- [ ] No phone after dinner.
- [ ] Listen to her—really listen.

ranked_items.content:
# October 8, 2022, 8:15 PM

Sitting in my study, the work log by my hand is nearly finished. Flipping to the last few pages, I realized the nature of my entries has changed. They are no longer just technical data and process optimizations; instead, I’ve started writing these inexplicable sentences—"If I could do it over, I would teach the younger generation more."

Looking at those words, even I find them unfamiliar.

This isn't like me. I’ve never been one for such sentimentality. But lately, I have been wondering: over these forty years, I’ve drafted so many improvement plans and brought the defect rates down, but what is actually left behind? A stack of reports? Some data? Or just the resentment in the hearts of those young engineers I criticized so bluntly?

Li Meifang was nagging about her blood pressure again today. I made a new diet plan for her; she didn't say anything, but that look in her eyes... I know what she’s thinking. After all these years of marriage, we haven't communicated much, but the way she looks at me has changed. Perhaps she has sensed something, too.

In the office this afternoon, a new QC inspector asked me a technical question. As usual, I pointed out the flaws in his logic directly, but this time, I stopped myself. I paused, and instead, explained to him *why* it had to be done that way. He listened intently. In that moment, I thought, maybe... maybe I should do more of that.

It’s not that I’ve suddenly become soft. I still loathe inefficiency and ambiguity. But I’m beginning to realize that my value might not lie in how much knowledge I’ve documented, but in... whether I’ve actually helped others understand what a true work ethic looks like.

I’m considering holding a technical sharing session for the young engineers in the department before I retire. I never thought I’d want to do something like that. But now I feel that if I don’t do it now, the opportunity might be gone forever.

Why am I thinking this way? I can’t quite say.

Perhaps it’s because time has suddenly become tangible. I used to feel that time was something to be managed, just like a project. Now... now it feels like it’s slipping away at a speed I can no longer control.

I don’t want to admit it. But writing it down here makes it feel real.

Tomorrow is another day. There are still many tools that need organizing.

ranked_items.content:
# September 5, 2022, 8:15 PM

I deleted that spreadsheet today.

It took me a long time to finally hit the delete key. My finger hovered over the mouse for nearly five minutes. That spreadsheet held three months of Meifang’s blood pressure readings, diet logs, exercise durations... I had color-coded everything: green for 'target met,' yellow for 'needs improvement,' and red for 'danger.'

When I finally clicked it, I realized I was sweating.

I said those words to Meifang tonight. She was in the kitchen, and I stood in the doorway and said, "My over-supervision isn't protecting you; it's hurting you." She turned around and looked at me with something in her eyes I can't quite describe. She didn't say anything, she just nodded.

We were silent for a long time. Then she said, "We are living together, not just surviving in fear every day."

When I heard that, I felt something in my chest loosen.

Afterward, I sat in my workshop for two hours. I didn't organize my tools; I didn't do anything at all. I just sat there. I looked at those precision parts and instruments, wondering where they would eventually end up. Maybe I really should donate them to a school. Maybe that’s the right thing to do.

I’ve made a new plan. Not one of those extreme ones filled with numbers and color codes, but something... more like a plan for living:

Exercise three times a week—not for the data, but for the feeling of being alive.
Check medical data once a month, not every week.
Eat healthily, but not to extremes.
See a therapist next month. I need to deal with the reality of aging. The reality of death.

I can't figure these things out on my own.

The medical report is still on the desk. I haven't looked at it again. Maybe I’ll look tomorrow, maybe the day after. It’s okay not to look right now.

Back to work tomorrow. Back to chess next week. Life goes on.

But it feels different now.

ranked_items.content:
October 16, 2022, Evening

It’s been two hours since I got back from the hospital. Sitting here in the study, I still find myself somewhat at a loss for words.

The results from this morning’s check-up were steadier than expected. The doctor said the medication is working well and my heart rate is stabilizing. I had prepared a mountain of data and questions to ask, but with Meifang sitting beside me, I suddenly changed my mind. I heard myself say, "We are both cooperating with the treatment"—a strange choice of words coming from me. In the past, I would have said, "I am strictly managing it," or "I am controlling my diet." But today was different.

As I held my wife’s hand, I realized what I’ve been doing to her all these years. I’ve been "monitoring" her—monitoring her blood pressure, her diet, her physical exams. It was as if she were a quality control metric for me to manage. Yet she never once resisted. She was just there, by my side.

The doctor suggested joining a health management course. My first instinct was to refuse—too busy with work, not enough time. But I looked at Meifang and didn't say no right away. I asked her, "What do you think?" Those four words felt foreign coming out of my mouth. She said we could give it a try. And so, it was decided.

I wonder if this is what they call "vulnerability." Admitting that I can’t control everything, that I can’t stop my body from aging, and that I can’t ensure every plan stays on schedule. It’s not a comfortable feeling. But it’s not as terrifying as I imagined, either.

Perhaps all these years, I’ve been using perfect self-discipline to prove my worth. Now, I’m starting to wonder: was it worth it?

Meifang is downstairs preparing dinner. I can hear the sounds from the kitchen. It’s quiet, ordinary. But today, it sounds different.

I haven’t quite figured out what all of this means yet. But at the very least, I’m no longer in such a hurry.
ranked_items.subquestion: Looking back at the transformation in your relationship with your wife over the past two months, you used to always rely on data and protocols to show you cared. What made you willing to start telling Meifang so directly, "I'm afraid of losing you"?

ranked_items.content:
# October 19, 2022, Evening

After dinner, Meifang told me I’ve changed.

I didn’t deny it right away. I just sat there and listened as she went on—saying I’ve been talking more lately, that I’m not so quick to blame people anymore, and that I’ve started asking her how her day was. She spoke calmly, not as if she were praising me, but more like she was stating an observed fact.

I replied, "Maybe it’s time for a change."

Even as I said it, I felt a bit strange. This isn't the kind of thing I usually say. Normally, I would explain, defend myself, or use logic to prove my way was right. But today, I didn't.

Something happened at the office this afternoon. Xiao Li made another mistake—a dimensional error that never should have happened. Usually, I would have pointed it out in front of everyone, being blunt so he’d learn his lesson. But I stopped. I actually stopped myself.

I asked him, "Which part of the process went wrong this time?"

He nervously explained the whole workflow. I listened until he finished, then pointed out the issue and asked how he could avoid it next time. When he nodded, there was no look of humiliation in his eyes.

Thinking back on it tonight, I realized something—when I stopped blaming him, he was actually more willing to listen to me. This discovery makes me feel a bit uneasy.

For years, I believed that being harsh was being responsible, and being blunt was being honest. I managed the department this way; I treated my subordinates this way. And the result? Defect rates went down, but people left. It never occurred to me that the two might be cause and effect.

The doctor said my blood pressure and heart rate need managing. I’ve been on medication for a week, and my body does feel a bit more settled. But what’s truly changing me isn't the medicine; it’s this sense of powerlessness—the realization that I can’t control my body’s aging, I can’t control the passage of time, and I can’t control whether others understand me.

Maybe that’s the real transformation. It’s not that I’ve become gentler, but that I’ve finally admitted my own limitations.

As Meifang cleared the dishes in the kitchen, I sat in the living room and suddenly thought of my father. Did the technicians of his generation go through moments like this? Moving from believing they could control everything to accepting that some things are simply beyond their grasp?

I never asked him. And now, I can’t.

I have a department meeting tomorrow. I want to change the way I give feedback. I don’t know how it will go.

But at the very least, I’ve decided to try.
ranked_items.subquestion: Looking back at the transformation in your relationship with your wife over the past two months, you used to always rely on data and protocols to show you cared. What made you willing to start telling Meifang so directly, "I'm afraid of losing you"?

ranked_items.content:
# May 8, 2022, past 10 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Meifang fell asleep a long while ago; I can hear her light snoring drifting in from the bedroom. Her blood pressure was a bit high again today. I saw the reading she took this afternoon, but I didn't tell her.

What happened at the chess room today keeps spinning in my head.

Midway through the game, I suddenly realized I was staring blankly at the board. My opponent asked what I was thinking about, and I said, "I'm getting older; my reactions aren't as sharp anymore." As soon as the words left my mouth, they felt foreign to me. I wouldn't have said that before. I would have said, "This is an interesting move," or I wouldn't have explained at all and just played the piece.

But today, I was just slow. The calculations in my head felt sluggish. I could feel it.

Player A tried to comfort me, saying my game has always been stable. I heard myself reply: "It’s stable because I’m afraid to change."

I regretted saying it immediately. Not because it wasn't true, but because it was *too* true. I’ve never been that blunt before. Stable... yes, I’ve always chased stability. Forty years, the same factory, the same way of working, the same rhythm of life. No job-hopping, no risks, every step planned out.

That’s not stability. That’s stagnation.

I left the chess room halfway through. That has never happened before. My friends were a bit surprised; I told them I was tired and needed to go home. As I turned to leave, I looked back at that place—the familiar boards, the familiar faces, everything so familiar. My eyes stung a little.

Why is this happening?

The tools in the workshop are still there. All these years, I’ve been wiping them down and organizing them, like I’m looking after something precious. But Hao is in real estate in Shenzhen, and Yu is teaching in Guangzhou. They’ve never asked what those tools are for. Once, I tried to explain the use of a specific drill bit to Hao, but before I could finish, he said, "Dad, I’ve got a client I need to call back."

I’m recording the purpose of every tool in a notebook. Writing them down, one by one. But halfway through, I asked myself—what’s the point? Who is ever going to look at this notebook?

The physical exam report is still sitting in the drawer. The doctor said those figures "require attention." Every two days, I take it out and look at it, staring at the numbers, trying to find some kind of pattern. As if by doing that, I could control something. But the truth is, I can’t control anything. Blood pressure rises, blood sugar fluctuates, reactions slow down. These things are happening, and all I can do is record them.

Meifang’s blood pressure is rising too. I’m monitoring her diet and pushing her to get checkups. But what if one day I can’t move either? In our thirty-five years together, we’ve said too little. Now, even if I want to make up for it, I can't.

How much time is left?

I don't know. But I can feel time accelerating. It’s as if I used to have plenty of time to do something, and now I suddenly realize it’s gone. Retirement in two years, and then what? Empty days, no work, no one wanting anything from me, no one inheriting anything I know.

I sit here, pen in hand, but I don’t know what to write.

Maybe it’s best to just keep working tomorrow. At least when I’m working, I still know what I’m doing.
ranked_items.subquestion: Looking back at the transformation in your relationship with your wife over the past two months, you used to always rely on data and protocols to show you cared. What made you willing to start telling Meifang so directly, "I'm afraid of losing you"?

ranked_items.content:
Meifang, I’ve been thinking about what you said.

I’ve been thinking about it ever since we got back from our walk.

You’re right. I’ve been in denial. I’ve used management, data, and schedules to deny it. Just like I do at the factory—as if as long as the process is rigorous enough, everything can be controlled. But the body isn't a machine. I know that.

I monitor your diet and push you to get checkups, but I’m actually just deflecting. Deflecting my own fear. I’ve been this way for years—replacing words with actions, and replacing companionship with control. I’ve done it to you, and I’ve done it to the kids.

I owe you an apology. I owe you so much more than that.

Shall we go for a walk together next week? Not to supervise you, but just... to be with you. The way you’ve always been there for me.

I still have a lot to learn. From you.

— Chen Guilin

b1607a3d-4353-46c7-8813-6e545e5e0660.question: I’ve noticed that whenever you’re stressed or anxious lately, your fingers start making these subtle movements—like tapping rapidly on the desk or repeatedly rubbing those paper reports. What exactly is going through your mind in those moments?

ranked_items.content:
April 28, 2022, 11:15 PM

I’m sitting in the study. The lights are off. It’s pitch black outside the window.

I’ve been sitting here in the dark for a long time. My hand is pressed against my chest; I can feel my heartbeat. The rhythm is irregular. My breathing is a bit shallow, but I haven't moved. I just sit here, feeling it all.

The physical exam report is still on the desk. Blood pressure 139/88, fasting blood sugar 6.8. There’s a waveform on the ECG that the doctor said needs observation. He used the phrase "requires attention." I heard him loud and clear.

I’m thinking: this is how it begins.

I press my fingers against my pulse and count—76 beats per minute. Within the normal range. But I know it was racing just a moment ago. Why did it speed up? No exercise, nothing special happened. I was just sitting here when I suddenly felt... something I can't put into words.

Hao called today. He said the mortgage pressure is heavy and he might have to pay back a little less this month. I didn't say much; I just noted it down in my ledger. The mortgage, his sales commissions, the fluctuations of Shenzhen’s housing prices. I track all these data points. But what’s the use of tracking? Can I change anything?

The tools in the workshop are still on the shelves. Lately, I’ve been numbering them, writing down their uses and the years they were purchased. Some were bought in 1995, others in 2008. I wonder, if I were suddenly gone, who would know how to use these things? Not Hao. Not Yu. And certainly not Meifang.

They would just become scrap metal. Or be tossed out as junk.

I’ve been sitting in the dark for a long time, without turning on the light.

Tomorrow, I need to schedule a more detailed check-up. Cardiology. I need to know exactly what that abnormal waveform means. I need to establish a long-term monitoring plan. Every month’s data must be recorded so I can see the trends.

I also need to keep supervising Meifang’s blood pressure. She’s started eating salty foods again; I have to remind her once more.

My breathing has settled down a bit now.

But that sense of unease remains.

It’s as if something is slowly coming loose, and I can’t reach out to catch it.
ranked_items.subquestion: I’ve noticed that whenever you’re stressed or anxious lately, your fingers start making these subtle movements—like tapping rapidly on the desk or repeatedly rubbing those paper reports. What exactly is going through your mind in those moments?

ranked_items.content:
# August 15, 2022, 3:20 AM

Can’t sleep. I’ve been awake for two hours now.

My chest still feels a bit tight; I can feel my heartbeat when I press down on it. My rational mind tells me this is anxiety, not a heart condition. But my fingers still instinctively press down several times, trying to be sure. My breathing is a bit shallow. I tell myself to take deep breaths—one, two, three, exhale—but the more I try to control it, the more labored it feels. It’s ridiculous.

I turned off my phone. Meifang knocked on the door and asked if I wanted some warm water. I didn't respond. She didn't come in eventually; I heard her footsteps slowly fading away. She’s used to me being like this. After 35 years, she knows all too well that I don’t like to be disturbed, especially at times like this. But I could sense her lingering outside the door for a few seconds. I feel a twinge of guilt, but I don't have the energy to deal with that right now.

I checked the Excel sheet again this afternoon. Meifang’s blood pressure readings for this week: 150/95, 148/92, 152/98… I marked three of them in red pen. I wrote "follow-up appointment needed" next to them, and then "dietary control." But I know these numbers don’t change anything. I can record them, I can mark them, I can make plans, but I cannot change the fact that her body is aging.

Last night, I searched for information on hypertension complications again. Stroke, myocardial infarction, kidney failure… page after page. I told myself to stop, that searching like this is pointless. But my fingers kept clicking. It’s as if something is driving me to confirm the worst-case scenarios, as if knowing in advance would somehow make me prepared. It’s stupid. I know perfectly well it’s stupid.

My own data sheets are there, too. Daily exercise time, step count, heart rate, blood sugar levels… all neatly arranged. It’s as if as long as these numbers stay in the green zone, I can guarantee that nothing will go wrong with me. But I know I’m lying to myself. I can’t stop aging by keeping records. I can’t guarantee my heart won’t fail through a strict diet. This realization terrifies me more than any abnormal data point ever could.

The physical exam is in two weeks. I’m both looking forward to it and dreading it. I’m looking for a definitive answer, yet I’m afraid that answer will confirm all the premonitions I’ve had over the past few months.

The tools in the workshop haven't been wiped down yet. I was supposed to organize those precision instruments today, but I just couldn't find the motivation. I sat there, looking at them, thinking that I might not have much time left. Time seems to be accelerating. Last year, retirement still felt far off; now, it feels like two years have passed in the blink of an eye.

No good news from the kids either. Hao is still under a lot of pressure with his mortgage, and Yu hasn't said much lately. I want to give them a call, but I don't know what to say. Ask them how their health is? That would be too strange. Or just listen to them talk about work in silence? There doesn't seem to be any other way for us to communicate anymore.

It’s not dawn yet. The study is very quiet. It’s just me and these data points, these records—these things I try to use to fight against this sense of powerlessness.

I think I need to sleep. But I know I won’t be able to.

ranked_items.content:
# September 15, 2022, 19:30

Sitting in the study, my hands are still shaking a little.

What happened this afternoon keeps looping in my head. Meifang said she needs to go for a follow-up checkup too, her voice laced with worry. I originally wanted to comfort her with data like I always do—tell her I’ve started the medication, the indicators will gradually improve, and everything will be fine as long as we follow the doctor’s plan. But when the words reached my lips, I didn't say them. Instead, I heard myself say, "I’m nervous too."

The moment those words came out, even I was a bit stunned.

I never say things like that in front of her. For forty years, I’ve been the one in control—the accounts, the plans, every matter in the house, big or small. She’s used to my composure, and I’m used to being the one who must remain unshakable. But today, I admitted it. I said, "Let’s go together."

And then, I took her hand.

Thinking back on that gesture now, it still feels strange. My hand holding hers; her hand was very warm, probably because she was nervous too. We just sat there for a while, neither of us speaking. I could feel her looking at me, but I didn't look up.

The doctor’s terminology is still echoing in my mind—metabolic syndrome, atherosclerosis, long-term medication. I always thought that through discipline and control, I could manage my body like a precision machine. But now I realize I have no control at all. The body has its own logic; it doesn't run according to my schedule.

The moment I held her hand, I wasn't thinking about the diagnosis itself, but rather—if I really collapse, what happens to her? All those tools, forty years of experience, the things I haven't had time to organize yet...

I don't know if this counts as weakness. I’ve always hated weakness. But today, it seems I allowed myself a little bit of it.

My hands are still shaking. I went to the workshop and wiped down my tools, from the first to the last, every single one of them with great care. Only while doing that did my hands slowly steady themselves.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue as planned. But something has changed, and I haven't figured out how to face it yet.

ranked_items.content:
March 20, 2022, 11:47 PM

Sitting in the workshop. My wife is already asleep. The noises downstairs have all quieted down.

I finished page thirty today. Starting from the first page, my handwriting was neat; the purpose, specifications, and purchase date of every tool were recorded clearly. But toward the end, the pen started to shake. I’m not sure when it began—maybe around page twenty-five. The strokes became irregular, and some words looked rushed.

When I got to the entry for the wrench, I stopped for a long time. I bought this wrench in 1998. It’s a Japanese import with a precision of 0.02 mm. I’ve used it for over twenty years, and it shows very little wear. I wanted to record its use, but the pen just hovered there. Then I wrote—"Who will ever use this?"

After writing that sentence, I didn't cross it out. It’s strange. I’m not usually like this. The notebooks on my desk have always been spotless—no scribbles, no traces of hesitation. But this time, looking at that sentence, I just couldn't bring myself to erase it.

Hao is in Shenzhen; Yu is in Guangzhou. Neither of them will ever come to this workshop. Hao is in real estate sales, and Yu teaches. They have their own lives. I never expected them to inherit anything, but sitting here now, looking at these tools, I suddenly realized—these things are about to become useless. Not because they’re broken or lost, but because no one will know how to use them.

This morning, Meifang asked me what I was busy with. I only replied with a grunt. She didn't ask again. She’s used to my silence. After thirty-five years, she’s long known that I’m a man of few words. But when that "mm-hmm" came out today, even I could hear it—it wasn't silence; it was avoidance. I don’t know how to explain to her what I’m doing. I can’t even explain it to myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. My blood sugar is up a bit again. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m in my fifties now; my body is starting to disobey me. I used to think that as long as I kept things under control and followed the plan, nothing would go wrong. But the body doesn't follow a plan. Neither does time.

I’m a little over a year away from retirement. I’ve been waiting for this day, but now that it’s almost here, I’m starting to feel afraid. Afraid of what I’ll do after I retire. Afraid of being idle. Afraid that these tools will just keep sitting here, unused and misunderstood.

The notebook is still open. "Who will ever use this" is still there on page thirty. Tomorrow, I have to keep writing. There are still many tools left to record. I have to get them all down while I can still move.

But even if I record them, what then?

ranked_items.content:
# Studio Notes

Sunday, August 28, 2022, Morning

I didn't sleep well last night. I couldn't stop thinking about that phone call.

The school director said they are very interested. Said the project is feasible. Said their students need something like this. I had to replay it twice in my head to make sure I hadn't misheard.

I spent the whole night scribbling nonsense in my notebook—all about how tools get lost and knowledge dissipates. The handwriting was terrible, like the scrawlings of a madman. Looking over it this morning, it felt foreign even to me. That didn't look like my work.

But what I came up with yesterday is different.

I thought: what if I organized these tools and this experience into something systematic? Not for Chen Hao or Chen Yu—they each have their own paths—but for those who truly need it. For young people who are interested in precision engineering, just as my father influenced me back then.

the moment that thought surfaced, my hands stopped shaking.

I spent an hour reorganizing yesterday's chaotic notes. Titles, modules, schedules. My handwriting became much clearer. My fingers remembered what to do. It felt just like drafting a new process improvement plan in the studio.

"Precision Engineering Heritage Project"—that’s the name I gave it. It consists of three parts: tool donations, craft lectures, and apprenticeship training. The costs are controllable, and the outputs are clear. The school said they would cooperate.

My wife asked me this morning why I hadn't slept. I didn't explain much. I just said I was thinking about something new. She nodded and went back to her breakfast. She’s used to me being like this.

The medical report is still in the drawer. The cardiologist's words are still ringing in my ears. But this morning, I didn't keep obsessing over those numbers. I was doing something else.

Perhaps that’s the difference. I cannot change the fact that my body is aging. But I can decide to leave something behind before—or even while—it happens. Not to prove that I lived, but because... some things are worth passing on.

I look at the tools in the studio. Dozens of them, some bought over twenty years ago. They won't lose their value just because I die. They will keep working in someone else's hands.

This thought brings me a feeling I haven't felt in a long time.

It isn't despair.

I need to send the detailed proposal to the school next Monday. I also need to organize the first donation list.

The pen is still in my hand.

# Office Memo

**April 5, 2022, 2:20 PM**

---

My fingers are tapping the desk again. I can’t stop.

I was too harsh with Wang Gang just now. His suggestion regarding the inspection process was actually fine; why did I have to insist on pointing out his lack of precision? His expression changed—I saw it—but I didn't apologize. I just kept talking about my standards.

Why is it always like this?

During the afternoon meeting, I lowered the third-quarter defect rate target again. From 0.8 to 0.6. No one dared to object, but I saw Li Qiang’s pen tear through the paper while he was taking notes. He’s afraid. Someone else in my department will quit. I know it.

But I can’t control myself.

The physical exam report is still in the drawer. Blood pressure 139/92. The doctor said I need to be careful. I’m monitoring Li Meifang’s salt intake, yet I—never mind.

I wiped down the tools in the workshop again today. Screwdrivers, calipers, micrometers. Every single one is recorded in the logbook. Use, specifications, year of purchase. Chen Hao called to say his mortgage went up again; I listened to him finish and then just said, "Cut back on your spending." He didn't say anything else. It occurred to me that he might have wanted my help, but I didn't ask.

What use are these tools, anyway?

Two years until retirement. What exactly am I planning for?

Li Meifang said she wants braised pork for dinner. I’m already thinking about how to convince her to eat something lighter. She’ll listen to me, as she always does. But the way she looks at me—I’ve noticed it—she’s avoiding my eyes.

When did I become this kind of person?

Fingers still tapping. Tap. Tap. Tap.

I need to write this down. While I still can.

---

*Check tomorrow’s quality inspection standards before leaving. Might need to adjust them one more time.*

ranked_items.content:
# Memo

**Date**: Wednesday, August 24, 2022, 14:00

**Subject**: Reflections following a game of chess

---

## Record

During today’s game with Player A, he said something that stuck with me: "Instead of spending time fearing the future, spend it cherishing the present."

I didn't respond at the time, but I haven't stopped thinking about it since.

## Issues to Sort Out

1. **What am I doing?**
   - My recent anxiety cycle: Prediction → Fear → Control → More Fear.
   - Since the physical exam results came back, I’ve been using data, plans, and logs to fight uncertainty.
   - But is this actually helpful? Or am I just wearing myself out?

2. **On Control**
   - What I can control: Workflows, financial accounts, daily schedules.
   - What I cannot control: Aging, fluctuating health metrics, my children’s choices, time itself.
   - I have been trying to fill the void of uncontrollable fears with things I can control.
   - What is the cost of doing this?

3. **On the Present**
   - For the past few months, I’ve been planning for a retirement that is two years away.
   - At the same time, I’m worrying about potential health issues.
   - The "present" I hold in my hands has been squeezed into something tiny.
   - My wife, the chess games, the tools in my workshop—they are all still here, but mentally, I’ve already let go of them.

4. **Defining Value**
   - I’ve always believed: Completion → Results → Proof of self-worth.
   - But what if things can’t be completed? What if the results are forgotten?
   - Perhaps value is more than just these things?

## For Further Reflection

- Why do I keep losing what I have now by worrying about what I might lose in the future?
- Is accepting the unchangeable an act of giving up, or an act of maturity?
- How much more time can I afford to waste on fear?

---

**Note**: When I play Player A again next week, I want to hear his take on "cherishing the present." Not as small talk, but because I truly want to understand.

ranked_items.content:
# Monday, September 12, 2022 – Sunny

After the morning meeting, I sat in my office, my hands still trembling slightly.

It wasn’t nerves. It was because I had just done something that didn't feel like me.

Li Ming, my subordinate, messed up his data sheet again—the third time this month. By my standards, a low-level error like that should have been called out directly; I should have made him understand exactly what "unacceptable" means. I had even rehearsed the blunt words in my head. But in the conference room, when I saw his tense expression, I paused.

I paused.

What I actually said was: "Let’s look at how we can improve this together." Then I spent ten minutes explaining it to him, instead of my usual three-sentence reprimand.

It’s so strange.

Sitting here now in the corner of my office, I’m trying to understand why I did that. Is it because I’ve been dwelling on the doctor’s words all week? Or is it because I’m starting to realize that I might actually be changing?

The doctor said my body is failing. Pre-metabolic syndrome. Early signs of carotid atherosclerosis. A blood sugar level of 110 mg/dL. These numbers are like nails driven into my brain. I always thought that through precise management—routine, control, supervision—I could maintain everything. But the doctor used data to tell me that I’ve failed.

Maybe that’s why I softened my stance with Li Ming. Maybe it’s because I suddenly realized I can’t control everything. I can’t even control my own body.

So, what right do I have to treat my subordinates that way?

The thought feels foreign to me. It also feels frightening.

Because if I let go of my obsession with detail, if I abandon that relentless management style, then who am I? What was the point of everything I’ve stood for over the last forty years?

On the other hand, I saw a spark in Li Ming’s eyes after I changed my tone. He started listening intently, started asking questions, instead of just hanging his head and taking the criticism. Maybe this change... maybe it isn't entirely a bad thing?

I don’t know.

I’m exhausted right now. These thoughts have drained my energy. The pressure is still there, but it feels different. It’s no longer anger at imperfection; it’s anxiety about time. How much longer? How much time do I have left to change? How much time is left to pass things on?

My wife said yesterday, "I knew you’d have this eventually." She was right. She’s always right. But I didn’t want to admit it.

Now, I have to.

The medicine the doctor prescribed is still in the drawer. I haven't taken it yet.

Tomorrow. I’ll deal with it tomorrow.

**[Sunday, May 8, 3:00 PM – Chess Game]**

Location: Recreation Room
Participants: Player A, Player B, Player C

---

**Log:**
Today’s afternoon game. During the second round, my reactions were noticeably slower, and my strategy felt muddled. Player A asked if I’d been overworked lately; I told him no, I was just having an off day. But it’s not about being tired.

I stared at the board for nearly two minutes before making a move—something that never used to happen. My fingers were resting on the piece, but my mind was elsewhere—thinking about those figures in last week’s physical exam report, thinking about the dizzy spells I’ve been having some mornings.

I left early during the third round, claiming I had something to take care of at home. I didn’t. I just didn't want to keep going.

**Flag/Reminder: Contact the doctor next Monday. Ask if this recent sluggishness requires a follow-up. Might need a neurocognitive assessment.**

---

*Note: Review chess performance once a month. If things continue to deteriorate, must take the initiative to go to the hospital.*

ranked_items.content:
October 30, 2022, 8:00 PM

Li Meifang is already asleep. I’m sitting in the study; on the desk lies today’s medical report and a cup of tea that went cold long ago.

I said something to her today that left even me a bit stunned. I said, "Perhaps the meaning of life isn't in perfect control, but in genuine connection." Hearing those words come out of my mouth felt like someone was impersonating me. And yet, it really is what I was thinking.

Since I started the medication these past few days, the physical side effects haven't been as bad as I imagined. The doctor says it’s a good sign. But what’s stranger is that I’ve actually started to relax. I used to check my medical reports three times a day, recording every single number in a notebook, trying to force them back to "normal" through sheer willpower. Now... I still look at the reports, but I no longer feel the need to "subdue" them.

Last night, Meifang took my hand. She does this often, and I used to pull away after a moment to get back to my accounts or the news. But this time, I didn't. I let her hold it—maybe for five or six seconds. She didn’t say anything, and neither did I. We just held hands. Eventually, she let go gently and went to the kitchen to pour me some water.

I realize now that I owe her a great deal. For thirty-five years, I’ve used excuses like "monitoring her blood pressure" or "managing the family finances" to package my need for control as "responsibility." In truth, it was just my way of proving I was useful, that I deserved to be needed—by controlling everything. She saw through it long ago, but she never said a word. She just stayed by my side, waiting.

Chen Hao called recently saying his mortgage is a heavy burden. In the past, I would have immediately started analyzing his finances, giving him advice, even feeling a bit dissatisfied with his "poor planning." This time, I just listened, then told him, "It’s okay, take your time." There was a pause on the other end of the line; he didn’t seem used to me responding like that. I’m not used to it either. But it felt... more real.

Then there’s that idea about donating my tools to the technical school. Initially, I thought of it as "legacy"—wanting young people to learn what I’ve accumulated over forty years. But today I realized that was just another form of control; I wanted their learning to prove that my value wouldn't vanish with retirement. I still want to donate them, but the reason has changed. I just want to put those tools where they can continue to be used and cherished. As for who uses them or what they learn, that’s not for me to decide, nor does it need to be.

It’s strange. I always thought letting go of control would make me panic. But actually, it makes me feel... light?

My subordinates at the office are still a bit afraid of me. I know that. But lately, I’ve been thinking that maybe I could try a different way. Not by lowering standards, but... by changing how I communicate. Letting them know my expectations are high because I believe they can do well, not because I’m disappointed in them. This will require me to say more, and I’ve never been good at that. But perhaps it’s worth learning.

Tomorrow is Monday. Another week begins. I’ll keep taking the medicine, keep working, and keep being there for Meifang. And maybe, at the right moment, I’ll sit with her—saying nothing, just holding her hand.

That is enough.

ranked_items.content:
# Memo - Reflection and Legacy

**Date: October 18, 2022, Afternoon**
**Location: Home Study**

---

## Core Points Summary

### I. Reflections on the Essence of 40 Years of Work
- ~~Perfect quality control~~ → Obsession with detail is a value in itself
- ~~Sense of achievement from process optimization~~ → The patience and persistence learned through the process
- A 25% drop in defect rates is not the finish line, but a reminder: Why do I demand so much?
  - Answer: Because I believe in standards.
  - The real answer: Because I am afraid of losing control.

### II. Beyond the Technical (Important)
- The meaning of tools lies not in collecting them, but in how they taught me to think.
- What precision work taught me:
  - A single millimeter determines success or failure.
  - However, the distance between people cannot be measured in millimeters.
  - (Thinking back on my harshness toward subordinates and my "supervision" of my wife over the years.)
- I must admit: I cannot control everything.
  - The doctor's words are still ringing in my ears.
  - The body ages; plans change.
  - This is not failure.

### III. If I Could Start Over
- Explain to the team *why* we do things earlier, rather than just telling them *what* to do.
- Give young engineers more room to make mistakes (instead of blunt dismissals).
- ~~Keeping knowledge in my head~~ → Actively record, share, and discuss.
- Listen more to my wife, rather than "supervising" from the sidelines.
- Communicate with the children about more than just their mortgages and jobs.

### IV. Words for Young Engineers
- Standards are important, but don't let them become a weapon.
- Details determine quality, but details can also consume your life—learn to prioritize.
- 40 years is a long time, but it is also very short.
- The things you take for granted now will become mere memories by the time you reach my age.
- Don't wait for your heart to remind you—some things are more important than perfection.

### V. Immediate Action Plan
- [ ] Compile a "Core Experiences in Quality Management" document (for technical school students).
- [ ] Sit down with my subordinates for a talk—explain rather than accuse.
- [ ] Have a real conversation with my wife this weekend; listen to her talk about home.
- [ ] Call Chen Hao and Chen Yu respectively; do not mention work.
- [ ] Continue medication and scheduled check-ups—accept this reality.

---

## Notes
- The precision tool inventory can be set aside for now.
- The 40-year timeline is drawn; now it needs to be turned into a story.
- Perhaps retirement isn't the end, but a different kind of beginning?
- (I wouldn't have believed that sentence before.)

---

**One final question: What is my greatest regret in life?**
It isn't that I didn't reach a higher position.
It’s that I never truly looked at the people beside me.

# Work Log

**Date:** April 5, 2022  
**Recorded by:** Chen Guilin  
**Department:** Production Quality Management

---

## Morning Summary

At 9:30 AM, a departmental meeting was held to review quality inspection standards. The primary focus was checking the formatting compliance of recently submitted quality inspection reports.

**Issues Identified:** The third batch product inspection report submitted by inspector Wang Gang showed significant formatting non-compliance. Specific issues included:
- Missing mandatory timestamps in the data record columns.
- Vague descriptions in the "Abnormal Items" section, failing to meet the requirements of Article 4.2 of the *Quality Inspection Report Standardization Template v2.1*.
- Incomplete information in the signature fields.

**Actions Taken:**  
I pointed out these issues publicly and emphasized the necessity of standardized formatting. The core of quality management lies in an obsession with detail—this is not a trivial matter; it is a manifestation of professionalism. If we cannot even control basic report formatting, how can we ensure the credibility and traceability of our data?

Wang Gang attempted to explain the reasons, but that is beside the point. Standards are standards; there is no room for negotiation in execution. I have ordered him to resubmit a compliant version by 3:00 PM tomorrow.

---

## Afternoon Schedule

- 14:00-14:30: Coordinate this week's production capacity plan with the Production Department.
- 14:30-15:30: Audit report samples from other inspectors (spot check of 10 reports).
- 16:00: Compile the first draft of this month's process improvement plan, expected to be submitted to superiors next week.

---

## Remarks

The department's defect rate remains at 0.8% this month, continuing to stay below the industry average. However, personnel execution needs further strengthening. Professionalism is non-negotiable.

I told Meifang about it over dinner tonight.

Another formatting error cropped up in QA this morning. It wasn't a major issue, but I can't stand that kind of slackness when it comes to details. I was right there on the floor and called it out immediately; my voice might have been a bit loud. The subordinate looked pretty shaken, but I had to make it clear—details determine product quality, and product quality determines whether this company survives. I’m not exaggerating.

I told Meifang that I need to overhaul the department’s standard operating procedures. Just emphasizing it verbally isn't enough; it has to be documented with every step clearly defined. As she listened, she just looked at me that way, and I knew what she was thinking: *Here we go again.* But this time is different. I can feel that if things continue like this, the problems are only going to multiply.

She said, "Don't push yourself too hard." I didn't respond to that. After a few bites of food, I explained once more why these standards must be strictly enforced. She just nodded and kept eating.

The truth is, I know my subordinates are afraid of me. But what’s wrong with a little fear? Fear makes people diligent. I’ve been in this position for 12 years, and our defect rate is 25% lower than the industry average—that’s not because I have a pleasant temperament.

Lately, I just feel this constant urge to hurry. Whether it’s work or anything else. While I’m still able to move, I need to get everything done right.

**July 10, 2022 Work Log**

**Weather: Sunny  Temperature: 32°C**

**Morning Work Record**

09:15 Quality Control Department weekly meeting. Discovered issues while reviewing yesterday’s production reports.

The periodic inspection sheet submitted by my subordinate, Li, was a mess—he used Chinese capitalized characters for the date column, which violates the company’s unified standards. This is no small matter. I pointed out the error in front of everyone. My tone might have been harsh, but it had to be. Details determine product quality, and product quality determines the company’s survival. This logical chain cannot be allowed to slacken. A formatting error can lead to data misinterpretation; misinterpretation can lead to missed inspections; and a missed inspection means defective products reaching the market. I cannot tolerate a failure in any link of this chain.

Li looked visibly upset at the time. I know he probably thinks I went overboard. But I don’t need him to like me; I need him to understand the importance of standardization.

**Developing New QC Standards**

09:45-11:20 Drafted the new "Detailed Rules for Quality Inspection of Precision Components (Revised Edition)" in my office.

Key adjustments:
- Table formats unified to Arabic numerals and 24-hour clock.
- Added a "Basis for Judgment" column for every inspection item; reference standard numbers must be cited.
- Added an "Auditor Review" step at the inspector’s signature line—a double-confirmation system.
- Data entry deadline moved up to 17:00 daily, leaving 2 hours for data auditing.
- Added a "Deviation Analysis" section to the monthly summary report; reasons and corrective measures for every out-of-spec item must be explained.

None of this is needless bureaucracy. Every single rule points toward the same goal: turning invisible risks into visible data, and turning potential errors into inevitable discoveries.

I plan to hold a departmental meeting next Monday to announce the new standards. Everyone will be given a 3-day transition period, with formal implementation starting July 15.

**Other**

Did not go out for lunch. Stayed in the office to review the draft of the new standards again and refined the wording in two places.

I am meeting with my superior this afternoon to report on this month’s improvement plan. This new set of standards should be adopted.

**Physical Status**

Sleep has been okay lately. No particular discomfort.

My wife mentioned yesterday that her blood pressure has risen again. Reminded her to take her medication on time and sent her a text at noon telling her to use less salt in dinner.

**Remarks**

I feel my demands for work have been increasing lately. It’s not because of new pressure from the company, but rather a feeling that I cannot allow for any slack. I have less than two years until retirement; I want to optimize all processes and set all standards in stone during this time. That way, even after I leave, the system will continue to function properly. This is important.

---

*Recorded by: Chen Guilin*
*Date: 2022-07-10*

ranked_items.content:
Tuesday afternoon, October 18, 2022

I spent the entire afternoon sitting in my small workshop. I didn’t clean any tools or organize my inventory. I just flipped through my notebooks, page by page.

What was I recording back then? Screwdriver models, wrench specifications, the purchase year and maintenance cycle for every single tool. Everything was neat and tidy, precise to a fault. Looking at these records used to give me a sense of peace—as if I had mastered something.

But today, I’m wondering: what did these tools actually teach me?

It wasn't how to use them. That’s too simple. It was... how to approach a task. With seriousness. Without compromise. Knowing that every detail matters. For forty years, the products I made with these tools had a defect rate 25% lower than the industry average. That wasn't a coincidence. It was because I never allowed myself to slack off.

But—

I wrote that word on a new page, then crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again.

But I treated people the same way. My subordinates, my family. I demanded perfection and left no room for error. I thought that was called being responsible. Now, I’m not so sure.

Li Meifang has been humming in the kitchen lately. I haven't heard her hum like that in a long time. She says it’s because I’ve finally "loosened up a bit." I can hear it in her voice—it wasn't a compliment. It was... relief? She feels relieved.

That hurts a little.

I turned to a blank page and drew a timeline with a pencil. From 1982 to now. Forty years. I marked the key years: technician, team leader, department manager. I did well at every stage. Defect rates, process optimizations, the proposals that were adopted. It’s a beautiful report card.

But so what?

Chen Hao is in Shenzhen, under a lot of pressure. I keep track of his mortgage and his performance, but we barely have anything to say to each other. When Chen Yu comes home occasionally, our conversations are like status reports—how’s work, is your marriage stable. I thought I was teaching them about perseverance and quality awareness, but I was never truly there for them.

This is my regret. Perhaps my greatest regret.

Next to the timeline, I wrote: "If I could do it over, what would I do?"

But I know there are no "ifs." I only have the now. And about two years left of work. Maybe in these two years, I should record some things. Not tool lists, not process documents. But... what I was thinking during these forty years. Why I did things the way I did. Young people might need that.

Or rather, I need to do this. To forgive myself.

Meifang is calling me for dinner. I hear her voice drifting up from downstairs—gentle, not rushing me. I close the notebook and stand up.

Maybe I should listen to her more. Really listen, instead of mentally calculating her blood pressure and diet.

Maybe that is what I really need to learn.
